subsections.title: Actuaries and Health & Care
bullets: Actuaries work with health professionals in the private sector to price and value health and care insurance products.
In the UK, actuaries have been involved in financial planning for the NHS for many years; this work is likely to expand as government copes with a changing population.
The SP1 Core Reading is primarily based on the UK model, but since SP subjects are not country-specific, other countries' approaches must be kept in mind.

subsections.title: Structure of Subject SP1
bullets: Chapters 1–5: basic contract types — IP, CI, LTCI, PMI, major medical expenses and health cash plans; Chapter 5 focuses on group business.
Chapters 6–8: main stakeholders — customer, insurer, regulator, and sellers of health and care products.
Chapters 9–10: the environment in which health and care insurers operate, with particular focus on the role of the State.
Chapters 11–16: modelling, data and assumptions — both in general terms and applied specifically to health and care products.
Chapters 17–20: pricing methods for different health and care products, including group contracts and options/guarantees.
Chapters 21–22: reserves, solvency capital requirements, and embedded value.
Chapters 23–25: many different areas of risk facing a health and care insurer.
Chapters 26–27: risk management — in particular reinsurance and underwriting.
Chapters 28–29: investment and monitoring respectively.
Chapter 30: problem solving.
Chapter 31: Glossary — questions on glossary terms were often asked in ST1 exams; advisable to familiarise yourself with the terms as you work through the course.

subsections.title: The Actuarial Control Cycle (ACC)
bullets: The ACC (from Subject CP1) gives a framework for solving actuarial problems and can identify big-picture topics in SP1.

The ACC has four main stages: (1) the general commercial and economic environment, (2) specifying the problem, (3) developing the solution, (4) monitoring the experience — with professionalism underpinning all stages.
The models used in risk management are dynamic: they change in light of experience or following changes in the environment.
General commercial and economic environment: sets the overall context; a sound understanding of the environment in which health and care insurers operate is crucial.
Specifying the problem: involves specification of the targeted outcome, assessment of risks, product design options and prices, and strategic courses of action to handle risks.
Developing the solution: involves selecting/constructing the model, selecting assumptions, interpreting model results, considering stakeholder implications, and formalising a proposal.

Monitoring the experience: comparing actual vs expected experience, identifying causes of departures, feeding back into other stages (e.g. identifying sources of surplus/deficit and modifying future assumptions).

Professionalism: determines the suitability of solutions; actuaries must take personal responsibility, act with integrity, maintain a trusting client relationship, respect others' views, and demonstrate and maintain competence.

SP1 mapped to ACC: products and stakeholders (Chs 1–10) sit in the environment; risk management (Chs 23–27) and contract design (Ch 6) in specifying the problem; modelling, data, assumptions, pricing, reserving and investment (Chs 11–22, 28) in developing the solution; monitoring (Ch 29) in monitoring the experience.

key_terms.definition: A framework for solving actuarial problems comprising four stages: the general commercial and economic environment, specifying the problem, developing the solution, and monitoring the experience — with professionalism underpinning all stages.

subsections.title: The Product Cycle
bullets: The product cycle runs: product design → pricing → marketing/sales → underwriting → claims management → experience monitoring → valuation → back to product design.
All areas within an organisation (pricing, underwriting, claims management, marketing and sales, experience monitoring, valuation) make an important contribution to the experience that emerges.
Many of these areas are more complex for health and care products than for life insurance products.
The stages of the product cycle are discussed in more detail in Chapter 1.

key_terms.definition: A cyclical framework describing the stages of an insurance product's life: product design, pricing, marketing/sales, underwriting, claims management, experience monitoring, and valuation — each feeding into the next.

subsections.title: Product Overview
bullets: IP insurance (Ch 1): provides regular short- or long-term payments during periods of incapacity in return for a regular premium.
CI insurance (Ch 2): pure protection — sum insured payable if the policyholder suffers one of the insured conditions during the term; regular premiums paid until claim, death, or term end.

LTCI (Ch 3): covers all forms of continuing personal/nursing care and domestic services for elderly people unable to look after themselves; covers care in own home, day centre, or care home; for those whose health is not expected to improve.

PMI and related products (Ch 4): usually indemnity-based, compensating for cost of private medical treatment; related products include health cash plans, dental/optical plans and major medical expenses.
IP, CI, and LTCI are usually sold as long-term policies (initial terms >5 years); PMI and related products are sold as one-year renewable policies.

Long-term policies may be sold by life insurance companies (sometimes classified as life insurance); short-term policies may be sold by general insurance companies; or products may be sold by specialist health and care insurers.

Questions may combine two or more basic contract types as a package — think about the separate underlying components but also consider synergies (e.g. increased claims on one component may be associated with reduced claims on another).
Group versions of IP, CI, and PMI may exist (e.g. for an employer to cover several employees) — covered in Chapter 5.

key_terms.definition: Provides regular short- or long-term income payments to the insured during periods of incapacity due to illness or injury. Also known as Permanent Health Insurance (PHI) or disability income insurance.
Pure protection product paying a lump-sum benefit if the policyholder suffers one of a specified list of insured conditions during the policy term.

Covers continuing personal/nursing care and associated domestic services for those (typically elderly) who are unable to look after themselves; care may be provided at home, in a day centre, or in a care home setting.
Usually an indemnity-based annual policy that compensates for the cost of private medical treatment. Related products include health cash plans, dental/optical plans, and major medical expenses.

subsections.title: Long-term Insurance Contracts
bullets: IP, CI, and LTCI are generally classed as long-term insurance; short-term versions of IP and CI do exist.

Key features of long-term insurance: cover provided for many years (e.g. 20 years); cover usually ceases on claim; claim amount may be known with certainty (fixed sum assured or fixed annual benefit); used for protection against ill-health or death and (sometimes) savings; may be sold individually or on a group basis (group cover typically for one or two years, renewable).
Most long-term health and care products are used for protection and do not offer a surrender value on lapse; LTCI products with a savings element may offer a surrender benefit.

subsections.title: Short-term Insurance Contracts
bullets: Short-term SP1 products include PMI, major medical expenses, and health cash plans.
Most short-term policies provide indemnity — reimbursing losses; others pay pre-specified amounts on particular contingencies (e.g. a fixed amount for a specified surgical procedure).

Key features of short-term insurance: cover typically for one year (reviewable); multiple claims possible; claim amounts generally unknown and volatile; delays in reporting/settling claims possible (though most PMI claims have relatively short delays); may be sold individually or on a group basis.

sections.title: Consumer Needs and the Personal Financial Life Cycle

subsections.title: How Consumer Needs Change
bullets: Products exist because they meet a consumer need; companies redesign products over time to meet changing needs.

Changes over calendar time: consumer needs change as lifestyles, standards of living, education, and technology evolve. Rising affluence increases demand for comprehensive products (e.g. PMI); better education enables more complex products to be sold.
Changes over personal lifetimes: personal financial needs change across an individual's life cycle, explaining the variety of products sold simultaneously in any marketplace.

subsections.title: The Personal Financial Life Cycle
bullets: Ages 16–25: likely in higher education or first job; probably no home or dependants; in good health; little need for individual health insurance, although employer benefits (IP, PMI) may apply; minor sporting injuries may create some demand.

Ages 25–35: may have partner, children, large mortgage, moderate income, high expenditure; main needs are IP and CI insurance to ensure liabilities (mortgage, dependants) can be met in case of ill-health.

Ages 35–60: children become independent; debts reduce; income may outstrip expenditure; biggest financial need is to save for retirement; health needs include IP (if still employed), CI, and LTCI; growing demand for PMI as disposable income rises — possible needs for all health and care products.

Ages over 60: retired; few debts but lower income; main risk is running out of money if very old or needing care; main health need is LTCI; PMI may still be attractive; CI insurance may be appealing if on acceptable terms.

sections.title: Investment Types for Long-term Insurance Contracts

subsections.title: Without-profits Contracts
bullets: Benefits (and often premiums) are fixed at outset — either in monetary terms or linked to an index (e.g. price or wage inflation index).
If experience is better than expected (e.g. higher investment returns), profits accrue to the insurer, not the policyholder.
Index-linked contracts: benefit moves in line with a specified economic index; premiums may also be index-linked or fixed.
Reviewable products: premiums and/or benefits can be reviewed at pre-determined points; policyholder may accept or decline the proposed terms at each review.

key_terms.definition: A contract where benefits (and often premiums) are fixed at outset in monetary terms or linked to a specified index. Any excess investment return accrues to the insurer.
A contract where premiums and/or benefits can be revised at pre-determined review points; the policyholder can accept or decline the proposed revised terms.

subsections.title: With-profits Contracts
bullets: Under a with-profits contract, the insurer and the policyholder share the profits that arise (and hence the risks).
Typically involves guaranteed benefits plus discretionary (bonus) benefits.
Commonly used for savings contracts — not covered in detail in SP1.

key_terms.definition: A contract where profits (and risks) are shared between insurer and policyholder, typically via guaranteed benefits plus discretionary bonuses.

subsections.title: Unit-linked Contracts
bullets: Premiums are invested in pooled investment funds (e.g. equity, fixed-interest, balanced); the policyholder's share is represented by units.

Expense charges include: allocation rate/bid-offer spread (deducted before allocation to units); fund management charge (taken by cancelling units or reducing unit price); policy administration charges (fixed monthly deduction from fund).

A protection element — typically a minimum guaranteed claim benefit — is usually present in health and care unit-linked contracts to ensure a reasonable benefit when the claim occurs early (when the fund value is low).
Where there is a minimum guaranteed claim benefit, the insurer makes up any shortfall between unit value and the guarantee; a monthly risk charge is deducted from units to cover this cost.

Insurer's profit comes from: expense charges minus actual expenses, and risk charge minus actual cost of providing the guaranteed benefit. Key risk: charges do not match expenses/guarantee cost in nature, timing and amount.

Unit-linked lump sum benefits (e.g. CI): policyholder receives the maximum of (unit fund value, guaranteed minimum benefit); if fund > guarantee, no top-up needed; if fund < guarantee, insurer pays the difference.

Unit-linked ongoing benefits (e.g. LTCI): situation more complex as total benefit is unknown at claim inception. If fund is protected, all benefits paid from non-unit fund (unit fund returned as lump sum — primarily investment vehicle). If fund is not protected, unit fund used first; once exhausted, non-unit fund covers remainder (primarily protection vehicle).
In SP1, unit-linked contracts are most likely to appear in the context of CI and LTCI, although unit-linked IP contracts can also exist.

key_terms.definition: A contract where policyholder premiums are invested in pooled investment funds; the policyholder's share is represented by units. Benefits depend on fund performance and charges. A protection element (minimum guaranteed benefit) is typically present in health and care unit-linked contracts.
A monthly deduction from the unit fund of a unit-linked contract, used to fund the cost of meeting any shortfall between the unit fund value and a guaranteed minimum claim benefit.

In a unit-linked LTCI contract, a protected fund means all care benefits are paid from the non-unit (insurer's) fund, with the unit fund returned to the policyholder as a lump sum — the contract is primarily used as an investment vehicle.

exam_qa.q: What is likely to be the main difference between the type of benefit paid on a critical illness claim and that paid on a long-term care claim under a unit-linked contract?
exam_qa.a: CI benefits are paid as a lump sum. LTCI benefits are usually paid as ongoing regular payments or contributions towards the cost of care (though lump sum benefits are also possible). This makes LTCI more complex on claim inception since the total benefit is unknown.

subsections.title: Claim Incidence vs Claim Inception
bullets: Claim incidence: used for one-off benefits (e.g. CI insurance, PMI) — refers to the occurrence of the insured event.
Claim inception: used to indicate the start of regular benefit payments (e.g. IP insurance, LTCI).
The two terms are often used interchangeably in practice; not something to worry about in the SP1 exam.

key_terms.definition: The occurrence of the insured event; typically used for one-off (lump sum) benefit products such as CI or PMI.
The start of regular benefit payments; typically used for income-stream products such as IP insurance or LTCI.

subsections.title: New Business Strain
bullets: New business strain arises when the initial asset share (premiums received less initial expenses) is less than the sum of the supervisory reserves plus the solvency capital requirement: C₀ = V₀ − A₀, where C₀ = capital strain, V₀ = supervisory reserves + required solvency capital, A₀ = asset share — all at time 0+ (immediately after policy issue, first premium paid, initial expenses incurred).
Any strain must be met from the company's free assets (those not earmarked for any particular purpose).

Long-term policies (regular premiums): asset share A₀ is typically negative in the early years because initial expenses (commission, sales and marketing, underwriting, policy documentation) exceed the first premium received; capital strain C₀ is therefore substantial.
Short-term policies (single or monthly premiums): C₀ is much smaller than for a long-term policy with a similar sized initial premium.
A company prefers contract types and designs that recoup the invested capital quickly so it can be recycled to fund further profitable business.

Consumers prefer regular level premiums for long-term contracts because they transfer the long-term health risk (which increases with age) to the insurer; level premiums allow better financial planning and avoid the risk of premiums becoming unaffordable.

key_terms.definition: Arises when the initial asset share is less than the supervisory reserves plus required solvency capital at policy inception (C₀ = V₀ − A₀). The shortfall must be funded from the company's free assets.
Essentially the accumulated cashflows (premiums less expenses and claims) to any particular date, accumulated at the actual rates of return earned by the company.
Reserves required by insurance supervisory authorities to ensure the company can meet its future obligations to policyholders.
A minimum amount of assets the company must hold in addition to those backing its assessment of liabilities — further capital tied up beyond supervisory reserves.

exam_qa.q: Why might consumers who purchase a long-term policy prefer regular level premiums rather than premiums that may change from year to year?
exam_qa.a: Consumers prefer to transfer the long-term health risk (which increases with age) to the insurer in return for level premiums. Level premiums enable better financial planning. Premiums that change year to year may become unaffordable at some future point, leaving the policyholder unable to maintain cover.

exam_qa.q: Describe how new business strain is likely to arise for: (a) CI policies with level regular premiums, (b) LTCI policies secured by a single premium.
exam_qa.a: (a) CI (regular premium): The asset share is normally negative in the early years because high initial expenses — commission, sales and marketing, underwriting, and policy documentation — typically exceed the first premium received. Supervisory restrictions may require reserves to be at least zero, and solvency capital is also needed. Therefore liabilities exceed assets and new business strain arises. (b) LTCI (single premium): Despite the large positive net cash inflow (the single premium) at time zero, new business strain commonly arises because the valuation basis is stronger (more prudent) than the premium basis, and/or because the company must hold required solvency capital. The reserves established on the valuation basis therefore exceed the premium received net of initial expenses.

subsections.title: Underwriting
bullets: Underwriting: the process used by insurers to assess how risky an applicant is and what premium should be charged; quite different for long-term and short-term insurance.

Long-term insurance underwriting: most commonly medical underwriting — questions on the application form cover gender, age, smoker status, weight, height, existing health conditions and family history. Further evidence (medical records, routine medical exam, specialist tests) may be sought for riskier or larger cases.
Outcomes of long-term underwriting: higher-than-standard premium, lower-than-standard benefit, or decline.

Short-term (PMI) underwriting approaches: (1) Full medical underwriting — full medical history gathered at application; applicant usually accepted at standard terms but pre-existing conditions specifically excluded. (2) Moratorium underwriting — no medical underwriting at application; conditions existing in a pre-specified period (usually 2–5 years) prior to inception are excluded; covered after typically 2 consecutive years with no treatment/symptoms/advice on those conditions; insurer on immediate risk for all other conditions. (3) Medical history disregarded (MHD) — no exclusions for pre-existing conditions; more likely on group PMI schemes.

PMI renewal underwriting exceptions: No Worse Terms (NWT) — new insurer agrees to offer cover at least as comprehensive as the current policy with no additional underwriting conditions. Continued personal medical exclusions (CPME) — NWT acceptance where the new insurer carries forward only such cover for medical conditions as existed under the previous policy.

key_terms.definition: PMI underwriting approach where full medical history is gathered at application; applicant usually accepted at standard terms but pre-existing conditions are specifically excluded.

PMI underwriting approach where no medical underwriting is done at application; instead, conditions existing in a pre-specified period (typically 2–5 years) before inception are automatically excluded, but are covered after typically 2 consecutive years with no treatment/symptoms/advice on those conditions.
PMI underwriting approach where the individual's past medical history is disregarded — no exclusions for pre-existing conditions. More common on group PMI schemes.
An arrangement on PMI or group business renewal where a new insurer agrees to provide cover at least as comprehensive as the policyholder's current policy, with no additional underwriting conditions.
A form of NWT acceptance where the new PMI insurer promises to carry forward only the cover for medical conditions that existed under the previous policy.

subsections.title: Reinsurance
bullets: Reinsurance is insurance for insurance companies — a key risk management tool.

Long-term insurance — three main reasons to reinsure: (1) cope with claim payout fluctuations; (2) finance new business strain (often using loans from the reinsurer); (3) obtain technical assistance and data for pricing new contracts.

Long-term reinsurance is mostly proportional (claim shared between insurer and reinsurer in pre-specified proportions), sold under a treaty covering all policies written during the reinsurance period (e.g. 1 year) for their whole term (e.g. 25 years).

Non-proportional reinsurance (excess of loss or stop loss) for long-term business is less common; may be used for group business requiring protection against catastrophe (e.g. multiple injuries) where total claim size is unknown.

Short-term insurance — three main reasons to reinsure: (1) protect against large claims; (2) take on larger/more risks than could be taken on otherwise; (3) reduce impact of accumulations of risk and catastrophes.
For short-term business, both proportional and non-proportional reinsurance can be used.

key_terms.definition: Reinsurance where the claim is shared between insurer and reinsurer in pre-specified proportions. Usually sold under a treaty covering all policies written in a period for their whole term. Predominant form for long-term H&C business.

Reinsurance where the reinsurer covers the amount of a claim above a specified threshold (excess of loss) or aggregate losses above a specified level (stop loss). Less common for long-term H&C business; more applicable to group business with catastrophe exposure.
A reinsurance agreement covering all policies written during a specified period (e.g. 1 year) for their full term (e.g. 25 years).

exam_qa.q: Q0.1(i): PMI is usually a one-year contract renewed annually with a new premium. Why might some policyholders (particularly older ones) be unhappy with this arrangement?
exam_qa.a: Premiums increase each year in line with expected claims costs, which rise as policyholders age. There may come a point where, even though the renewal premium is actuarially fair (expected claims cost plus small loading), the consumer cannot afford to pay it. Policyholders therefore face the possibility of lapsing the policy and bearing the risk themselves.

exam_qa.q: Q0.1(ii): Why is it difficult for insurers to offer long-term level-premium PMI policies to remove the above issue?
exam_qa.a: To offer level premiums for a long-term PMI contract, the insurer must assess long-term risks statistically — which is very difficult because average claim sizes are unlikely to remain stable over time. Changes include: medical inflation; the types of ailments requiring treatment; and treatment regimes used. As one ailment becomes cheaply treatable, others emerge and are treated using costly new regimes that are hard to predict. The wide distribution of cost estimates requires large contingency premium loadings, potentially making the resulting premiums unacceptably high.

exam_qa.q: Q0.2: Describe how new business strain is likely to arise for (a) CI policies with level regular premiums, (b) LTCI policies secured by a single premium.
exam_qa.a: New business strain arises when the initial asset share (premiums received less initial expenses) is less than supervisory reserves plus the solvency capital requirement; the shortfall is funded from the company's free assets. (a) CI (regular premium): The asset share is normally negative in the early years because high initial expenses — commission, sales and marketing, underwriting, and policy documentation — exceed the first premium received. Supervisory reserves must be at least zero; solvency capital is also required. Liabilities therefore exceed assets, producing new business strain. (b) LTCI (single premium): Despite the large positive net cash inflow at inception, new business strain commonly arises because the valuation basis is stronger (more prudent) than the premium basis, and/or because the company must hold required solvency capital — so the reserves on the valuation basis exceed the net premium received.

subsections.title: History of IP
bullets: IP insurance is the modern name for Permanent Health Insurance (PHI) — renamed to better reflect the coverage and attract more customers.
PHI name emphasised that the insurer must pay a valid claim as long as conditions are met (eg claimant is sick and below retirement age).
Also known as disability income insurance (USA) or long-term sickness insurance.
Name change was partly to avoid confusion with PMI — PHI pays a regular income, not medical costs.

key_terms.definition: Former name for IP insurance — emphasised the insurer's obligation to continue paying valid claims.
US name for IP insurance — provides regular income during periods of incapacity.

subsections.title: Definition and Aims
bullets: IP insurance provides the insured with regular, short- or long-term payments during periods of incapacity (inability to work due to illness or injury).
Benefits are in the form of a temporary annuity — continuing until policyholder recovers, dies, or policy expires.
Aim: to replace part of income lost while unable to work due to accident or illness.
A deferred period (up to 2 years) applies before benefits become payable — reduces premium and matches customer need (State/employer usually covers short-term sickness).
Premiums do not usually increase with age (unlike PMI), but inflation-linking is common; premiums may be reviewable at portfolio level.
Policy must carefully define what constitutes incapacity — not all illness/injury covered (eg attempted suicide, unemployment, redundancy excluded).
Policy is typically long-term — multiple separate claim periods can occur without the policy ceasing.
Guaranteed premiums: cannot be changed. Reviewable premiums: can be changed at review date for the whole portfolio.

key_terms.definition: Being unable to work due to illness or injury — the insured event under IP insurance.
Period of sickness that must elapse before IP benefits are paid; integrates with employer/State benefits and reduces premium.
Premiums that can be changed at a review date — the review applies to the whole portfolio, not individual policies.
Ratio of post-claim net income to pre-claim net income. Higher ratio = lower incentive to return to work = worse morbidity experience.

exam_qa.q: Why is underwriting more difficult for IP insurance than for term insurance?
exam_qa.a: Probability of claim: IP depends on many more factors (occupation, mental health) vs term (mainly age/gender). Size of claim: IP benefit duration is unknown (depends on length of sickness), whereas term sum assured is fixed. Additional difficulties with systems, managing claims, and measuring experience.

subsections.title: The Product Cycle
bullets: IP fits into the product cycle: product design → pricing → marketing/sales → underwriting → claims management → experience monitoring → valuation.
Product design is complex — admin systems must cope with recording claim start dates, reasons, cessation dates, and causes.
Pricing must reflect practices across the product cycle (eg weaker claims management = higher premiums).
IP may be actively bought by the self-employed; others need active marketing.
Underwriting and claims management are more complex for IP than most life products — both fundamentally drive claims experience.
Experience monitoring is complicated by actuarial difficulties in measuring morbidity risk.

key_terms.definition: The iterative process: product design → pricing → marketing → underwriting → claims management → monitoring → valuation, feeding back into design.

subsections.title: General Policy Conditions
bullets: Policy conditions are the most complex part of IP design — must cover lapse, claim, recovery, partial recovery, retirement, and death scenarios.
Unclear conditions risk being interpreted by courts in favour of the claimant.
Conditions should: reflect the insurer's true intentions; give cushion against adverse events (eg war exclusions); be simple and unambiguous to aid sales, underwriting, and claims.
Simple conditions help policyholders understand coverage at sale and ensure accurate information on proposals — leading to accurate underwriting.

subsections.title: Benefit Definitions (Amount)
bullets: Replacement ratio = post-claim net income / pre-claim net income. Higher ratio → worse morbidity (lower incentive to return to work). A 1% rise may imply ~1% higher expected claim cost.
Over-insurance: benefit + other income sources must not exceed full pre-claim income — insurers typically limit benefit to 50–75% of pre-claim earnings.
Escalating benefits: benefits (and premiums) increase annually in line with inflation (eg RPI, NAEI) — protects real value of benefit; subject to maximum increase (eg 5% pa).
Proportionate/rehabilitation benefits: reduced benefit when partially incapacitated or returning to part-time work — incentivises rehabilitation.
Waiver of premium: premiums are waived during a valid claim — usually subject to a short deferred period (eg 3–6 months).
Tax treatment matters: if benefits are taxed as income, the effective replacement ratio is lower.

key_terms.definition: Situation where post-claim income (benefit + other sources) exceeds pre-claim income — creates perverse incentive not to return to work.
Reduced benefit paid when the claimant can work part-time — encourages gradual return to full employment.
Policy feature whereby premiums are not collected while a valid IP claim is in payment.
Benefit that increases each year in line with a specified inflation index, protecting its real value over long claim periods.

exam_qa.q: Why might increasing the benefit each year in line with an inflation index be popular with policyholders?
exam_qa.a: Protects the real value of benefit over time — ensures the level of cover is maintained relative to normal income as wages rise with inflation.

subsections.title: Benefit Definitions (Timings)
bullets: Waiting period: period at start of policy during which no benefits are paid — now uncommon; initial underwriting is preferred to reduce anti-selection.

Deferred period: period of sickness before benefits begin — common deferred periods: 4, 13, 26, 52 weeks (UK). Integrates with employer sick pay, reduces admin and claim costs, matches customer needs.
Split deferred: policy with two benefit levels, eg £250/week after 13 weeks rising to £500/week after 26 weeks.

Early notification clause: policyholder must notify insurer after (eg) 1 month of sickness, even if deferred period is longer — enables early rehabilitation support and thorough claim assessment before benefit starts.
Linked-claims period: deferred period is waived if sickness recurs within (eg) 26–52 weeks — encourages return to work without fear of resetting deferred period.
Expiry age/term: benefits cease at expiry age (usually retirement age — typically 50, 55, 60, or 65) or at end of fixed term. Minimum 5-year term ensures classification as long-term business.

key_terms.definition: Period at start of policy during which no benefit is paid regardless of sickness — now largely replaced by upfront underwriting.
If sickness recurs within the linked-claims period, the deferred period is waived, encouraging policyholders to attempt return to work.
Age at which IP benefits cease — typically aligned with expected retirement age (eg 60 or 65).

exam_qa.q: How does a linked-claims period encourage claimants to return to work?
exam_qa.a: Without it, a claimant who returns to work and relapses must serve another full deferred period. With a linked-claims period, they can try returning without that risk, removing a barrier to rehabilitation.

subsections.title: Claim (Incapacity) Definitions
bullets: Occupational definitions — the most common: Own occupation (most generous), Any occupation (most restrictive), Suited occupation (intermediate — unable to do work for which suited by training/experience).
'Own occupation' definition: unable to perform the specific duties of the policyholder's own job. Most generous to claimant; creates highest moral hazard.
'Any occupation' definition: unable to perform ANY occupation for remuneration. Most restrictive; rarely used in individual policies now.
'Suited occupation': unable to perform occupations for which reasonably suited by education, training, or experience. Common in group IP.
Activities of Daily Work (ADW): functional definition — unable to perform a given number of specific work-related activities. Used for higher-risk occupations.

Non-occupational definitions: Activities of Daily Living (ADL) — used for LTCI and sometimes IP for non-workers; typically 2 of 6 ADLs (washing, dressing, feeding, mobility, continence, transferring).
Changing definitions: policy may start with own-occupation definition and switch to any/suited occupation after (eg) 2 years — reduces moral hazard for long-term claims.

key_terms.definition: Claimant is incapacitated if unable to perform the duties of their specific occupation — most generous; highest moral hazard.
Claimant is incapacitated only if unable to perform any work for remuneration — most restrictive definition.
Incapacity defined as inability to work in any occupation for which the person is reasonably suited by training, education, or experience.
Functional incapacity test: typically 2 of 6 basic tasks (washing, dressing, feeding, mobility, continence, transferring).

exam_qa.q: What are the advantages and disadvantages of using the 'own occupation' definition for IP insurance?
exam_qa.a: Advantages: clear and unambiguous; policyholder knows exactly when entitled to claim; attractive product. Disadvantages: highest moral hazard — claimant may not return to work even if capable of other employment; most expensive for insurer to price; significant risk of over-insurance.

subsections.title: Risk and Rating Factors
bullets: Key rating factors: age at entry, gender (where permitted), occupation class, deferred period, benefit amount, smoker/non-smoker status, policy term/expiry age.
Occupation is a critical rating factor — affects both probability and duration of claim. Occupations are grouped into classes (eg Class 1 = professional/office, Class 4/5 = manual/hazardous).
Mental illness claims are a major risk — difficult to assess, long duration, hard to manage. Growing proportion of claims in many markets.
Geographical/regional variations in claims experience are significant — some insurers use region as a rating factor.
Claim duration is as important as claim inception rate — longer claims are far more expensive per inception.
Aggregate policy limits may be placed on the total annual benefit payable to all policyholders in a household.

key_terms.definition: Classification of occupations by claim risk — typically Class 1 (low risk: professional/office) to Class 4/5 (high risk: manual/hazardous).
Probability of a new claim starting in a given period — one of two key components of IP claim cost (the other being claim duration).

subsections.title: Variations of the Product
bullets: Short-term IP (accident & sickness, A&S): benefit limited to a maximum period (typically 1 or 2 years). Lower premium than full IP; sold widely through bancassurance and group channels.
A&S is simpler to price and administer — limited claim duration means lower tail risk.
Mortgage payment protection insurance (MPPI): pays mortgage instalments during incapacity. Often written on A&S basis.
Long-term IP (full IP): benefits potentially to retirement age. More complex underwriting and claims management.
Unit-linked IP: protection element embedded in an investment-linked contract — rare.
Group IP: employer purchases policy to cover employees. Simpler underwriting (free cover limit), different distribution, commission, and pricing basis.

key_terms.definition: Income protection with benefit capped at a maximum duration (eg 1–2 years) — lower cost and simpler than full IP.
IP product specifically designed to cover mortgage payments during a period of incapacity.
Level of group IP benefit that can be granted without individual medical underwriting — claims above this trigger individual assessment.

subsections.title: Introduction to CI Insurance
bullets: CI insurance pays a lump sum (or sometimes income) on diagnosis of one of a defined list of serious conditions, or on undergoing a specified surgical procedure.
Usually a pure protection product — sum insured payable on first insured event (diagnosis, death, or policy expiry — whichever comes first).
Regular premiums paid until the insured event occurs, the insured dies, or the term ends.
If the policyholder suffers a CI event and survives, the sum insured is paid and the policy ends.
CI insurance covers both morbidity and (sometimes) mortality risk — a death benefit may be included.
Historically grew out of the recognition that surviving a heart attack or cancer diagnosis creates a large financial need, even if not permanently disabled.

key_terms.definition: Pure protection product paying a lump sum on diagnosis of a specified serious condition or on performing a specified surgical procedure.
CI benefit structure where the CI sum insured is an advance (acceleration) of the life sum insured — if CI is claimed, the life cover is reduced.

subsections.title: Characteristics of CI Insurance
bullets: Benefit is typically a fixed lump sum — policyholder decides how to use it (pay off mortgage, fund treatment, replace income).
Unlike IP, CI does not require the policyholder to be unable to work — benefit is paid on diagnosis regardless of ability to work.
CI is usually written as long-term insurance (terms > 5 years), although short-term versions exist.
Premiums are usually level throughout the term (or reviewable), unlike IP which may escalate.
Can be sold standalone or as a rider to life insurance (accelerated or additional benefit basis).
Accelerated basis: CI sum = life sum; if CI claimed, life cover ends. Additional basis: CI sum is extra over and above the life cover.
Products may be sold on with-profits, unit-linked, or without-profits basis.
Increasing use of partial/tiered payments for less severe conditions (eg early-stage cancer) — reduces cost and moral hazard.

key_terms.definition: CI policy sold independently of any life insurance — pays solely on the occurrence of a defined critical illness.
CI benefit paid on top of (additional to) the life sum insured — both CI and death benefits can be paid.
Premiums fixed throughout the policy term — common for CI; distinguish from the escalating premiums used in some IP products.

exam_qa.q: What is the difference between 'accelerated' and 'additional' CI benefit structures?
exam_qa.a: Accelerated: the CI sum is paid from (reduces) the life sum insured — if CI is claimed, life cover is extinguished or reduced. Additional: CI benefit is paid in addition to the full life sum insured, so both can be paid independently.

subsections.title: Core and Additional Conditions
bullets: Conditions covered are exhaustive (listed in the policy) — only named conditions are insured.
Core conditions (typically always included): cancer, heart attack, stroke. These three account for the majority of CI claims.

Additional conditions vary by insurer and market — may include: kidney failure, major organ transplant, multiple sclerosis, total permanent disability, Parkinson's, Alzheimer's, blindness, deafness, loss of limbs, coma, aorta graft, coronary artery bypass, heart valve replacement.
ABI Model Wording (UK) was developed to standardise definitions — reduces disputes but limits product differentiation.
Each condition must be precisely defined — ambiguity leads to claim disputes. Definitions typically specify: diagnostic criteria, required medical evidence, and minimum severity.
Exclusions are common: pre-existing conditions, self-inflicted injuries, alcohol/drug abuse.
Survival period: typically claimant must survive a minimum period (eg 14–28 days) after diagnosis before benefit is paid — reduces the overlap between CI and death benefits.

key_terms.definition: The standard set of CI conditions always included: cancer, heart attack, stroke — account for the vast majority of claims.
UK standard definitions for CI conditions developed by the Association of British Insurers — promotes consistency and reduces claim disputes.
Minimum period the claimant must survive after the CI event before the benefit is paid (typically 14–28 days).
CI conditions that were present or symptomatic before policy inception are excluded from cover.

exam_qa.q: Why are CI conditions defined in an exhaustive list rather than a broad definition of serious illness?
exam_qa.a: Exhaustive list gives both insurer and policyholder certainty about what is covered. A broad definition creates ambiguity, claim disputes, and makes pricing very difficult. The insurer must be able to estimate the expected cost of claims for each condition separately.

subsections.title: Terminal Illness
bullets: Terminal illness (TI) benefit: an accelerated payment of the CI/life sum insured when the insured is diagnosed with a condition expected to cause death within a specified period (typically 12 months).
TI is not the same as CI — CI pays on diagnosis of specific conditions; TI pays when any condition is expected to cause death imminently.
TI benefit allows the insured to use funds while alive — for care, family provision, or bucket-list spending.
Underwriting perspective: TI accelerates what would otherwise be a death claim — limited additional risk if already covered by life insurance.

key_terms.definition: Accelerated payment of sum insured when life expectancy is less than a defined period (typically 12 months) — allows early payment while the insured is alive.

subsections.title: Total and Permanent Disability (TPD)
bullets: TPD is often included as a CI condition — pays if the insured is permanently and totally unable to work in any/own/suited occupation.
Definition of TPD is critical — must specify: occupation test (own/any/suited), permanence criteria, and medical evidence requirements.
ADL-based TPD: incapacity judged by inability to perform a specified number of ADLs — used for non-working claimants and older ages.
TPD is the hardest CI condition to assess — permanence is difficult to establish early; moral hazard is significant.
Some policies use a 'loss of limbs' or 'loss of sight' definition as a proxy for permanent disability.

key_terms.definition: CI condition where the claimant is permanently unable to work in a defined category of occupation — permanently and totally disabled.

subsections.title: Children's Benefit
bullets: Some CI policies extend cover to the policyholder's children — a reduced lump sum (eg 25% of sum insured) paid on diagnosis of a specified CI condition in a child.
Conditions for children may differ from adult conditions — certain childhood illnesses (eg Type 1 diabetes) may be included.
Often included at no extra premium — reflects relatively low probability of childhood CI claims.
Provides financial support for a parent needing to take time off work to care for a seriously ill child.

subsections.title: Definition of Tiered Benefits
bullets: Traditional CI pays full sum insured for all covered conditions — but severity of conditions varies enormously (eg early-stage basal cell carcinoma vs advanced cancer).
Tiered (or multi-benefit) CI: pays different percentages of the sum insured depending on severity of the condition.
Typically: full payment for severe conditions; partial payment (eg 10–25%) for less severe/early stage conditions.
Partial payment conditions may be payable multiple times — up to the full sum insured across all partial claims.
After a partial claim, the remaining sum insured is reduced; policy may continue for the balance.

key_terms.definition: CI design where payout varies by severity — full sum insured for serious conditions; partial payment for less severe/early-stage events.
Payment of a fraction of the CI sum insured for a less severe defined condition — policy continues for the remaining balance.

subsections.title: Reasons for Offering Tiered Benefits
bullets: Consumer need: early-stage cancers do not necessarily cause inability to work or require large financial sums — a reduced payout better matches actual financial impact.
Competitive advantage: tiered products cover more conditions and offer more perceived value.
Insurer perspective: partial payments for minor conditions are lower cost than full payment — reduces claim severity risk.
But: increases complexity — more conditions to define and administer; more potential for disputes.
Potential anti-selection: policyholders with early-stage conditions may be more likely to buy tiered products.
Pricing challenge: estimating expected partial claims requires more granular morbidity data.

exam_qa.q: What are the main problems for the insurer in offering tiered CI benefits?
exam_qa.a: Greater complexity in definitions and administration; more conditions to price (requires more data); greater risk of disputes at claim stage; potential anti-selection on tiered conditions; partial claims may be paid repeatedly, increasing overall liability.

subsections.title: Defining Long-term Care
bullets: Long-term care (LTC) may be defined as all forms of continuing personal or nursing care and associated domestic services for people unable to look after themselves without support — provided in their own home, at a day centre, or in a care home.

LTC is essentially for people who are not going to get better — it is distinct from acute medical care, which aims to cure or alleviate conditions. LTC treats the results of a condition (reduced ability with daily tasks), not the condition itself.
SP1 focuses on LTC for the elderly, but LTCI benefits can be paid for care needs arising at any age (eg those disabled from birth or as the result of an accident).
The responsibility for LTC is often split between the State (via welfare programmes) and the individual (via accumulated assets). The insurance solution focuses on the latter.

The costs of care can be divided into: living costs (food, clothing, heating), housing costs (rent, mortgage, adaptations such as a bath hoist), and personal care costs (the additional costs of being looked after). For insurance purposes, it is the extra costs — not the total costs — that matter.

Personal care includes nursing care (requiring a qualified nurse's knowledge or skills — the most expensive form) and other forms of personal care. Intermediate care focuses on recuperative services following an acute event to reduce hospital re-admission.

Uncertainties individuals face in assessing their LTC needs include: likelihood of needing care (depends on medical advances, genetic predisposition), extent of care needed, and duration (depends on longevity and mortality improvements).

key_terms.definition: Continuing personal or nursing care for people unable to look after themselves, provided at home, in a day centre or in a care home. Designed to help with the results of a condition rather than cure the condition itself.
The most expensive and narrowest form of LTC — care requiring the specific knowledge or skills of a qualified nurse.
Recuperative services following an acute event (eg stroke, heart attack) aimed at reducing avoidable hospital readmission and minimising dependence on ongoing LTC.

exam_qa.q: Outline the uncertainties individuals face when assessing their long-term care needs in old age.
exam_qa.a: Likelihood of needing care: depends on medical advances and individual predisposition to debilitating conditions. Extent of care: depends on medical/technological advances, speed of deterioration, and availability of informal support. Duration: depends on longevity (life expectancy figures are averages) and future mortality improvements, both of which are highly uncertain for any individual.

subsections.title: Informal and Formal Care
bullets: Care may be provided informally (normally by a spouse or children) or formally (by paid carers). Much informal care is limited to four hours per week, but a smaller group provides substantial personal care — many of these informal carers are themselves over age 60 (as those needing care may be in their 80s/90s, so carers are often a partner, sibling, or adult child).
Informal care has an indirect cost: either lost economic activity or the cost of replacing the care if the carer can no longer provide it.

Formal care can be delivered in many settings: in the person's own home (preferred by most and often encouraged by government policy), in the homes of relatives, in privately owned residential homes, or in State-sponsored care homes.

subsections.title: Pre-funded vs Immediate Needs Plans
bullets: There are two generic types of LTCI: (1) pre-funded plans — purchased by relatively healthy people to protect against the risk of future disability; (2) immediate needs plans — purchased by current long-term care claimants to protect against uncertain survival duration.
Pre-funded plans can be secured by a single premium at outset or by level annual premiums (paid throughout life or to a specified age), with a premium waiver when a claim is being paid.

Immediate needs plans are in practice impaired-life annuities, secured by a single premium when the policyholder already needs care. They are not indemnity products — they pay a guaranteed cash income.

LTCI may ideally indemnify the policyholder for additional day-to-day costs when in need of care, but to control claims costs these payments may be subject to overriding maxima. Alternatively, products may provide cash benefits rather than covering direct care costs.

key_terms.definition: An LTCI product purchased by a relatively healthy person to protect against the risk of future disability. Premiums may be single or regular.
An LTCI product purchased by a person already in need of long-term care, providing a guaranteed lifetime income (equivalent to an impaired-life annuity) in return for a single premium.

exam_qa.q: Why is poor health bad news for pre-funded plans but good news for immediate needs plans?
exam_qa.a: Pre-funded plans: poor health increases the likelihood of claims and reduces the expected premium-paying period, so premiums must increase. Immediate needs plans: funded by a single premium, so poor health means a shorter expected annuity payment period. For a given level of annuity, the worse the proposer's health, the lower the single premium required.

subsections.title: Claim Definition — ADLs and Cognitive Impairment
bullets: Most pre-funded LTCI products use Activities of Daily Living (ADLs) and cognitive impairment as the claims trigger. The number of ADLs failed indicates the level of dependency.

Standard ADLs: washing, dressing, feeding, toileting, mobility, transferring. Mental impairment (from an organic cause such as Alzheimer's or irreversible dementia, excluding depression) is usually an overriding trigger — benefit is payable when satisfied regardless of ADL performance.
Different benefit levels may be payable at different levels of disability (eg 50% benefit on failure of 2/6 ADLs; 100% on failure of 3+ or mental impairment).

LTCI benefits can be provided as a rider to another policy: as an IP rider (extending cover beyond retirement using ADL-based definition), a CI rider (TPD definition switches to ADL-based at age 60), a PMI rider (care for disabling chronic conditions), or a whole of life rider (accelerates a % of sum assured on meeting LTC criteria).

An IP+LTCI rider is generally considered a poor design: the benefit level under IP depends on earnings while the LTC benefit depends on care costs, so the two benefits don't integrate well. CI, PMI and whole of life provide better natural overlaps with LTCI.

key_terms.definition: Defined functional tasks — washing, dressing, feeding, toileting, mobility, transferring — used as the claims trigger in most pre-funded LTCI products.
An overriding LTCI claims trigger based on loss of mental capacity from an organic cause (eg Alzheimer's, irreversible dementia). Payable whether or not ADLs are failed.

exam_qa.q: What are the advantages and disadvantages of having benefit levels vary with the number of ADLs failed?
exam_qa.a: Advantages: care provided at an early stage may slow deterioration and improve quality of life; better meets customer needs; may differentiate the product. Disadvantages: increased administration; more complex waiver of premium calculation; harder to price; more complex product may be harder to sell.

subsections.title: Benefit Types
bullets: Benefits may be: a single lump sum, an annuity certain, a lifetime benefit (subject to ongoing disability), or a restricted benefit (capped by time period or maximum total).
Benefits can be defined on an indemnity basis (insurer pays actual care costs, usually subject to a cash maximum) or as fixed cash amounts specified in the policy.

Indemnity benefits ensure costs are actually covered but are more expensive and difficult to administer. Cash (non-indemnity) benefits are simpler and cheaper but may be inadequate if care costs escalate or if the claimant is incapable of selecting care independently.

Under cash plans, there is a risk that inflation erodes the real value of the benefit — the policyholder may discover at the time of claim that the cash is insufficient to buy adequate care, which could lead to mis-selling complaints.

To reduce the premium while retaining an indemnity basis: remove supplementary cash benefits (assistive devices, death/surrender), reduce benefit escalation rates, limit the benefit payment period, or increase the deferred period.
Death benefits are sometimes provided (eg return of part of a single premium on early death). Surrender benefits may also be available on more comprehensive products.
Assistive devices (eg wheelchairs, hearing aids) and independent care advice may also be included in some products.

key_terms.definition: LTCI benefit that pays the actual assessed cost of care required (subject to policy limits), rather than a pre-specified cash amount.
A pre-specified fixed amount payable on satisfying the claim trigger, regardless of actual care costs. Simpler and cheaper but may not be sufficient if costs escalate.

subsections.title: Premiums
bullets: Methods of funding include: single premium (up-front), regular premiums (throughout life or until a specified age), or retrospective payment from equity released on sale of the home.
Regular premiums typically escalate in line with the chosen benefit escalation rate and include a waiver of premiums when the policyholder is claiming (or reaches a less severe level of disability).

Premiums increase with age at entry and with deteriorating health because: (1) the expected premium payment period is shorter, (2) claims are more likely and may occur earlier, (3) less investment return is earned on premiums.

Guaranteed premiums cannot be changed; reviewable premiums can be changed at review date across the whole portfolio. Policyholders on fixed retirement incomes are not well-suited to reviewable premiums.

Guaranteed terms may be age-dependent (eg the guarantee takes effect at age 70 or 75). The cost of guaranteeing premiums at older ages is less significant because fewer policyholders survive to these ages, a premium increase would generate little income at that stage, and many will already be receiving (or about to receive) benefits.
Full guarantees are not offered in the USA or UK. Some insurers offer limited guaranteed protection of five or ten years instead.

The long-term nature of LTCI and the high variance of claims means guaranteed premiums include a substantial contingency loading — reviewable premiums are often significantly cheaper. But if experience improves, guaranteed rates remain fixed while reviewable rates can fall.

key_terms.definition: A premium rate that the insurer cannot increase or reduce throughout the life of the policy, regardless of changes in claims experience.
A premium rate that the insurer may adjust at review dates for the whole portfolio. Can be reduced if experience improves, but includes less contingency loading than guaranteed premiums.
A policy feature under which premiums cease to be payable when the policyholder is receiving LTC benefits (or reaches a specified level of disability).

exam_qa.q: Why are premiums higher if a pre-funded LTCI policy is taken out at an older age or in poor health?
exam_qa.a: Older age/poor health shortens the expected premium payment period, reducing the total premium income and investment return earned on premiums. Poor health also makes claims more likely and potentially earlier, increasing the present value of expected benefits. Both effects require a higher premium to make the product financially viable.

subsections.title: Product Structure
bullets: LTCI can be written as long-term or general (short-term) business, and can be written on- or off-shore.

Unit-linked investment plans can be written off-shore — beyond the legislation of the country of residence. These offer a wide range of investment funds and access to the unit fund on death or surrender, typically accumulating free from tax.

subsections.title: Key Features and Structure
bullets: Immediate needs products protect individuals already in need of care from uncertain survival duration, providing more certainty of capital costs. The insurance provides a guaranteed lifetime income for a single premium.
The premium is individually calculated based on the current health status of the applicant (impaired life basis). Benefits are typically cash amounts, not indemnity.
The benefit can be level or escalating (at a fixed rate, often 5% pa, or linked to an inflation index). Care costs are primarily staff-based so should rise in line with earnings over the long term.

Death benefits are often included and can be structured as: a minimum payment period, amortisation of the single premium (eg 25% returned if death in year 4 of a 4-year spread), or capital protection of part of the premium. The higher the death benefit, the less impact the policyholder's health has on the premium.

Immediate needs plans may use various product structures: purchased life annuities, pure endowments, or disability income covers (similar to IP but with LTCI claim definitions). The actuary must design a product that meets the policyholder's needs while minimising tax and capital requirements.
Some plans have pre-agreed benefit escalation with specific nursing homes to immunise against care cost inflation, though the insurer must make clear it is not responsible for the quality of care.

key_terms.definition: A single-premium annuity purchased by a person already in need of long-term care. Priced on impaired-life mortality. Provides guaranteed income for life, protecting against the risk of living too long (longevity risk).

exam_qa.q: Why might buying fixed-interest securities directly be inferior to purchasing an immediate needs annuity?
exam_qa.a: An annuity provides insurance against longevity risk (running out of capital). If the individual invests directly, they bear the risk that their savings are exhausted before death. At that point — in poor health and perhaps unable to supplement income — having to change care arrangements (eg move to a cheaper home) would be extremely difficult. Additional arguments: the insurer may achieve better investment terms than an individual; the individual may lack investment expertise; the insurer's expense loading may be modest relative to the value of longevity protection.

subsections.title: Unit-linked Investment Products
bullets: Unit-linked LTCI products were developed to make LTC cover more attractive by combining it with a flexible investment contract. The product is primarily an investment vehicle and secondarily an LTC insurance contract — often sold as an inheritance plan.
On a claim, LTC benefits are paid from the non-unit fund (protection element). The unit fund (savings element) may be used to extend the effective deferred period or may be partially/fully protected.

Three levels of fund protection are available: (1) protect the entire investment fund; (2) protect only the initial investment; (3) allow the fund to be fully exhausted before insurance benefits begin.

From the insurer's perspective, the choice of fund protection is equivalent to varying the deferred period. Full fund protection means the minimum (fixed) deferred period applies; partial protection means the effective deferred period is extended by the period the fund would take to exhaust at the benefit rate.

Risk charges are deducted monthly from the unit fund to cover the protection element. The longer the effective deferred period (ie the more the fund absorbs claims costs), the lower the monthly risk charge.
A protected fund requires a higher risk charge because the full LTC benefit must be paid from the non-unit fund from the outset, without a contribution from the unit fund.

key_terms.definition: The policyholder's investment account within a unit-linked contract. Monthly risk charges are deducted from this fund. On LTC claim, the fund may be used to pay some or all benefits (depending on the protection option chosen).
The insurer's fund within a unit-linked contract, used to meet protection benefits (LTC payments) and expenses. Funded by risk charges deducted from the unit fund.
A feature of unit-linked LTCI allowing the policyholder to choose whether some or all of their investment fund is returned on death or preserved during a claim, rather than used to pay LTC benefits.

exam_qa.q: Why does full fund protection lead to a higher risk charge in a unit-linked LTCI policy?
exam_qa.a: If the entire unit fund is protected, it cannot contribute to paying LTC benefits. The non-unit fund must cover all LTC benefit payments from the outset. The risk charge — deducted monthly from the unit fund — must therefore be higher to build up sufficient non-unit fund reserves. Conversely, if the fund is not protected, LTC benefits are initially paid from the unit fund, reducing the insurer's exposure in the early claim period and allowing a lower risk charge.

subsections.title: Pension-based Solutions
bullets: LTC has a natural link with pensions — both deal with post-retirement needs. Pension-linked LTCI solutions aim to provide LTC cover from pension savings, potentially using tax-free lump sums or ongoing pension income.

One approach is a pseudo-pension annuity: a conventional immediate annuity (from the pension fund) with a rider LTC benefit that increases the pension income when the LTC trigger is satisfied. This is structured as an impaired deferred life annuity.

Pension annuities could include a higher income from a specified age (eg 85) or when a disability criterion is met — the additional benefit does not necessarily increase overall pension costs, but may reduce the starting pension.
The absence of annuity purchase requirements (eg in the UK) means pensioners may have lump sums available to purchase standalone LTCI products.

Concerns about pension-based solutions: insufficient pension reserves, high employer cost burden. These carry more weight for those close to retirement, but pension-based solutions may be optimal for younger generations.

key_terms.definition: An LTCI solution incorporating LTC benefits into a pension arrangement, typically as a rider that increases pension income on satisfying an LTC trigger. Structured as an impaired deferred life annuity.

4.title: Private Medical Insurance and Related Products

subsections.title: Definitions and Nature of Cover
bullets: PMI is usually an indemnity-based product providing compensation for the cost of private medical treatment. It covers acute (curable) conditions rather than chronic (incurable) or long-term conditions, which are regarded as the responsibility of the State healthcare system.

Individual PMI is a short-term, annually renewable contract. At each renewal, premiums change (typically increasing with age and medical inflation) and the insurer can amend policy terms and conditions. The policy will not be re-underwritten at renewal.

The premium for an individual policy does not normally depend on individual claims experience, though some insurers operate no-claims discount (NCD) schemes. In practice, individual policies are rarely cancelled but the insurer may cease renewal of an entire product.

PMI is purchased primarily to access speedy, good-quality diagnosis and treatment where the State cannot provide it — particularly for acute conditions. The longer-term residual effects of illness may be adequately covered by the State service.

Typical inclusions: acute condition treatment, in-patient tests and surgery, hospital accommodation and nursing, specialist consultations. Typical exclusions: pre-existing conditions, GP services, chronic conditions, A&E treatment, cosmetic surgery, self-inflicted injuries, experimental drugs, war risks, hazardous pursuits.

key_terms.definition: A medical condition that is expected to be cured as a result of active treatment. PMI covers acute conditions.
A medical condition for which there is no prospect of improvement through treatment (though treatment may make life more comfortable). Usually excluded from PMI.
Treatment involving an overnight stay in hospital (eg major surgery).
Treatment received during day visits that do not involve an overnight stay (eg physiotherapy, dressing changes).
A premium discount applied to PMI policies that have not made a claim in recent years. Discount scales tend to be smaller than for other short-term business.

exam_qa.q: Why does PMI usually exclude chronic conditions?
exam_qa.a: Chronic conditions require ongoing treatment for many years, making claims costs very high and highly uncertain. This uncertainty requires large pricing margins. The resulting premiums would be unaffordable for most policyholders and would make PMI appear poor value. The cost and complexity of ongoing claims administration is also significant.

subsections.title: Policy Excess and Cost Control
bullets: An excess (deductible) requires the policyholder to meet the first tranche of any claim cost personally. This lowers the premium and creates an incentive for policyholders to seek treatment through the public system for minor conditions.

A co-insurance arrangement shares the claim cost in fixed proportions between insurer and policyholder (eg insurer pays 75%, policyholder pays 25%). Advantages: reduces moral hazard and claim exaggeration, lowers premiums. Disadvantages: insurer is involved in every claim (higher admin cost); no cap on policyholder liability.
NCD scheme: encourages policyholders to absorb small claims, reducing claims frequency and admin costs. Risk of being viewed as discouraging policyholders from seeking needed treatment.
Policy limit (maximum claim per year): limits insurer's liability and lowers premiums. Risk of bad publicity if cover runs out during serious illness, particularly for longer-serving policyholders.
Preferred provider arrangements: insurer directs policyholders to a restricted list of hospitals, buying in bulk to control costs.

key_terms.definition: The amount of each claim met by the policyholder before the insurer pays. Reduces premium and incentivises use of the public system for minor conditions.
A PMI condition where the policyholder pays a fixed proportion of each claim (eg 20%), with the insurer paying the remainder.
An arrangement where the insurer negotiates discounted rates with a network of hospitals; policyholders must use these hospitals to receive full coverage.

exam_qa.q: Why is A&E treatment usually not covered under PMI?
exam_qa.a: A&E is typically provided by the State and is free at the point of use. Private hospitals cannot reliably provide fast emergency infrastructure. The usual claims assessment and pre-authorisation process is impractical in emergencies. Patients may be incapacitated and unable to contact the insurer. Patients in emergency situations are generally less concerned about accommodation quality or provider choice.

subsections.title: Major Medical Expenses and Waiting List Plans
bullets: Major Medical Expenses (MME): pays a lump sum when the policyholder undergoes surgery from a specified list. The lump sum is sized to cover in-patient costs and incidentals. There is no cover for out-patient episodes. Benefit is fixed — simpler claims administration — but may not cover extreme surgical complications. Can be written as short-term (annually renewable) or long-term (reviewable premium) business.

MME vs CI: MME triggers on undergoing a specified operation (not necessarily life-threatening, eg hip replacement); CI triggers on diagnosis of a specified serious illness (not necessarily requiring an operation). Some overlap exists for conditions requiring major surgery (eg heart bypass), but they are fundamentally different in structure.

Waiting list plans: provide standard PMI benefits only if the State health service cannot provide treatment within a specified period (typically 6 weeks). If the State can provide timely treatment, no benefit is payable. Designed for policyholders whose primary motivation is to avoid waiting for treatment.

Budget plans: cheaper PMI products achieved by restricting out-patient cover (monetary limits, time restrictions, initial consultation only) or limiting the range of hospitals (preferred provider arrangements). These target lower socioeconomic groups priced out of comprehensive PMI.

key_terms.definition: A product paying a fixed lump sum when the policyholder undergoes one of a specified list of surgical procedures. Covers in-patient costs but not out-patient treatment.
A PMI product that only pays benefits if the State health service is unable to provide the required treatment within a specified period (typically 6 weeks).
A lower-cost PMI product achieved by restricting out-patient cover or limiting the range of hospitals, aimed at widening distribution beyond higher socioeconomic groups.

subsections.title: Health Cash Plans
bullets: Health cash plans provide defined cash benefits for a range of healthcare-related events (dental, optical, physiotherapy, maternity, hospitalisation, recuperation, hearing aids, consultations) for low premiums (as little as $2/week).
Benefits may be limited to a percentage of the medical bill (eg 50%) — a form of coinsurance. Benefits are bought in 'units' with proportionally higher premiums for higher benefit levels.

Health cash plans are typically community-rated — no individual underwriting. Full medical underwriting is impractical because: premiums are small (underwriting costs would represent too large a loading), the process delays acceptance, and simplicity is a key selling point.

A waiting period (often 6 months) applies before benefit eligibility to reduce anti-selection in the absence of full underwriting. No waiting period usually applies for accidents. A moratorium may exclude pre-existing conditions.
Profitability depends on: long-term policyholder loyalty (so initial expenses can be spread over many years), cost-efficient claims administration, and large volumes in force to spread fixed costs.

Community rating: premiums do not vary by individual characteristics (age, gender, medical history). This creates cross-subsidy between policyholders — older/sicker policyholders pay less than their expected cost, subsidised by younger/healthier ones. This creates scope for selection against the insurer by older/sicker lives.

Lifetime community rating: allows premiums to vary by age at entry but remain level thereafter (if renewed). Reduces anti-selection by giving all individuals an incentive to join early (rather than waiting until older).

key_terms.definition: A low-premium product paying defined cash benefits for a range of healthcare events. Community-rated, typically with a waiting period. Success depends on high volumes and renewal rates.
A rating system where all policyholders (or a defined subset) are charged the same premium, regardless of individual characteristics such as age, gender or health status.
An initial period during which claims related to pre-existing conditions are excluded. If no related treatment occurs during the moratorium, the exclusion is removed.
An initial policy period during which premiums are paid but no benefit is payable. Designed to reduce anti-selection where full medical underwriting is not performed.

exam_qa.q: Why is full underwriting not feasible for health cash plans?
exam_qa.a: Cash plans have small premiums, so the cost of full medical underwriting would represent a disproportionate loading. This would reduce sales and make it harder to cover fixed costs. Cash plans also appeal through their simplicity and speed of acceptance — underwriting delays act as an additional disincentive to applicants.

exam_qa.q: How does community rating lead to cross-subsidy and potential selection against the insurer?
exam_qa.a: Under community rating, all policyholders pay the same premium regardless of risk. Higher-risk individuals (older, sicker) pay less than their expected claims cost, subsidised by lower-risk individuals. Better risks may therefore switch to individually-rated insurers (which charge them lower premiums), leaving the community-rated insurer with a deteriorating risk pool — a classic adverse selection spiral.

subsections.title: Dental and Optical Plans
bullets: Dental plans can be included within comprehensive PMI, within health cash plans, or purchased as standalone cover. Two principal methods: (1) capitation basis — insurer and dentist agree a fixed sum per insured mouth per year (dentist bears the claims risk, insurer handles administration and covers emergency treatment only); (2) indemnity basis — insurer bears the claims risk and pays actual treatment costs, often subject to limits, excesses or coinsurance.

Optical plans provide cover for spectacles, contact lenses, eye tests and optical treatments. May include waiting periods and exclusions for pre-existing conditions. Most often found within cash plans or comprehensive PMI, but available standalone.

key_terms.definition: A dental insurance structure where the insurer pays the dentist a fixed annual amount per insured mouth. The dentist bears the risk of providing more treatment than the capitation covers.

subsections.title: Benefits and Exposure
bullets: Personal accident policies are short-term, annually renewable products providing lump sum benefits for bodily injury from an accident. Benefits are fixed amounts for specified injuries (loss of limb, sight, hearing, permanent total disability, accidental death) — not indemnity products because the loss of a limb cannot be financially quantified.
Benefits can cover the policyholder's family. Rider benefits for children are common, usually at a lower level.

The standard exposure measure is the person-year. Where family details are not available, the member-year (or employee-year) is used — the insurer charges an average family rate and expects to be right on average.
Rating factors are usually simple: occupation is the primary factor (demolition vs clerical work); age and gender are generally not used. Detailed medical history questions are not usually asked.
For group schemes, the insurer may only know the member's age and industry. The industry serves as a proxy for the underlying risk factor (occupation).

key_terms.definition: A short-term product providing fixed lump sum benefits for bodily injury resulting from an accident. Not an indemnity product as the financial value of physical loss cannot be objectively determined.

A measure of the amount of risk underlying each policy. For personal accident, this is the person-year (or member-year). Should be a good indicator of expected claim frequency and severity, and be objectively measurable.
A personal accident benefit payable if the policyholder is permanently and totally incapacitated as a result of an accident. May take months or years to confirm.

exam_qa.q: What are the main differences between MME and CI policy benefits?
exam_qa.a: MME triggers on undergoing a specified operation (which may or may not be for a serious illness). CI triggers on diagnosis of a specified serious illness (which may or may not require an operation). Some overlap exists where CI lists specific operations (eg heart bypass), but CI also covers conditions not requiring surgery, while MME covers non-life-threatening operations (eg hip replacement) that CI would not cover.

subsections.title: Claim Characteristics
bullets: Personal accident claims are usually reported quickly and settled without much delay — the incidence of an event is generally clear, reducing reporting delays, and claim costs are predetermined by the benefit schedule.
Claims can be very large (often much higher than typical health insurance claims). Claim frequency tends to be reasonably stable.
Exception: permanent and total disability claims may take months or years to confirm, adding uncertainty.

5.title: Group Health and Care Insurance Products

subsections.title: Definition and Key Differences from Individual Business
bullets: Group business is any collection of individuals who combine to make a single proposal for uniform insurance cover. The employer is typically the policyholder; the employees are the insured.

Group business is written over a short period (typically one or two years), covering claims that occur during that period. This makes group policies short-term, regularly renewable products even when written by a long-term insurer.
Key difference from individual business: premiums for each period depend on the number and characteristics of covered individuals. LTCI group products are not common.

Flexible (cafeteria-style) benefit schemes allow employees to choose from a menu of benefits. These increase anti-selection risk (members choose benefits where they expect to claim) and administrative complexity for the insurer.
Group covers may have tax advantages over individual insurances, making the employer-provided benefit worth more to the employee than its cost to the employer.

key_terms.definition: Any collection of individuals who combine to make a single proposal for uniform insurance cover. Usually employer-sponsored, with the employer as policyholder and employees as the insured.
