An employer scheme offering a menu of benefit options from which employees can choose. Increases anti-selection risk and administrative complexity.

subsections.title: Purposes of Group Insurance
bullets: Group arrangements may be: a legal requirement (eg IP in some territories); a positive recruitment/retention message to employees; a mechanism to smooth early retirement (group IP) or promote return to work after illness (group PMI); or an employee benefit with tax advantages.
Group IP can act as a mechanism for early retirement when an employee is in continuing poor health. Group PMI can promote health and ensure speedy return to work following operations.

Group CI is used as part of the overall employee reward package — particularly valuable when TPD cover is included — and can cover occupations (eg some blue-collar) for which individual IP is not available.

subsections.title: Underwriting and Acceptance
bullets: Group underwriting uses 'average' assumptions: the underwriter assesses whether the group's overall experience will approximate that in the premium basis. Some unhealthy lives can be accepted because they are balanced by healthy lives.
Anti-selection is generally lower for group business than individual, but remains a risk. Extent depends on eligibility rules and whether a flexible benefit scheme is used.

Measures to restrict anti-selection: applying exclusions; setting free cover limits; requiring members to be 'actively at work' when cover begins; setting minimum take-up rates on voluntary schemes; imposing take-over terms for schemes transferring from another insurer.

Schemes with compulsory membership, determined-by-formula benefits (linked to salary), and a large number of members present less anti-selection risk — these can have higher free cover limits and smaller premium loadings.

Free cover: a certain level of benefit is automatically available to all group members without individual underwriting. Cover above the free cover limit requires individual medical evidence. Free cover limits are lower for smaller schemes (to avoid one individual carrying a large proportion of total risk).

Free cover does not mean the cover is free of charge — it means standard premiums apply without individual underwriting. The aim is to reduce underwriting costs and avoid inconveniencing most members.
Free cover applies to group IP and CI (where benefit levels vary by salary), but not group PMI (where benefits are indemnity-based and the benefit amount is unknown in advance).

Actively at work criterion: members must have been continuously at work (or unrestricted in daily activities, for the non-employed) during the past 2-3 months to qualify for free cover. Cheap and verifiable from employer records.

key_terms.definition: The level of benefit automatically granted to group scheme members without individual underwriting. Standard premiums apply. Only members seeking benefit above this limit need medical evidence.

A requirement that group members must have been at work (or unrestricted in daily activities) for a minimum recent period (typically 2-3 months) to qualify for free cover. Reduces the risk of anti-selection at scheme inception.

exam_qa.q: Why does compulsory membership, a minimum number of members, an actively at work criterion, and a prescribed benefit formula each enable the insurer to offer a higher free cover limit?
exam_qa.a: Compulsory membership: ensures good and bad risks join, allowing cross-subsidy. Minimum number of members: larger groups have a better mix of risks and cross-subsidy is more reliable. Actively at work criterion: excludes those currently sick, cheaply verified from employer records. Prescribed benefit formula (salary-linked): prevents worse risks from over-insuring relative to better risks, reducing the adverse impact on claims experience.

subsections.title: Benefits and Key Features
bullets: Group IP is used by employers to provide sick pay to employees who are unable to work due to illness or injury. Benefits are often based on salary gross of tax, though net pay schemes exist.

Additional benefits may include: employer and employee State welfare contributions; pension contributions during incapacity; continuation options (allowing a leaving employee to continue cover individually without medical evidence).
Group IP is effectively a short-term, annually renewable product even though written by a long-term insurer — cover and premiums are renegotiated each year based on current membership.
Group IP can be used to smooth the process of early retirement when an employee is in continuing poor health.

key_terms.definition: A feature of group schemes allowing a departing employee to continue their cover individually, without individual underwriting. Valuable for lives who may be impaired at departure.

subsections.title: Benefits, Purposes and Underwriting
bullets: Group CI operates on the same basic principles as group life insurance. It is part of the overall employee reward package and can attract and retain staff.
Group CI is particularly valuable for employees in occupations where individual IP is not available (eg some blue-collar occupations), especially when TPD cover is included.
The free cover limit for group CI depends on: size of the scheme, whether membership is compulsory or voluntary, take-up proportion if voluntary, and the total and average sum insured.

subsections.title: Benefits and Key Features
bullets: Group PMI benefits and exclusions are broadly similar to individual PMI, but pre-existing conditions are more likely to be covered under group business due to the lower anti-selection risk.

There is greater scope for benefit customisation for larger groups: choosing specific excess or limit levels; adding dental or prescription cover not on standard products; excluding benefits the group does not require (eg psychiatric treatment, complementary medicine).

For experience-rated large groups, the insurer may offer bespoke benefit structures even where it has limited experience data for those specific benefits — the group bears much of the claims risk via the high credibility factor.

Free cover limits are not used for group PMI because benefits are indemnity-based: the eventual claim amount is unknown in advance, making it impossible to determine which members would exceed a free cover limit.

exam_qa.q: Describe the underwriting procedures appropriate for a group CI scheme offered to parents of school pupils, where membership is voluntary and premiums are collected by the school.
exam_qa.a: The intermediary (school) has limited contact with parents, so procedures must be simple. No financial underwriting is needed — the benefit is formula-based (school fees) and earmarked for a specific purpose. If voluntary, offer free cover to those joining at inception. Require subsequent joiners to complete simple individual underwriting (DOB, smoking status, declaration of medical treatment, actively at work/unrestricted activities declaration). Consider a moratorium excluding pre-existing conditions in year 1. A free cover limit based on the number of children at the school could be applied. If large take-up, reduced underwriting is appropriate.

6.title: Product Design and Stakeholder Interests (1) – Customer Perspective

subsections.title: General Needs and Microinsurance
bullets: From a customer viewpoint, a good product should: meet needs; be clear in purpose (risks covered and benefits payable); have an appropriate charging structure; and above all have an affordable premium that offers value for money.
Health and care insurance products meet three specific needs: providing a lump sum to repay loans; replacing income; and financing medical treatment bills.

IP insurance: meets the need for replacement income when policyholders are unable to work. Benefits are typically limited to 60–75% of gross salary to maintain an incentive to return to work. Main buyers: self-employed individuals, employers (group IP), and small professional practices (eg Locum Protection insurance).

CI insurance: meets a variety of needs — repaying a mortgage or loan; funding medical costs; providing income via annuity; funding business partner buyout (Keyperson cover); lifestyle change after illness. Tiered benefit CI is a closer fit to medical distress and financial need, and is deemed fairer, but is more complex.

LTCI: meets the need for financial protection against costs of care when unable to look after oneself. Care needs progress from domestic support → live-in care → residential care → medical care. Demand depends on adequacy of State and informal (family/friends) provision.

PMI: assists the policyholder in covering the cost of medical care — complementing or replacing State provision. In markets with State provision, PMI is bought for shorter waiting times, better accommodation, choice of doctor, and local hospital access.

Microinsurance: targeted at those with low incomes (especially in developing world). Characterised by limited benefits and very low premiums. Main products: versions of PMI (hospitalisation, primary care) and IP. Often sold alongside microfinance.

key_terms.definition: A CI or life insurance policy taken out by business partners on each other's lives, where the benefit funds the buyout of the sick partner's stake in the business.
Insurance targeted at those with low incomes, characterised by very low premiums and limited benefits. Common in developing markets such as India and parts of Africa.
A CI product design where the sum insured varies according to the severity of the diagnosed illness, providing a closer match to the financial need and allowing cover for less severe conditions.

exam_qa.q: In what sense could you argue that PMI is more focused, and IP more comprehensive, than CI?
exam_qa.a: PMI is more focused: it is an indemnity product that pays for actual medical treatment expenses incurred — the payment is directly related to the actual loss. CI is less focused as it pays a fixed lump sum regardless of the actual loss suffered. IP is more comprehensive: it pays whenever the policyholder cannot work in their own occupation due to any illness or injury, whereas CI only covers specific pre-defined critical illnesses. For example, a broken arm preventing a carpenter from working would trigger an IP benefit but not a CI or PMI payment.

subsections.title: Cash vs Indemnity by Product
bullets: Cash benefits give the policyholder choice — they can be applied to any personal need, converted to income or used for any purpose. However, the customer must understand the likely costs to set an appropriate benefit level.

Indemnity benefits are directly related to the actual loss. An advantage is that the insurer may negotiate cheaper prices from healthcare providers due to volume, making treatment cheaper than if funded from a CI cash benefit.

IP: cash benefit, paid as income stream. May include a rehabilitation or proportional benefit to encourage return to work — benefiting both insurer (lower claim costs) and policyholder (faster recovery).

CI: cash benefit, paid as lump sum. The lump sum cannot be subsequently withdrawn by the insurer once paid. Can be more than the immediate cost of care — surplus available for recuperation or lifestyle change. Risk of 'windfall gains' where benefit greatly exceeds loss (eg angioplasty now a minor procedure) — can cause anti-selection and moral hazard.

LTCI: typically cash benefits (income stream). Few insurers offer indemnity benefits due to the difficulty of forecasting future care costs, longevity risk, and benefit inflation uncertainty. Immediate needs plans convert a single premium into a lifetime income at point of need.

PMI: normally an indemnity product — pays actual medical treatment costs, often directly to the provider. Exceptions include: health cash plans (partial cash contribution); daily benefit for use of State hospital; major medical expense plans (lump sum for surgery); personal accident plans.

key_terms.definition: A benefit that reimburses the actual costs incurred (eg treatment expenses), returning the policyholder to the financial position they were in before the insured event. Contrasts with a cash benefit of fixed amount.
A type of PMI variant that provides a partial cash contribution when certain medical events occur (eg physiotherapy, new spectacles), rather than full indemnification. Reduces anti-selection risk.

subsections.title: Peace of Mind by Product
bullets: Customers are only concerned about risks they recognise — many are unaware of the true likelihood of long-term sickness vs critical illness, leading CI to outsell IP in some markets.
IP: concern about being unable to provide for dependants or repay loans. State sickness benefits are often inadequate and employer benefits are usually short-term.
CI: provides financial peace of mind, but is not comprehensive — it only covers specific headline illnesses. A combination of IP and PMI may be better targeted and more comprehensive.

LTCI: concerns about insufficient funds for care in old age and inadequacy of State/informal provision. Key issue — policyholders may believe care is being bought, when only cash is provided. Some insurers address this with a care management advisory service.
PMI: concern about unaffordable treatment costs, waiting times, or quality of State provision. Employer may also share the concern about employees being unable to work.

subsections.title: Simplicity and Clarity
bullets: IP: simple concept but complications arise from: benefit limits (replacement ratio cap); payouts not always linked to current salary; complex underwriting (occupation, medical history); exclusions (alcohol, drug abuse, self-inflicted injury, war); difficulty defining incapacity objectively. ADLs, ADWs, FATs and PCAs are objective tests used.

CI: theoretically simple (fixed lump sum on diagnosis). Attractions: lump sum cannot be withdrawn; claims trigger (diagnosis or procedure) is easily explained. But: exclusions and point-of-claim underwriting complicate matters; claim definitions may differ between insurers or require evidence of severity beyond the layperson's understanding. Tiered CI is more complex — greater potential for claims dispute.

LTCI: concept is straightforward but confusion arises over: whether cash benefits will meet actual care costs; benefit caps or maximum payment periods; trigger points (ADLs, cognitive impairment) need explanation.

PMI: indemnification is clear in concept but complications arise from: underwriting/acceptance process; exclusions (chronic and pre-existing conditions, cosmetic surgery); cost-sharing (coinsurance, excesses/deductibles); pre-authorisation requirements; restrictions to hospital bands or networks.

key_terms.definition: A set of defined personal care activities (eg bathing, dressing, eating, mobility) used as objective trigger criteria for LTCI and some IP claims. Inability to perform a specified number of ADLs without assistance triggers the claim.
A PMI requirement for the insurer to approve a planned procedure before treatment begins, ensuring it is covered by the policy and performed in an appropriate facility. Reduces disputed claims.
A cost-sharing arrangement under which the policyholder pays a fixed percentage of every claim cost, with the insurer covering the remainder.

sections.title: Guaranteed vs Reviewable Premiums and Benefits

subsections.title: Guarantees and Reviewability by Product
bullets: Policyholders prefer guaranteed premiums (certainty of future outgo) but guarantees are expensive to price and reserve for, especially in health and care insurance where there are many factors outside the insurer's control.

IP: premiums may be guaranteed or periodically reviewable. Claims experience is affected by economic and social influences (eg unemployment trends). Selective lapsing limits the size of any premium increase under reviewable policies — capping increases at new business rates helps retention.

CI: originally premiums were reviewable due to unknown future risks. Competition pushed some insurers to offer guaranteed premiums at little extra cost, but the true reserving cost is now better understood. With many illness trends moving in different directions, future premium increases under reviews are difficult to predict.

LTCI — immediate needs: no premium guarantee issues — single premium buys a lifetime income stream. LTCI — pre-funded: reviewable premiums predominate. Degree of variation depends on claims experience and trend in care cost index.

PMI: almost invariably a one-year product, annually renewable. Premiums (and sometimes policy terms) can change at each renewal. Guaranteed premium products for limited periods (3–5 years) are rare and only viable when medical inflation is stable.

key_terms.definition: Premiums that cannot be changed by the insurer throughout the policy term. Provide certainty to the policyholder but are expensive to price, as the insurer must hold sufficient reserves to cover all future adverse scenarios.
Premiums that the insurer may change at defined review dates, based on the emerging experience of the whole portfolio. The policyholder retains the option to accept or decline the new terms.

exam_qa.q: Outline the factors that influence the suitability of offering guaranteed vs reviewable premiums on long-term health and care insurance business.
exam_qa.a: Factors favouring guaranteed premiums: customer preference for certainty; competitive pressure (if competitors offer guarantees); simpler product (easier to sell); less risk of selective lapsing on review. Factors favouring reviewable premiums: less need for prudent reserves upfront (cheaper premium); greater ability to respond to adverse experience (eg new diseases, medical advances for CI); economic and social influences on IP claims are hard to predict long-term; for PMI, claims inflation is inherently unpredictable over multiple years; if the insurer has limited capital, smaller reserves required for reviewable products; where data are scarce, pricing is uncertain and review allows correction. In practice, the insurer must balance cost competitiveness, policyholder expectations, regulatory requirements, and its own risk appetite.

7.title: Product Design and Stakeholder Interests (2) – Insurer, Regulator and Other Stakeholders

subsections.title: Insurer Product Design Considerations
bullets: Product design and pricing is an iterative procedure balancing: customer acceptability; regulator requirements; needs of distributors; price competitiveness; adequate profitability/return on capital; company culture in product style and price; systems and other internal constraints; and underwriting methodology.

Customer acceptability: the product must be clear about both the benefits provided (claims triggers, cash values) and the amounts and variability of premiums. Innovative features and options increase marketability but must be understood by the target customer base — simplicity matters for the chosen distribution channel.
Adequate profitability: premiums must cover benefits, expenses, and leave a surplus to reward shareholders. The balance between competitiveness and profitability is a fundamental management task.

Company culture: new product structures should be similar to existing products to avoid systems development costs, staff retraining, and the risk of dissatisfying existing policyholders who may feel unfairly treated.

Systems constraints: IT must record all processes, provide profitability information, and be capable of handling the product design. Systems limitations may restrict product features. Data capture is critical for pricing, monitoring, and repricing.

Underwriting methodology: medical underwriting is a fundamental element of product design. For long-term guaranteed premium policies, stringent underwriting is most important as there is no opportunity to revise terms. For one-year renewable policies (PMI), moratorium underwriting (collecting evidence only at claim) reduces expense but increases anti-selection risk.
Claims procedures must be consistent with underwriting criteria and pricing data. They should match policyholder expectations and broadly match competitor practices to avoid adverse anti-selection.

Risk pricing: a clear set of conditions and policy rules enables statistical costing and controls anti-selection and moral hazard. Reinsurer assistance is often vital for data, contract design, and pricing, especially for new products.

Financing requirement: the insurer will want to minimise its capital strain on new business. Guarantees have a big influence on up-front reserve requirements. This is particularly important for relatively new health and care insurers with limited free assets.

Cross-subsidies: where premium averaging is needed, the actuary must monitor the mix of business to ensure cross-subsidies do not create systematic losses. The rights of different policyholder classes must not be diminished.

key_terms.definition: The tendency for those with a higher-than-average risk to seek insurance cover, while lower-risk individuals decline. Arises when the insurer does not capture all relevant risk information. Also known as adverse selection.
The risk that a policyholder's behaviour changes adversely as a result of having insurance — eg taking greater risks or using more healthcare services than they otherwise would.

A PMI underwriting approach where medical evidence of existing conditions is only collected at the point of claim, rather than at inception. Reduces initial expense but increases the risk of accepting sub-standard risks.

The initial negative cashflow arising when a new policy is written, equal to the initial supervisory reserve plus solvency capital plus initial expenses minus the first premium. Must be financed from free assets.

exam_qa.q: Why is reinsurer expertise more important for health and care insurance products than for life insurance protection products?
exam_qa.a: Health and care insurance involves greater complexity — incidence rates are harder to predict, claim definitions are more subjective, and experience data are scarcer. When launching a new health and care product (eg CI in a new market), a reinsurer can provide: statistics from other markets; relevant population data; actuarial and pricing expertise; assistance with contract wording; regulatory knowledge; and training for internal staff. Reinsurers accumulate experience across many insurers and territories, making their data particularly valuable in markets with limited historical data. For standard life insurance protection (term assurance), mortality data are well established and standard tables are widely available, making external reinsurer expertise less critical.

sections.title: Product-Specific Control Procedures for the Insurer

subsections.title: Control by Product
bullets: IP: initial underwriting/acceptance with premiums reflecting risk factors (occupation, age, gender where permitted); claims underwriting (checking benefit against current salary); use of claims counsellors; monitoring by specialist nurses throughout claims.

CI: effective underwriting and claims control to guard against anti-selection and non-disclosure (especially important for tiered benefit CI); survival period on stand-alone policies (avoids 'death' claims); adequate IBNR reserves for late-notified claims; managing policyholder expectations as new diseases emerge; careful policy wording (especially for tiered benefits); large margins and/or reinsurer co-operation where data are scarce.
LTCI: policy designs must be robust to changes in State provision of care and regulatory interpretation of benefits promised.
PMI: control of claim costs by agreeing and monitoring provider prices; pre-authorisation of benefits; ensuring premium increases can be justified to policyholders at renewal.

key_terms.definition: Claims that have been incurred (the insured event has occurred) but have not yet been formally notified to the insurer. Reserves must be held to cover expected IBNR claims.

subsections.title: Regulatory and Other Stakeholder Interests
bullets: Regulators' priorities: (1) the insurer remains solvent; (2) consumers are considered in all decisions and disadvantages to consumers in sales, administration, and claims are minimised.

Regulatory requirements may include: adequate and accurate reporting to the regulator; approval of premium increases; clear and unambiguous policy wording; a range of suitable benefits representing reasonable value for money; monitoring of sales processes and target market appropriateness.

Distributors (intermediaries, tied agents, own salesforce): interested in having products that are easy to explain, competitively priced, and carry sufficient commission. The sales incentive must be sufficient to encourage distribution but appropriate from a regulatory perspective.

Actuarial profession: professional guidance gives actuaries a framework for maintaining professional standards and may provide protection against pressure from management. Guidance typically covers: policy conditions; adequacy of premium rates; value of the liabilities. Professional guidance must not unduly restrict actuaries' judgement.

Good product design rule of thumb: ensure there is a customer need; price accurately, projecting a range of outcomes; charge the cost of capital to the product; obtain sound reinsurance; ensure policy literature clearly describes guarantees; ensure adequate reserves are in place.

subsections.title: Four Channels and Worksite Marketing
bullets: Insurance intermediaries (brokers): act independently of any particular insurer; find the contract that best meets the client's needs in terms of benefits and premiums; remunerated by commission from insurers or fees from clients. Clients usually initiate the sale. Risk of mis-selling if intermediaries are motivated by commission levels rather than client suitability.

Tied agents: tied to one (or sometimes a few) insurer(s); offer only those insurers' products (ranges are usually mutually exclusive). Typically employees of a bank or other financial institution — bancassurance. Remunerated by commission from the insurer. Active selling is common. Product ranges of multiple companies are usually mutually exclusive to avoid conflicts.

Own salesforce: employees of the insurer, selling only that insurer's products. Remunerated by salary, commission, or a mixture. Sales are usually initiated by the salesperson. Benefits from having a 'warm' client base once established.

Direct marketing: four forms — mailshots (insurer initiates); telephone selling (either party initiates); press advertising (debatable); internet/comparison websites (usually client initiates). Internet enables basic underwriting at quotation stage. Simple products (cash plans, basic PMI) are most suitable for internet distribution.

Worksite marketing: a broker or insurer representative obtains permission from an employer to address the entire workforce. Intended to sell simple products to individuals who lack cover; these are individual (not group) policies and continue independently of employment. LTCI is unsuitable for worksite marketing (too complex, post-employment need, expensive). CI, IP, and health cash plans are suitable.

key_terms.definition: The distribution of insurance products through a bank's distribution network. The bank acts as a tied agent for its associated insurance company, marketing products to its existing customer base.

A distribution approach where an insurer or broker obtains permission from an employer to address the workforce and sell individual health and care insurance products, using the workplace as a convenient point of access.

subsections.title: Types of Commission
bullets: Initial and renewal commission: high initial commission for a defined initial period (eg 24 months), then a lower renewal commission thereafter — both expressed as percentages of premiums in the same period. PMI equivalents: higher commission for new policies vs renewals.

Indemnity commission: a form of initial commission — a lump sum paid immediately when the policy is sold (typically as a percentage of the first premium). Involves new business strain as commission is paid before premiums are fully received. A credit check on the distributor is required.

Clawback: the repayment of commission by the intermediary if a policy lapses before the commission is fully earned. The insurer bears a credit risk that the intermediary may be unable to repay (eg through bankruptcy). The proportion clawed back typically varies with the premiums actually paid relative to those expected.

Renewal commission: a lesser ongoing amount paid after the initial period to encourage persistency; paid annually on long-term policies after the earnings period, or annually on renewal for short-term policies.

Level commission: a fixed proportion of every premium paid, throughout the policy term. Does not involve new business strain; matches commission outgo to premium contributions and profitability. Less popular with intermediaries (lower initial payment) but more suitable for the insurer's cashflow.
Commission may also be expressed as a proportion of the sum insured (rather than the premium), which better reflects the insurer's preference for higher sums at risk.

key_terms.definition: A lump sum commission paid to the distributor immediately on new business being written, representing an advance against future premiums. Involves new business strain and a credit risk on the distributor.
The recovery by the insurer of part of the initial commission paid to a salesperson when a policy lapses before the commission is fully earned. Subject to credit risk if the intermediary defaults.
A commission structure where a fixed percentage of every premium is paid to the distributor throughout the policy term. No new business strain; better aligns commission outgo with profitability.

subsections.title: Impact on Experience and Risk
bullets: Different channels appeal to different customers based on financial sophistication and income. The sophistication of the client base influences the complexity of products that can be sold.

Claims and persistency experience will vary by channel, depending on: financial sophistication; income levels; level of underwriting applied; and who initiated the sale (customer-initiated sales tend to have better experience as the policyholder has a genuine perceived need).
Greatest competition — and hence pressure for low premiums — exists in business sold through insurance brokers and internet comparison websites.
Worksite marketing: benefits from the prospect of engaging a large number of similar risks (keeping risk premiums low) and possible expense savings; limited by small average policy sizes.

Group risks via brokers: the broker handles communication, data gathering, and transfers. The insurer benefits from assured quality of selection and minimal additional administrative expense, but has limited ability to: build a relationship with the employer; influence retention of the business; or influence the employer's risk management.

sections.title: Propensity to Buy vs Need to Sell

subsections.title: Bought vs Sold Products
bullets: Insurance that is legally compulsory is 'bought'. Insurance that is neither legally required nor obviously needed must be 'sold' — the intermediary must first make the customer aware of the need.

IP: should be a need for most, but is often not perceived as such. Many people are unaware of the inadequacy of State sickness benefits. Primarily a 'sold' product. Short-term IP may be legally binding on employers.

CI: not directly matched to a specific financial need; provides a lump sum at time of crisis. The 'lottery syndrome' (attraction of a large payout) means it is sometimes 'bought'. Provides peace of mind even if the policyholder can't specify what the money would be used for.

LTCI: need depends on perceived government attitude to care provision and individual priorities. Elements of legal compulsion exist in some countries. The State may incentivise purchase by providing low State provision or means-testing assets before providing care.

PMI: if all medical costs are individual responsibility, PMI is a need. If the State provides free healthcare, PMI must be sold — purchased for shorter waiting times, choice, and better accommodation.

Growing awareness that the State cannot maintain current welfare benefit levels (due to ageing populations, rising medical costs, and fiscal pressures) is increasing the underlying demand for health and care insurance.
Increasing sales regulation and disclosure requirements are reducing insurers' ability to 'create needs' through the sales process — products must genuinely meet customer needs to succeed.

subsections.title: Types of Regulatory Restriction
bullets: The primary aim of regulation is policyholder protection, ensuring public confidence in the insurance industry so that it can continue to attract business.

Regulatory restrictions may include: (1) restrictions on the types of contract an insurer can offer; (2) restrictions on premium rates or charges (eg maximum premium rates); (3) restrictions on rating factors (eg gender, genetic test results); (4) requirements on terms and conditions of contracts (eg claim definitions, maximum frequency and size of premium increases for reviewable products); (5) restrictions on sales channels and procedures (eg cooling-off periods, benefit illustrations, minimum salesperson training requirements); (6) restrictions on underwriting (eg prohibition on use of genetic test results or past claims history); (7) indirect capital constraint via minimum reserving and solvency capital requirements; (8) restrictions on types or amounts of assets used for solvency demonstration.
Regulation may discourage innovation or restrict benefits that could otherwise be provided to policyholders — a trade-off against policyholder protection.

The wider regulatory environment also matters — other institutions (eg hospital networks, care homes) may offer competing products under different regulatory constraints, creating an uneven playing field.

Genetic test results: insurers are generally prohibited from requiring genetic tests (an ethical infringement of personal liberty) or using results in underwriting, though they may continue to collect gender data.

key_terms.definition: A regulatory requirement giving policyholders the right to cancel a new insurance policy within an initial period (eg 30 days) without any financial loss.
The supervisory value of assets minus the supervisory value of liabilities (including SCR). Represents the capital available to write new business. Also called free capital or free reserves.

subsections.title: Taxation Approaches and Contract Design
bullets: Two main approaches to taxing insurance business: (1) tax on annual profits (increase in free assets over the year); (2) tax on investment income/gains less operating expenses (I–E basis).

The I–E basis is less suitable for health and care products than for life insurance, as most health and care products are primarily protection-based with small reserves and little investment income — little or no tax would be payable, which may not be acceptable for proprietary companies.
LTCI is the exception — it has a significant investment component and would generate more tax under an I–E basis.
Premium taxes exist in some territories — the insurer collects these on behalf of the State. Higher premium taxes reduce the attractiveness of insurance, increasing lapse rates.

Different types of business (short-term vs long-term, protection vs savings, conventional vs unit-linked) may be taxed differently within a country. Savings business may be exempt if policyholders receive gross investment returns, with benefits taxed instead.

Tax treatment of premiums (deductibility), investment return, and benefits all affect the overall attractiveness of a product. The State may use tax concessions to incentivise purchase of certain health products (eg tax relief on PMI or LTCI premiums).
Product design aims to make the best use of tax opportunities and must work within any tax-driven constraints (eg minimum insurance cover required to qualify for concessions).

key_terms.definition: A method of taxing insurance companies on investment income and capital gains less operating expenses, rather than on total profits. Common in some long-term insurance markets.

subsections.title: Professional Guidance
bullets: Actuarial associations issue professional guidance to give actuaries a framework for maintaining professional standards in advising health and care insurers.
Guidance typically covers: policy conditions; the adequacy of premium rates for new business; the value of the liabilities.
Guidance may also interpret government regulations where the government does not wish to be overly prescriptive — the profession effectively sets acceptable limits on behaviour.
Professional guidance provides protection to actuaries against pressure from management to agree to courses of action not in the best interests of policyholders.

subsections.title: Economic and Other Influences
bullets: Inflation: benefits must meet both current and future policyholder needs. Fixed level benefits risk becoming inadequate over time. Fixed escalation rates may be insufficient or excessive relative to actual inflation. Index-linked benefits may not match the specific inflation of health/care costs. Indemnity contracts transfer inflation risk to the insurer — especially risky for long-term guaranteed premium indemnity products.
Business cycle: demand for employer-sponsored insurances is higher in business upturns (used as a staff retention tool). In downturns, employers cut benefits or reduce coverage to save costs.

Employment security: high job security increases demand. Low job security increases some claim inceptions — eg employees claim under PMI before cover is lost; employers may encourage IP claims as a way to reduce the salary bill in downturns; work-related stress and depression increase IP claims.

Political stability: some political regimes are more favourable to health and care insurance than others (eg willingness to reduce State provision). Political stability generally indicates: economic wellbeing; stability of regulatory and tax rules; stability of the future economic value of insurance policies.

exam_qa.q: If premium regulation specifies minimum premium rates for CI insurance, what is the regulator's concern, and is this appropriate?
exam_qa.a: The regulator's concern is that excessively low premium rates may threaten the financial security of the insurer and hence the security of its policyholders. Minimum premium rates could prevent insurers from under-pricing and becoming insolvent. However, the regulator might also consider maximum premium rates to prevent overcharging, particularly in markets where competition is limited and consumers may not have access to easily comparable pricing information. Both upper and lower limits are theoretically possible — the appropriate choice depends on market structure and whether effective competition can be relied upon to control excessive pricing.

subsections.title: Introduction — The Governing Questions
bullets: Before designing a State healthcare system, a government must answer six fundamental questions: (1) Who gets what benefits, and when? (2) Who pays, when, and how much? (3) Who organises collection and payment to providers? (4) How are spending decisions made, and by whom? (5) How do patients access services? (6) What risks arise for patients and the State once the system is in place?

There is no single 'right' answer — each country's approach depends on: its political stance; the wealth, size and characteristics of its population; the quantity and quality of medical services available; the state of infrastructure and the economy; the overall level of development; the existence of other State benefits; history of State care; and the social and cultural stance of the country.

The four headline objectives of any government's healthcare provision are: (1) protecting the nation's health, (2) subsidising the poor, (3) balancing the budget, and (4) following social culture and/or political promises. Each is discussed in subsections 1.2–1.5 below.

subsections.title: Protecting the Nation's Health
bullets: A healthy population is a productive population: healthy workers boost GDP, which raises tax revenues, which fund the healthcare system — a self-reinforcing cycle. In this sense, a well-designed State healthcare policy should effectively pay for itself.

The spectrum of State involvement ranges from a relaxed, market-focused approach (minimal State involvement; private providers and insurance compete freely — as in the USA and Canada, where most people self-fund or buy private insurance) to a centrally provident approach (healthcare is entirely the State's responsibility — as in France, where public health insurance is compulsory for all legal residents).

Protecting health goes beyond providing doctors and hospitals. It includes: (a) Education — health lessons in school curricula, workplace health education (compulsory in areas such as catering), and public advertising campaigns via TV, radio, cinema and magazines. Advice areas include: disease prevention; early identification of diseases (e.g. sexually transmitted diseases); dangers of smoking and alcohol; hygiene; and accident prevention. (b) Research and technology — the State takes a long-term view on scientific advances and sponsors their introduction into the country, rather than leaving this entirely to commercial considerations.

Other State tools for enhancing national health: provision of screening facilities; well-man and well-woman clinics and regular check-ups; ensuring health insurance is available, affordable and appropriate; making overseas operations a viable option; investment in sports and exercise initiatives; dietary advice; health and safety rules for employers; drink-drive campaigns.

exam_qa.q: Can you think of ways of enhancing the nation's health beyond directly providing doctors and hospitals?
exam_qa.a: Possibilities include: provision of screening facilities; provision of well-man and well-woman clinics and regular check-ups; ensuring that health insurance is available, affordable and appropriate; making overseas operations a viable option; greater investment in sports and exercise initiatives; providing advice on diet; accident prevention measures such as health and safety rules for employers and drink-drive campaigns; campaigns warning of the dangers of smoking and alcohol.

subsections.title: Subsidising the Poor
bullets: Even in market-focused countries, the State typically maintains a role in ensuring the poorest can access primary medical assistance (doctors and medicines) and hospitalisation. Children and the elderly are usually priorities.

This provision is seen as part of the State's wider welfare package and an integral part of attempts to redistribute wealth — aiming to ensure all citizens can achieve a minimum standard of living when in ill health.
Even the USA maintains a minimum level of provision for those who would find self-funding an onerous burden. The political process is the mechanism through which redistributive aims are furthered.

key_terms.definition: A system where State benefits are only provided to individuals whose income or wealth falls below a defined threshold. The means test assesses what proportion of the total benefit the individual should be able to provide from their own income and savings. Benefits may be conditional on income, accumulated wealth, or both.

subsections.title: Balancing the Budget
bullets: Healthcare provision is part of State expenditure whether universal or restricted to those who cannot pay. Funding sources: specific health charges (e.g. National Insurance in the UK — earmarked for health and welfare; since the early 2000s NI has funded 15–20% of the NHS); general taxation; or a mixture of both.

Healthcare spending always involves redistribution from the healthy to the sick. Under NI-style systems, the rich pay more (contributions are proportional to earnings), though an upper earnings cap limits this cross-subsidy.

Healthcare costs rise faster than general consumer prices for three reasons: (1) improvements in medical technology continually expand what is possible and expected; (2) growing public expectations of what the State should deliver; (3) mortality improvements lead to increased morbidity and higher health and care costs for a larger elderly population. This creates persistent upward fiscal pressure on the healthcare budget.

Implication for tax-raising mechanisms: any taxation mechanism used to fund healthcare must be capable of rising faster than consumer price inflation. A tax on consumer goods (such as VAT) — which by definition rises only in line with consumer prices — will be insufficient to keep pace with healthcare costs. The government must therefore use income-based or earnings-related contributions (which grow with the economy) or earmark specific healthcare levies with the flexibility to increase rates above general inflation.

exam_qa.q: What is the implication of healthcare costs rising faster than the cost of consumer goods?
exam_qa.a: Any means of raising money for State healthcare costs will need to rise at a rate faster than that of consumer goods, otherwise the healthcare budget will become increasingly in deficit. For example, raising the money by relying on a tax on consumer goods and services sold (known as Value Added Tax (VAT) in some countries) will be insufficient, because VAT revenues only grow in line with consumer prices — not at the faster rate at which healthcare costs grow.

subsections.title: Following Social Culture and Political Promises
bullets: The State's healthcare role may change as different political parties gain power, but the foundations of healthcare provision tend to be embedded in national culture and expectation — making radical change politically very difficult, especially in the short term. The loss in political capital from pushing through controversial changes is typically too great.

Radical restructuring of State healthcare is rare and usually occurs only in the wake of large social or economic upheaval. In the UK, the NHS was built on the social and political changes following the Second World War; despite many changes of government since, the principle of free healthcare at the point of need has remained intact.
The role of commercial insurance depends entirely on the nature of State provision. The four main models of co-existence are summarised in Section 2.

sections.title: Models of Co-existence: State and Private Insurance

subsections.title: Four Models of Co-existence
bullets: Insurance as an optional alternative: the State promises a comprehensive service but private insurance provides a superior alternative — higher quality, choice of doctor, choice of place and time of treatment, and significantly shorter waiting times. Example: UK — the NHS provides care free at point of need, but PMI allows patients to bypass NHS waiting lists (e.g. for hip replacements), choose their hospital, and access private rooms. Insurance demand rises when NHS waiting lists lengthen.

Insurance as an optional complement: the State provides a limited range of services and individuals fund the balance themselves. Example: USA and Canada — most people need private insurance or must self-fund for services beyond the State minimum.
Insurance as a compulsory alternative: the State provides everything up to a defined salary or wealth threshold; healthcare insurance is obligatory for those above this threshold.

Insurance as a compulsory complement: the State provides certain procedures free; insurance is mandatory for all other procedures. Example: Germany — virtually all people in gainful employment must be insured under a statutory health insurance scheme funded by NI contributions; those with large incomes can opt for a private alternative.

Co-existence applies not only to direct medical services (PMI-type cover) but also to income provision when a person is unable to work due to invalidity (IP-type cover) and to long-term care provision (LTCI-type cover).

Country example — Republic of Ireland: State healthcare is free for the very poor; others contribute towards treatment. PMI is popular — driven historically by tax benefits on premiums and some dissatisfaction with State provision. The main PMI provider is government controlled. Tax relief is available on PMI and long-term care insurance premiums, granted at source by insurers.

Country example — Australia: the Commonwealth collects taxes but individual states deliver healthcare services with Commonwealth subsidies. A national system provides health benefits to all, funded through general taxation plus a compulsory health tax levy on income (dependent on income levels). A large private sector exists — participation is voluntary but government-encouraged (roughly half the eligible population holds private insurance). Benefits are scheduled (some unlimited, some capped). Waiting lists can be lengthy. Medical practitioners can bill either the government or the patient; fee schedules are not fixed. Payment to providers is complex: some paid retrospectively, some prospectively; some regulated, some not; some paid daily.

subsections.title: Means Testing vs Universal Provision
bullets: The State may be the sole provider from inception to recovery (universal) or may only pay in cases of financial hardship (means-tested). The means test assesses the proportion of total benefit the individual can self-fund, based on income, accumulated wealth, or both.

Trade-offs — means-tested vs universal: Advantages of means testing: more cost-effective and better targeted; redistributive if funded by taxes on the wealthy; may encourage return to work for those who fail the test. Disadvantages of means testing: creates a poverty trap (rising income reduces benefit leaving the individual no better off); encourages people to squander or hide wealth to qualify; discourages self-provision; stigmatises recipients; leads to under-take-up because the claiming process is complex or perceived as degrading. Universal benefits avoid these behavioural distortions but are more expensive.

subsections.title: Direct Provision vs Reimbursement of Commercial Providers
bullets: The State may run its own medical establishments (better cost and claim control; avoids paying commercial profit margins) or reimburse commercial providers after treatment (partially or fully).

Advantages of using commercial providers: benefit from their expertise, experience and economies of scale; administration can be transferred to providers; commercial businesses are often more responsive to customer needs; and if multiple providers exist, competition for the State's business should reduce costs and improve patient experience.

subsections.title: Cash Benefits — Lump Sum vs Regular Income
bullets: Lump sum payments are appropriate when the onset of disability requires capital expenditure — e.g. adapting a home for restricted mobility or buying a specially adapted car. Often paid on an indemnity basis (the State pays the actual costs of the specific help required).

Regular income payments are appropriate when the disabled person is unable to work and needs ongoing financial support. As with commercial insurance, this may be subject to periodic reassessment to confirm continuing disability.
The level of benefit may also differ by lifestage or circumstances of the recipient (see Section 3.4).

subsections.title: Flat-Rate vs Earnings-Related Benefits, and Benefit Differentiation
bullets: Flat-rate benefits pay the same amount to all claimants regardless of prior earnings. Advantages: simple to understand and administer; lower admin costs; higher take-up (simpler application process); provides an incentive to return to work; encourages self-provision via insurance. Disadvantages: benefit level may be unrelated to pre-disability living standards; may be insufficient for those with dependants; may actually exceed the income of lower-paid workers — a disincentive to return to work and a source of higher State costs.

Earnings-related benefits pay a proportion of the individual's prior earnings. Advantages: no abrupt change in lifestyle on becoming sick; better reflects financial responsibilities of higher earners; less likely to exceed normal earnings (preserving the return-to-work incentive). Disadvantages: more complex and expensive to administer; takes time to gather individual earnings data causing payment delays; lower earners may receive less than under a flat-rate system.

Income benefits are typically index-linked to consumer prices. Many countries use a combination — e.g. earnings-related benefit for an initial period of disability, transitioning to a flat-rate benefit thereafter.

Benefit differentiation by recipient circumstances — Employed: contributing through NI/taxation so entitled to full benefits; cost-sharing (e.g. prescription charges) may apply, waived for the poorly paid. Unemployed: State aims to encourage return-to-work ethos; benefits may be limited in amount or term unless unemployment is due to the general economy or the individual is performing a broader social good (e.g. informal carer). Students: State should pay all reasonable costs (temporary hardship); welfare benefits may be limited if the student receives government loans or has parental support. Pensioners: no option of work so benefits should not be restricted; regular income preferred over lump sum (lower life expectancy); benefits related to pension rather than salary; pensioners expect generosity having contributed throughout their working lives, even under PAYG. Widow(er): similar to pensioner but generally even lower life expectancy and potentially greater need given loss of a home-care partner. War veterans: disability suffered in service of country — benefits should be generous; high long-term disability needs are common.

subsections.title: Pay-As-You-Go (PAYG)
bullets: Under PAYG, the current working population funds the benefits of those currently in need. There is no pre-funding — contributions flow directly to beneficiaries. If contributions are insufficient in a given year, the shortfall is met from general tax revenues.

The four-step PAYG process: (1) establish the degree of State subsidy (what the State pays vs what individuals contribute, determining individual contribution rates); (2) estimate coming year's expected outgo by projecting benefits and expenses from past experience; (3) estimate coming year's tax revenue; (4) adjust the tax structure so that general taxation revenue plus individual contributions equals expected welfare outgo.

Key risk of PAYG — demographic imbalance: in most developed countries the population is ageing. The ratio of pensioners and elderly dependants to the working-age population is rising, making contributions progressively more insufficient to cover welfare costs. Shortfalls must be funded by raising tax rates, cutting benefits, or government borrowing — all of which are politically and economically difficult.

Inter-generational trust: PAYG depends on each generation trusting the next to honour the same obligation. The current generation pays for their parents and grandparents in the expectation that their own children will do the same for them. If a future government cuts benefits rather than raising contributions or borrowing, that trust is broken. The smoothing provided by general tax revenues does not change the fundamental reality: under PAYG, the current generation pays all the costs (except insofar as the government runs a deficit and borrows to fund the shortfall).

key_terms.definition: A State healthcare funding method where current workers' contributions directly fund the benefits of those currently in need, with no pre-accumulation of assets. Vulnerable to demographic changes that increase the ratio of beneficiaries to contributors.

exam_qa.q: What could be a danger with a PAYG method of funding State healthcare?
exam_qa.a: If, as in most developed countries, the population is changing such that the number of pensioners (and elderly dependants) is increasing relative to the working population, then the contributions and tax income from earners will become more and more insufficient to pay for welfare costs. The system therefore faces a structural funding gap that must be met by raising tax rates, cutting benefits, or borrowing — all of which create political and economic difficulties.

exam_qa.q: Explain how the pay-as-you-go (PAYG) system of funding State healthcare and welfare operates and depends on the trust between successive generations in the community. [4]
exam_qa.a:
The current working generation pays for the cost of healthcare and welfare benefits for their children, their parents and grandparents, and themselves. The major costs arise from benefits for children (welfare benefits such as child allowances and primary healthcare) and senior citizens (old-age pensions and all forms of healthcare).

Payment is made through earmarked taxation and general taxation revenues when there is a shortfall from earmarked revenues. Contributions from each individual of working age rise when the current working generation is small relative to the generations it is supporting.

The smoothing provided by general tax revenues does not alter the fact that the current generation pays all the costs — except insofar as the government runs a budget deficit and funds the shortfall by borrowing.

Each generation effectively promises to pay the costs incurred by their grandparents, parents and children. The current generation trusts that the succeeding generation will not abuse the system — e.g. by cutting benefits rather than increasing contributions or borrowing more. If this trust is well founded, future support for the current generation will match the support that they provided for other generations. [Maximum 4]

subsections.title: Forward Funding
bullets: Forward funding takes a longer-term view: rather than balancing the welfare budget year-by-year, the State models expected future outgo over a chosen period (e.g. 5–20 years), estimates population and workforce trends, forecasts tax revenues, and calculates an earmarked healthcare fund such that accumulated contributions will meet the intended State healthcare provision over that period.

The six-step forward funding process: (1) choose a future planning period (critics note governments may only plan to the next election); (2) analyse the level of State provision at that future point; (3) model State outgo at that and intervening years (expected cash outgo); (4) estimate population and workforce trends; (5) forecast taxation revenues (expected cash income); (6) calculate a specific healthcare fund — earmarked taxation builds up the difference in the meantime.

Advantages over PAYG: (a) less sensitive to demographic changes — the fund absorbs short-term imbalances; (b) fosters long-term planning, allowing the government to take corrective action (e.g. adjust contribution rates) in good time; (c) increases public confidence that State healthcare will be available when needed, improving willingness to contribute and government popularity. Disadvantages: (a) money tied up in the fund could be deployed immediately for other purposes; (b) more complex to administer; (c) assets must be held and actively managed — an investment policy is required and assets must be bought and sold over time.

In practice, most countries use a combination of PAYG and forward funding, especially where demographic forecasts indicate a declining ratio of workers to beneficiaries (as in the UK and most developed countries).

key_terms.definition: A State healthcare funding approach that models future outgo and builds up an earmarked fund in advance to meet those obligations, rather than relying solely on current-year contributions. More robust against demographic change but requires long-term political commitment and active asset management.

sections.title: Incentives for Self-Provision and the Role of Insurance

subsections.title: Incentives for Self-Provision
bullets: To promote insurance take-up and/or limit State outgo, governments often offer incentives for self-provision. The four main methods: (1) Tax relief on premiums for appropriate insurance — e.g. Republic of Ireland offers tax relief on PMI, dental insurance and LTCI premiums, granted at source by insurers. (2) Exclusion of some or all the population from certain State benefits — e.g. in England and Wales most people pay for eye tests and glasses (only certain groups are exempt), whereas in Scotland eye tests are free for everyone. (3) Reduced general taxes where appropriate insurance is held. (4) Direct subsidies to providers of healthcare services to reduce the cost of private healthcare premiums.
Methods (3) and (4) (reduced general taxes and direct subsidies to providers) are rarely used in practice. Most countries rely primarily on methods (1) and (2).

Key principle: the more the State reduces its own provision — or subjects care to means-testing of assets — the stronger the commercial incentive for individuals to purchase private health and care insurance (PMI, IP, LTCI).

subsections.title: The Role of Insurance
bullets: The role of insurance within a national healthcare market is determined by four interlocking factors: (1) historically evolved cultures and expectations; (2) budget constraints on the State; (3) differing perceived priorities for different population segments; (4) the approach taken to funding and self-provision.

To understand the role of insurance in a given market and develop an appropriate commercial strategy, actuaries must study all four elements — they are deeply interconnected and vary substantially between countries.

subsections.title: Q10.1 — Advantages and Disadvantages: Market-Focused, Means-Tested, Forward Funding

exam_qa.q: List the advantages and disadvantages of: (a) a market-focused approach, (b) means-tested State health benefits, (c) forward funding.
exam_qa.a:
MARKET-FOCUSED APPROACH
Advantages (+):
+ Cheaper for the State — saves care costs and reduces administrative burden.
+ Encourages competition in both healthcare insurance and healthcare provision.
+ Encourages the population to take better care of itself (people know they will pay if they become ill).
+ No cross-subsidies between rich and poor, or healthy and unhealthy — popular with those who consider themselves healthy.
+ Can result in lower taxes, or savings can be redirected elsewhere.
Disadvantages (-):
- Lower security of benefits and greater uncertainty for the public.
- Unpopular with unhealthy and/or poor people and generally against the public spirit.

MEANS-TESTED STATE HEALTHCARE BENEFITS
Advantages (+):
+ Cost-effective way to target benefits at those most in need — the State provides little or nothing to those with sufficient private means.
+ Redistributive if taxes are raised from the wealthy and benefits flow to those with low income or wealth.
+ Those who fail the means test may be encouraged to return to work.
Disadvantages (-):
- May discourage self-provision — people may see no point in saving if doing so disqualifies them from benefits.
- Creates a poverty trap: increases in income or assets merely reduce the value of State benefits, leaving the individual no better off.
- May encourage people to squander or hide wealth to qualify for benefits.
- Recipients may be discouraged from returning to work.
- Perceived as unfair to those who do provide for themselves.
- Benefits are not taken up by all who are entitled to or need them.
- Receipt of benefit may identify an individual as 'poor', deterring them from claiming.
- Perceived by some as 'degrading' — State benefits should be a universal right, not a payment to the poor.

FORWARD FUNDING
Advantages (+):
+ Less sensitive to demographic changes compared with PAYG — the fund absorbs short-term imbalances.
+ Fosters forward planning, allowing the government to take corrective action (e.g. adjust contribution rates) in good time.
+ Increases public confidence that State healthcare will be available when needed, improving willingness to contribute and government popularity.
Disadvantages (-):
- Money tied up in the fund could be used immediately, with immediate effect, elsewhere.
- More complex to administer.
- Assets must be held and managed — an investment policy is required, and investment decisions and asset transactions must be made over time.

subsections.title: Q10.2 — State Benefits by Recipient Category

exam_qa.q: Explain how the circumstances of each of the following categories of person might be expected to affect the welfare benefits they are offered by the State: employed; unemployed; student; pensioner; widow(er); war veteran.
exam_qa.a:
EMPLOYED: The employed contribute through taxation and NI, so should receive full benefits. Since poverty is not generally a problem, some cost-sharing may apply (e.g. prescription charges) — waivable for certain conditions or the poorly paid.

UNEMPLOYED: The State aims to pay medical costs but encourages a return-to-work ethos, since the unemployed are not funding benefits through salary taxation. Benefits may therefore be limited in amount or term. However, if unemployment is due to the general state of the economy, or the individual is performing a broader social good (e.g. informal carer, voluntary aid worker), benefits may be more generous.

STUDENT: The State should pay all reasonable costs — the student will eventually be working and should not be penalised for temporary hardship. Welfare benefits may be limited if the student already receives government loans or other financial support. The extent of parental financial support may also affect entitlement.

PENSIONER: There is no option of work, and society generally takes care of the elderly. Benefits should not be restricted, but regular income may be preferred over lump sum given lower life expectancy. Benefits will not be salary-related but could be related to pension. Pensioners typically feel entitled to generous benefits having contributed throughout their working lives — even under PAYG, where the healthy working population in effect pays for the sick elderly.

WIDOW(ER): Similar to pensioners, but generally with even lower life expectancy and potentially greater need — reflecting the loss of home care from a partner and greater reliance on paid care services.

WAR VETERAN: War veterans may be unemployed and suffering from long-term disability, so medical needs can be high. Given that the disability was suffered while serving the country, benefits should be generous. Other comments applicable to pensioners also apply.

subsections.title: Q10.3 — PAYG Funding and Inter-Generational Trust [4 marks]

exam_qa.q: Explain how the pay-as-you-go (PAYG) system of funding State healthcare and welfare operates and depends on the trust between successive generations in the community. [4]
exam_qa.a:
The current generation of working age pays for the cost of healthcare and welfare benefits for: their children; their parents and grandparents; and themselves. The major costs arise from benefits for children (welfare benefits such as child allowances paid to parents, and primary healthcare) and senior citizens (old-age pensions and all forms of healthcare).

Payment is made through earmarked taxation and general taxation revenues (e.g. income tax, sales tax) when there is a shortfall from earmarked revenues. Contributions from each individual of working age will rise when the current generation is small compared to the preceding and the next generation.

The smoothing provided by general tax revenues does not alter the fact that the current generation pays all the costs — except insofar as the government runs a budget deficit and effectively funds the shortfall by borrowing.

Each generation effectively promises to pay the costs incurred by their grandparents, parents and children. The current generation trusts that the succeeding generation will not abuse the system — e.g. by cutting benefits rather than increasing contributions or borrowing more. If this trust is well founded, then future support for the current generation will match the support that they provided for other generations. [Maximum 4]

subsections.title: Q10.4 — Government Objectives in Healthcare and Welfare Provision [6 marks]

exam_qa.q: The government of a developed country is considering the ways in which it can provide health and care benefits (direct medical services) and welfare benefits (financial benefits when unable to work due to illness). Outline the objectives that the government may have, the rationale behind each one, and any limitations on its ability to meet these objectives. [6]
exam_qa.a:
OBJECTIVE 1 — To protect the health of the nation
Rationale: a healthy population will be happier and thus re-elect the party in power. A healthy workforce will also be more productive and hence increase the GDP of the country. Encouraging people to keep healthy (e.g. through education) can help reduce the costs of healthcare and thus also help to balance the budget.

OBJECTIVE 2 — To subsidise the poor
Rationale: the aim is to ensure that even the poorest have access to a basic level of healthcare — access to doctors, medicine and hospitalisation where necessary — and thus ensure that all of the population remain healthy. It may be a key aspect of the government's attempts to redistribute wealth.

OBJECTIVE 3 — To balance the budget
Rationale: the cost of State-provided healthcare must be met by contributions. These may be specific health charges (e.g. National Insurance in the UK), general taxation, or a combination of the two.

OBJECTIVE 4 — To follow social culture and/or political promises
Rationale: the foundations of healthcare provision (such as providing access to free healthcare for all) tend to be part of a developed national culture and it would be unpopular to change things radically, especially in the short term. However, the political ethics of the party in power will have some influence.

LIMITATIONS on ability to meet these objectives:
- The wealth of the country.
- The priority given to the provision of healthcare.
- The style and culture of politics within the country. [Maximum 6]

subsections.title: Q10.5 — Flat-Rate vs Earnings-Related State Income Benefit Design [6 marks]

exam_qa.q: A government is planning to provide State-funded income benefits for those unable to earn a living as a result of sickness or injury. State the advantages and disadvantages of: (a) a flat benefit equal to 40% of national average earnings, increased annually as latest data become available; (b) a benefit of 70% of the individual's average weekly earnings in the last 13 weeks of employment for the first 26 weeks of sickness, followed by 40% of national average earnings with annual increases. [6]
exam_qa.a:
(a) FLAT BENEFIT AT 40% OF NATIONAL AVERAGE EARNINGS
Advantages (+):
+ Simple to administer — all claimants receive the same benefit.
+ The benefit is likely to be sufficient only to cover basic living costs, so (unless pre-sickness income was lower than the benefit) it acts as an incentive to return to work as soon as possible.
+ Overall claim costs are likely to be lower under this method.
+ The benefit is linked to average earnings, so the claimant benefits from the economic growth of the country — a better uprating basis than price inflation alone.
Disadvantages (-):
- May be sufficient for single people but unfairly penalises those with dependants, who suffer through no fault of their own.
- May give lower-paid workers more income than they earned when able to work — a significant disincentive to return to work, and would increase State costs.
- For those with well-paid jobs (and no employer sick pay), the benefit does not allow maintenance of their lifestyle in the short term — causing an abrupt decline in family finances.

(b) FIRST 26 WEEKS AT 70% OF INDIVIDUAL'S AVERAGE WEEKLY EARNINGS, THEN FLAT RATE AT 40% OF NATIONAL AVERAGE EARNINGS
Advantages (+):
+ The earnings-related initial benefit means there is no abrupt change in lifestyle as a result of sickness.
+ Less likely that benefits in the first 26 weeks will exceed normal earnings, so a return-to-work incentive remains.
+ For those sick for at least 26 weeks, the benefit transitions to scheme (a), and the same advantages then apply.
Disadvantages (-):
- More complicated and expensive to administer — the initial benefit differs for each individual.
- May take time to gather earnings data, causing delays to payments.
- The high initial benefit level (70%) provides only a limited incentive to return to work in the first 26 weeks.
- Those earning significantly below average earnings will receive less under this scheme than under scheme (a) and may have insufficient income, unless eligible for other welfare benefits.
- The 13-week reference period may not capture the impact of annual bonuses and similar payments on pre-sickness income.
- Overall claim costs are likely to be higher. [Maximum 6]

subsections.title: Q10.6 — Country A (Free at Point of Use) vs Country B (Tariff Co-payment System) [15 marks]

exam_qa.q: Country A: all health and care is free at the point of use. Country B: the State reimburses a fixed percentage of a published tariff for each treatment (Major Surgery 95%, Minor Surgery 80%, Pregnancy/Childbirth 95–100%, Prescribed Medicines 35–65%, X-Rays 70%, GP/Specialist Visits 70%); providers may charge above the tariff price. (i) Describe the advantages and disadvantages to the State of each system. [10] (ii) Describe how each system might influence the market for private medical insurance in that country. [5] [Total 15]
exam_qa.a:
(i) ADVANTAGES AND DISADVANTAGES TO THE STATE

COUNTRY A — Free at Point of Use
Advantages (+):
+ Ensures all who need treatment receive it according to clinical need, not ability to pay.
+ All resources can be devoted to providing treatment rather than collecting fees from patients.
+ PMI is not essential, so insurance regulation (though important to protect consumers) is not a major task.
+ Should be popular with consumers if the system works well.
Disadvantages (-):
- May encourage inappropriate use of health and care because it is free to the user — e.g. seeking medical advice for personal or social difficulties rather than genuine medical problems.
- May cause consumers to under-value healthcare because they have no idea of the costs involved — e.g. missing appointments, not taking prescribed medicines.
- Cost controls are only possible through third-party providers — no market mechanism operates on the demand side.
- May generate excess demand, resulting in waiting lists and the need to ration expensive treatments.
- No profit motive, so little incentive for staff to control costs or provide good customer service.
- No financial consequence for ill health, so individuals have no incentive to look after themselves.
- Does nothing to encourage commerce or competition in healthcare provision.

COUNTRY B — Tariff Co-payment System
Advantages (+):
+ Consumers appreciate the costs of healthcare, encouraging them to look after themselves and only seek treatment when genuinely necessary — exercises demand-side control.
+ Those who pay are less likely to abuse the system (e.g. more likely to keep or cancel appointments appropriately).
+ Providers may charge above-tariff, making consumers more aware of quality and expertise — this appreciation helps the State explain healthcare trade-offs.
+ The published tariff is a mechanism for the State to exercise cost control over providers.
+ By varying the reimbursement percentages, the government can vary the share of costs borne by patients — e.g. consumers bear a larger share of primary care costs (GP visits and medicines at 35–70%) than of acute care (major surgery at 95%).
+ Insurers have an interest in controlling all care costs (basic PMI pays a proportion of every treatment), providing a further market force to control prices.
+ Competition encourages innovation in treatments and delivery.
Disadvantages (-):
- A PMI market becomes necessary — basic policies (to cover the patient's co-payment at tariff rates) and enhanced policies (to cover the additional cost of above-tariff providers) must exist.
- All consumers need insurance, so comprehensive regulation of insurers is essential to protect consumers.
- If basic PMI premiums are too high for the poorest, an 'underclass' may arise who cannot afford adequate treatment — politically unpopular across the electorate, not just among the poorest. [Maximum 10]

(ii) INFLUENCE ON THE MARKET FOR PRIVATE MEDICAL INSURANCE

COUNTRY A:
- PMI is optional, so the market is confined to those with higher incomes (and generally better health).
- Because cover is optional, premium levels are unlikely to be regulated — insurers can risk-rate freely.
- Insurers must design products offering benefits superior to the free State service — e.g. treatment when and where the consumer wants it, shorter waiting times, and superior non-medical care.
- The market is sensitive to economic prosperity (buoyant in good times, sluggish in bad) and to the quality of the State service (long waiting lists increase demand for PMI).

COUNTRY B:
- Some PMI cover is essential, creating a large market for basic policies — this pools risks and spreads overheads, but means policy servicing costs are a large proportion of the premium.
- The large compulsory-participation market creates more competition between insurers, potentially leading to a more efficient PMI market.
- Because basic cover is bought by nearly everyone, some price control (including possible community rating) is likely — making it harder for insurers to match premiums to individual risk.
- There is a secondary market for policies meeting the cost of above-tariff care — confined to higher-income consumers.
- Insurers designing enhanced products may find it easier than in Country A, because all consumers already have experience of using PMI and understand its value. [Maximum 5]

subsections.title: Q10.7 — Consultation Fee Proposal: Advantages, Disadvantages and Insurance Opportunities [12 marks]

exam_qa.q: A State has provided free medical care to all its citizens; all patients must register with a State-approved doctor who manages their care. Demand has risen dramatically. The government proposes a consultation fee of approximately 5% of average weekly wages, rising with price inflation, paid directly to the doctor at the time of consultation. Patients on State welfare benefits can claim a means-tested refund with their next welfare payment. (i) Outline the advantages and disadvantages of this proposal. [7] (ii) Assuming the proposal is implemented, describe the opportunities for a major health and care insurer to market new products. [5] [Total 12]
exam_qa.a:
(i) ADVANTAGES AND DISADVANTAGES

For doctors and the State (+):
+ Likely to reduce demands on doctors' time — for example, by encouraging self-diagnosis and self-medication of minor illnesses.
+ The means-tested refund makes the system progressive — those with higher incomes pay more in net terms.
