+ Raises the profile of healthcare and may encourage individuals to take better care of themselves.
+ The additional income reduces the financial burden on the State healthcare system.

For doctors and the State (-):
- No provision for unemployed persons not receiving welfare benefits, such as students and children.
- Because the fee is paid at the time of consultation, it may encourage patients who have made appointments not to keep them.
- May increase long-run costs of care: patients may delay seeking advice, allowing minor conditions to become more serious and expensive to treat (e.g. ignoring angina symptoms leading to more expensive cardiac treatment later).
- Politically difficult — a significant change in a system where consultations were previously free.
- May encourage doctors to recommend (free) out-patient care for the chronically ill when doctor-managed care might be cheaper and more effective — merely transferring costs within the system.
- Requires a potentially large and expensive administrative system — though mitigated because only a small proportion of the population will be entitled to refunds and electronic systems can minimise costs.

For patients (+):
+ Should improve access to doctors — patients should not have to wait as long as demand falls.
+ Additional income to the State system might be used to improve quality, reduce taxes or contributions, or be redirected to other public services such as education.

For patients (-):
- May cause cashflow problems for the less well-off, who may struggle to make cash payments from limited income.
- May reduce frequency or quality of care for the chronically ill unless specific provisions are made (e.g. those needing regular blood pressure checks may attend less often). [Maximum 7]

(ii) OPPORTUNITIES FOR INSURERS TO MARKET NEW PRODUCTS
- The primary market is middle-income consumers who face regular cash outflows under the new system. The less well-off have little need for insurance (many entitled to means-tested refunds); the very well-off can meet the fee from regular income.
- The insurer is already a major provider of health and care insurance. Even though existing wealthier policyholders can likely afford the fee, the insurer could add consultation fee cover to existing policies as a straightforward product enhancement.
- For new customers without existing PMI, the insurer could use its brand and reputation to market a cash plan providing a partial or full refund of the consultation fee — tapping into a new, broader market.
- To manage high administration expenses associated with small, frequent claims: consider applying an excess to the benefit; or require that all consultation fee payments are accumulated and a single claim made at year-end.
- Products covering more than just consultation fees — e.g. adding physiotherapy or dental care — increase the premium but increase appeal and help spread fixed costs.
- To reduce selling costs: market group schemes through employers or affinity groups. Employers may subsidise premiums if the product reduces short-term sickness absence among employees.
- Direct selling saves commissions but increases initial selling costs — carefully targeted off-the-page selling may be effective if the insurer has a strong brand.
- Direct debit premium collection lowers collection costs and encourages renewal.
- Key pricing risk: claim incidence rates under an entirely new system are unknown and difficult to estimate. However, claim size is fixed by the statutory formula — reducing one dimension of pricing uncertainty. [Maximum 5]

sections.title: Uses and Basic Features of Models

subsections.title: Uses of Models
bullets: The main uses of models are: costing and reserving for options; model office (new business projections, embedded values, solvency, takeovers); reserves (statutory and management accounting); and pricing (profit, premium rates).
Models involve projecting all cashflows that may arise, including cashflows from any requirement to hold reserves and an adequate margin of solvency.
Models may need to project cashflows separately from different states (eg healthy vs claiming under IP).
Cashflows need to allow for any interactions, particularly between assets and liabilities — the model should be dynamic.
Stochastic models may be used where appropriate, particularly for health and care insurance where future incidence experience is far less easy to predict than for pure life insurance.

Four model types: (1) single policy profit test — projects one policy's cashflows; (2) new business model — projects all future new business cashflows; (3) existing business model — projects in-force business cashflows; (4) full model office — sum of (2) and (3).

key_terms.definition: A data record fed into the modelling program, representing either a single policy or a group of policies, containing key characteristics such as age, benefit, term.
The value of the future profit stream from existing business together with the value of any net assets attributable to shareholders.
Embedded value plus the value of future new business (goodwill). Used when valuing an insurer for sale.

exam_qa.q: List the uses of actuarial models in health and care insurance.
exam_qa.a: Pricing; product design; setting reserves (statutory and management); costing/reserving options and guarantees; assessing profitability; analysis of surplus; embedded value; capital requirements; return on capital; reinsurance needs; investment strategy; ongoing financial projections; expense budgeting; valuing business for merger/acquisition.

subsections.title: Basic Features and Operational Issues
bullets: A model must be adequately documented; workings easy to appreciate and communicate; outputs verifiable and communicable.
The model must not be overly complex; it should be capable of development and refinement.

In the health and care context: the model must allow for all cashflows including those from reserves/solvency; allow for different states (eg healthy vs claiming) and transitions between them; allow for interactions between assets and liabilities; and be capable of stochastic simulation where appropriate.
Dynamic links between financial and demographic variables are vital in stochastic models — eg sickness claims experience is correlated with economic conditions.

subsections.title: Deterministic Models
bullets: Each parameter has a fixed value; the model produces a point estimate; sensitivity testing is done by varying parameter values.
A deterministic model is a 'one question, one answer' model — useful but limited unless supplemented by sensitivity/scenario testing.

key_terms.definition: A model where all parameters are fixed, producing a single point estimate. Sensitivity testing requires re-running the model with different parameter values.
A form of sensitivity analysis where multiple parameters are changed simultaneously in a consistent way, reflecting how they might move together in reality.

subsections.title: Stochastic Models
bullets: Some parameters are allowed to vary with their own probability distributions; the model must be run many times using random samples; outputs are in the form of a probability distribution.

Stochastic modelling is more important for health and care than for pure life insurance, because incidence experience is harder to predict and benefit amounts can vary with inflation, medical protocols, or other factors.
Stochastic models can be invaluable when assessing guarantees; when variables have reasonably stable probability distributions; and for identifying high-risk scenarios.
Disadvantages of stochastic models: time and computing constraints; sensitivity of results to the (deterministically chosen) parameter distributions — 'rubbish in equals rubbish out'.
Stochastic calibration approaches: risk-neutral (market-consistent) — used for valuation, replicates market prices; real-world — used for projections, reflects realistic long-term expectations.

key_terms.definition: A model where some parameters are allowed to vary with probability distributions, run many times to produce a probability distribution of outcomes.
Setting stochastic model parameters so that simulated average present values replicate observed market prices. Used for valuation, especially where guarantees exist.
Setting stochastic model parameters to reflect realistic long-term expectations rather than market prices. Used for projecting future solvency and determining capital requirements.

exam_qa.q: When would a stochastic model be preferred over a deterministic model?
exam_qa.a: When assessing the impact of guarantees or options; when the variable has a reasonably stable probability distribution (eg investment returns in a stable economy); for assessing year-on-year volatility; and when identifying high-risk scenarios. However, a deterministic model may be preferred when only one answer is needed (eg estimating the loss from a specific epidemic) or when data are insufficient to define a credible probability distribution.

subsections.title: Sensitivity Testing
bullets: Sensitivity analysis varies each parameter value to assess the financial impact of its mis-estimation. Correlations between parameters should be allowed for.

When pricing: sensitivity results help determine the margins needed in parameter assumptions. If a product is overly sensitive to any factor, the product may need to be redesigned or reinsurance reconsidered.
When assessing return on capital / profitability: sensitivity results enable the actuary to quantify the effect of departures from chosen assumptions when reporting to management.

Key parameters to sensitivity test: claim inception, recovery and transition rates; mortality; lapse/non-renewal rates; average claim size (PMI); future expense levels and inflation; benefit/claims inflation; investment yield; volumes and mix of new business; taxation basis.
A simple qualitative check: increasing mortality should generally increase discounted future profits on most health and care products (unless a death benefit is provided).

key_terms.definition: The process of varying individual parameter values in a model to assess the impact on the output, showing how sensitive the result is to each assumption.

subsections.title: Formula Approach to Pricing
bullets: The formula approach equates the present value of income (premiums + investment income) against the present value of outgo (claims, expenses, commission, tax), with the unknown being the premium.
A profit objective may be included by subtracting the target profit from the denominator of the pricing formula.

Drawbacks: does not allow for the proper timing of events; does not accumulate reserves; ignores capital needs; cannot track premium-related or claim-related cashflows separately; cannot easily vary assumptions over time; cannot handle complex product structures.
The formula approach is used mainly for short-term (eg one-year) contracts, where its simplicity is an advantage.

key_terms.definition: The principle that expected present value of income (premiums + investment income) must equal expected present value of outgo (claims, expenses, tax, profit). The premium is the unknown derived from this equation.

subsections.title: Cashflow Approach to Pricing
bullets: Model points are chosen to represent expected new business; cashflows are projected for each model point, discounted at the risk discount rate; premiums or charges are set to satisfy the profit criterion.

Typical cashflow elements for conventional business: premiums; expenses (initial, renewal, claim); commission; claims; contribution to reserves; contribution to capital requirements; interest on cashflows and reserves; tax.
For unit-linked business, additional cashflows include: cost of unit allocations; bid-offer spread; risk and unit charges; fund management charges; unit fund growth and payouts.
The cashflow approach is now the most widely used for long-term insurance premium calculation; it is not widespread for PMI (short-term, one-year contracts).
Policies should be self-financing — future negative cashflows should be covered by adequate reserves.
A separate risk discount rate should in theory be applied to each component of cashflow, though in practice a single rate is used for simplicity.
The cashflow approach can also be used to: assess profitability of existing business (embedded value); determine expected return on capital; assess capital requirements for new business.

key_terms.definition: The rate used to discount net projected cashflows in a profit test, reflecting the return required by shareholders and the statistical risk of the cashflows. Discussed in detail in Chapter 16.
The present value of the future profits from a model point (or portfolio), used to assess whether a product meets the profit criterion.

exam_qa.q: Why are cashflow techniques not generally used for pricing one-year PMI contracts?
exam_qa.a: For short-term products, the premium is essentially the present value of expected benefits and expenses for the policy year. Investment income is relatively insignificant. A formula approach gives the same result with much less complexity. Cashflow techniques may still be used for PMI for other purposes, such as assessing long-term profitability or spreading initial acquisition costs.

subsections.title: Multi-State Models for Pricing and Reserving
bullets: Multi-state modelling is used where policyholders can exist in different states, each associated with different cashflows — primarily applicable to IP and LTCI.

States for a basic IP contract: Healthy, Sick-but-not-claiming (within deferred period), Sick-and-claiming, Dead, Lapsed. In practice the 'sick but not claiming' state is often merged into the Healthy state with explicit adjustments for the deferred period.
Transition intensities m(x, i→j) represent the annual rate of moving from state i to state j at exact age x. These are used to project probabilities of being in each state at each future time.

Pricing using multi-state models requires determining the proportion of lives in each state, valued claim outgo by multiplying lives in benefit-receiving states by average sum insured, then balancing with premiums and investment income.

Practical limitation: many sub-cohorts quickly arise. Lack of detailed statistics and the need to avoid spurious accuracy typically require combining sub-cohorts and using fewer, more reliable transition intensities.
Multi-state modelling can be used for pricing (eg individual IP), while the simpler inception/annuity approach may be used where data are insufficient (eg group IP).
For reserving, the same tools apply but assumptions are adjusted for the purpose of the valuation.

key_terms.definition: The annual rate at which lives are moving from state i to state j at exact age x, denoted m(x, i→j). The building block of multi-state models.
A simpler alternative to full multi-state modelling for pricing IP insurance, used when data are insufficient for estimating all transition intensities (eg for group IP business).

subsections.title: Criteria for Data
bullets: Data used to estimate future claims experience should be: relevant (similar class of lives and policy conditions); credible (large enough volume for precise estimates); available; and reliable for the purpose intended.
The cost of obtaining and analysing the data and its format may also need to be considered.
Actuaries use judgement to compile statistics from one or more sources meeting these criteria, then project the data forward to estimate future experience.

key_terms.definition: The extent to which a body of data is large enough to produce statistically reliable estimates — ie with relatively small sampling error.
The extent to which a data source relates to a class of lives and policy conditions similar to those expected for the product being priced.

subsections.title: Projecting to the Mid-Point of Insurance Usage
bullets: Since the pricing database is historic, assumptions must be projected forward to the mid-point of insurance usage — ie the average time over which the pricing basis will be used and applied to in-force policies.
Claim incidence rates must be projected allowing for age-related trends and calendar-year trends.
Claim termination rates must also be projected for improvement or deterioration over time.
For PMI (short-term), projections are much simpler — only one year at a time is needed as premiums are reviewed annually.
Where future experience is uncertain, the contract should allow for some form of premium review — this reduces the margins needed and keeps premiums competitive.

subsections.title: Own Data
bullets: Own data is the most relevant source — it reflects the insurer's specific approach to underwriting, policy conditions, claims management, distribution channel, and target market.
Cost, reliability and format should not present problems with own data.
Credibility is a major issue — for many health and care product lines, individual insurer volumes are insufficient for detailed risk-cell analysis.
A brand-new insurer, or one launching a new product line, may be entirely reliant on external data for several years.

subsections.title: Population Data
bullets: Many governments publish periodic healthcare experience analyses for the national population — this can be the only base for some risk cells.
Drawbacks: accuracy and reliability may be questionable; data may not be in the right format; can be out of date by the time it is published.

Relevance drawback: insured lives are not representative of the population (they have been underwritten), but the improvement may not be as marked as for life insurance due to anti-selection and moral hazard in health products.
Relevance drawback: the definition of sickness/disability in population statistics may differ from the policy claim definition.
Credibility advantage: volumes in national population data make it highly credible.
There is no universal rule for adjusting population data to insured lives — each situation requires specific judgement.

subsections.title: Reinsurers' Data
bullets: Reinsurers draw information from their involvement across many companies in the market — they can assist a new entrant into a product line where no insured experience yet exists.
Reinsurers typically provide data in return for accepting a share of the business (reinsurance treaty) rather than charging a direct fee.
Relevance: the actuary must adjust for the insurer's specific underwriting and claims management practices; the reinsurer may not know these details.
Credibility increases as the product market grows — for truly new products, reinsurers' data may itself lack credibility.

subsections.title: Market Data (Insured Lives Data and Published Returns)
bullets: Insured lives data (industry data): pooled claims and policy statistics from multiple insurers, eg the UK Continuous Mortality Investigation. Published as standard morbidity tables (eg for CI and IP in the UK).
Advantages of industry data: represents insured lives; statistically credible; reflects local market practices and expert preparation.
Disadvantages: it is only a market average (not specific to any one company); may be published years after the experience it covers.
Key point: industry data has more credibility than own data but less relevance; more relevance than population data but less credibility.
Published returns to the insurance supervisor: accurate but hard to extract detailed cell-level information; most useful as a high-level check or for estimating competitors' claim rates.
Market data for short-term classes (especially PMI) is often very limited.

key_terms.definition: A UK body that collects data from nearly all insurers in the IP and CI markets and publishes pooled industry experience, including standard morbidity tables.

subsections.title: Other Sources
bullets: Overseas data: may be credible but is likely to be less relevant due to different cultures, State healthcare provision, market practices, legislation, and policy conditions. A population ratio approach is used to adjust overseas insured experience to local context.
Actuarial consultants: similar role to reinsurers but typically charge a fee rather than sharing in the risk.
Rate table software: useful for gathering initial data and checking the competitiveness and reasonableness of office premiums.
Trade magazines: provide market information and a reasonableness check on premium levels, though rarely include claim statistics.

exam_qa.q: An insurer is expanding into an overseas market for unit-linked IP insurance. Discuss the main data problems it will face.
exam_qa.a: Sickness data: own domestic data may be irrelevant due to different health of population, underwriting, target market, State provision, and attitudes to work. Industry and reinsurer/consultant data from the overseas market may be very heterogeneous and not relevant to unit-linked design (which is rare in the new market). National population statistics may be available but require cautious adjustment. Expense data: domestic data may be unsuitable due to different practices, regulation, distribution methods and commission levels; plus extra costs to educate sellers/consumers about the unit-linked design. Persistency data: domestic data nearly irrelevant; overseas data from without-profits contracts will be misleading for a different contract design. Overall risk: very difficult to price with confidence — significant margins or variable charges will be needed.

subsections.title: Key Principles
bullets: When setting assumptions it is important to: consider the use to which they will be put; take particular care over financially significant assumptions; achieve consistency between assumptions; consider legislative/regulatory constraints; consider the needs of the client; ensure parameters are produced accurately from the data; ensure data are relevant to the insured lives; and ensure bases are flexible to reflect changing risk circumstances.

The general process: (1) investigate historical experience and make best estimates; (2) consider future conditions; (3) determine best estimates for the future period; (4) adjust for margins where required.

Consistency is a guiding principle — assumptions must be considered in totality, not in isolation. Key correlated pairs: morbidity and lapse rates; morbidity and claims expenses; morbidity and investment return; new business volumes and fixed expenses; lapse rates and investment return; investment return and inflation.

key_terms.definition: The complete set of assumptions used for a particular modelling application (eg pricing basis, reserving basis). Includes all demographic, financial, and other assumptions.
An assumption that reflects the actuary's best estimate of future realistic experience, without any margins for prudence.
An adjustment to a best estimate assumption to provide a buffer against adverse experience. The direction (higher or lower than best estimate) depends on which direction is adverse for the purpose.

subsections.title: Pricing Basis and Margins
bullets: The starting point for pricing is a best estimate basis, but margins must be added to guard against adverse experience — the 'funnel of doubt' is wider for health and care than for pure life insurance.

Risk from adverse future experience in a cashflow model may be allowed for through: (1) margins applied to expected parameter values; (2) a stochastic approach; or (3) a risk element in the risk discount rate.
The size of margins depends on: the degree of risk associated with each parameter; and the financial significance of the risk from each parameter.

The margins and price ultimately charged depend on: competitiveness of the market; the company's USP; the company's attitude to risk; the credibility, accuracy, and relevance of the data; and the size of free assets or parental guarantee.

Under-estimating morbidity (leading to under-pricing) is more dangerous than over-estimating (leading to under-selling) — it is better to sell little profitable business than much loss-making business.

key_terms.definition: Standard premium rates applied to all policyholders without reference to their individual past claims experience. Contrasts with experience rating, typically used for group business.
The degree to which a company's product is differentiated from competitors. A high USP allows greater pricing margins.

subsections.title: Reserving Basis Principles
bullets: The starting point is again best estimate assumptions; margins are then added depending on the purpose of the valuation.
Published accounts: must comply with local legislation and accounting principles; consider going-concern vs break-up basis, true-and-fair view, and required level of prudence.

Solvency accounts: may have specific regulatory constraints on assumptions (eg minimum valuation interest rate = pricing interest rate) or more general constraints (eg result must not be lower than a prescribed basis).
Some regimes (including EU/Solvency II) use best estimate base reserves with a separate risk margin — not embedding margins in assumptions.
Two approaches to statutory reserves: (1) prudent assumptions embedded throughout; (2) best estimate assumptions with a separate risk margin and minimum solvency capital requirement.
Best estimate reserves are used for: internal management accounts; sale of business; assessment of reinsurance; investment strategy. For these, there is no elimination of negative reserves.

Statutory reserving vs pricing: where pricing uses realistic assumptions (risk allowed for via risk discount rate), the same basis cannot be used for prudent statutory reserves — a separate, more cautious reserving basis is needed.

key_terms.definition: An assumption that the insurance company will continue to operate, servicing existing and new business.
An assumption that the insurer continues to service in-force business but writes no new business.
An assumption that the insurer is wound up; existing policies with unexpired cover are cancelled (possibly with return of part of the premium).

subsections.title: Embedded Value and Appraisal Value
bullets: Embedded value (EV) = shareholders' share of net assets + present value of future shareholder profits. It requires two separate bases: a reserving basis and a projection basis.
The risk discount rate for EV calculations is typically lower than for pricing, because new business volume/mix uncertainty does not apply to existing business.
EV for internal management accounts and published accounts: likely to use a realistic/best estimate basis.
EV for sale (appraisal value): seller uses realistic assumptions; purchaser uses cautious assumptions — the difference reflects bargaining power.

An allowance for risk must be included in EV — either through a higher risk discount rate (to reflect risky future profits) or through a market-consistent approach (risk-free discount rate with a separate cost-of-capital risk margin).

A market-consistent EV uses the risk-free rate as the discount rate, assumes investments earn the risk-free rate, and deducts a risk margin for non-investment risks using the cost of capital approach.

key_terms.definition: The value of the future profit stream from existing business plus the net assets attributable to shareholders. Considered the best measure of an insurer's real worth.
Embedded value plus the value of expected future new business (goodwill). Used when valuing an insurer for acquisition.

subsections.title: Data, Grouping, Calculation and Adjustment
bullets: The process: (1) collect appropriate data for a suitable period (adequate volume without excessive heterogeneity); (2) group into relevant homogeneous risk cells; (3) calculate rates — often as an adjustment to a standard table; (4) adjust for differences between the data and the expected future experience.
Morbidity investigations are typically based on 3–4 years of data — balancing the desire for large data volumes against the introduction of excessive heterogeneity from different cohorts of lives.

Rates are commonly derived as an adjustment to a standard table: ix = a·ix(S) + b, where ix(S) is the standard table rate at age x, and a and b are chosen constants. This is simpler, more reliable, and enables ongoing comparison with industry experience.

The key risk factors (subdivisions) for long-term health and care products: age; gender; smoker status; occupation; size of benefit; class of product; individual vs group; distribution channel; region; duration since policy entry; underwriting status.
For PMI and some other short-term products: gender and benefit size are less commonly used as rating factors; age (sometimes in bands) is the primary rating factor.

key_terms.definition: A collection of risks sharing the same attributes across all factors that influence morbidity (eg male, aged 45, smoker, professional occupation).
The aggregate time during which policyholders were exposed to the risk of an event (eg claim inception). Used as the denominator when estimating transition rates.

subsections.title: Incidence Rates by Product
bullets: IP: single rates of transition from healthy to claiming, projected allowing for trends.

CI: separate rates derived for each main illness type (cancer, heart attack, stroke, CABG, TPD, etc), then combined. Individual rates must be projected separately before combining — as trends can differ by illness.
LTCI: rates of transition from able to claiming, and from lower to higher benefit levels. No return transitions (recovery is generally ignored).
PMI: incidence rates and average claim costs assessed separately for each benefit/treatment category.

Social and economic influences on incidence: State welfare generosity (affects PMI and IP demand); economic wellbeing and unemployment (worsens IP claim inception rates and recovery rates); inflation (increases PMI claim costs, may reduce renewal rates).

Lapse and re-entry risk: when premiums fall (eg due to competition), healthy lives lapse and re-enter at cheaper rates, leaving only unhealthy lives at the original price — lowers overall portfolio profitability.

subsections.title: Recovery Rate Factors
bullets: Recovery rates are crucial for IP insurance (and relevant to LTCI, though recovery is rarely assumed there).

Recovery rates typically decrease with increasing duration since sickness onset — the majority of claims are short (<6 months); those still claiming after 6 months often remain claiming for long periods.
Recovery rates also decrease with increasing duration since start of claim payments, for similar reasons.
Longer deferred periods are associated with lower recovery rates after claim inception — only the more seriously ill survive to claim.
Claims for large benefit amounts tend to continue longer than small claims.
Medical advances can aid speedier recovery or prolong life in disability — overall effect on claim costs is ambiguous.

Key factors for recovery: motivation and financial incentive to return to work (replacement ratio); availability of suitable work (economic conditions); claims management and rehabilitation by the insurer.

key_terms.definition: The ratio of post-disability net income (benefits + any other income) to pre-disability net income. High replacement ratios reduce the incentive to return to work, worsening recovery rates.

subsections.title: Significance of Mortality
bullets: Separate mortality rates are needed for: healthy (non-claiming) lives; lives in claim under IP and LTCI; and within the CI survival period.

For non-claiming lives: mortality is typically based on a standard table with adjustments for future improvements. The assumption is relatively unimportant unless there is a significant death benefit.

For IP and LTCI claims in payment: the assumption is more important — sick lives have much higher mortality than healthy lives, and the duration of claim payment is directly affected. More research effort is warranted.
For CI survival period: the effect of mortality post-diagnosis is typically allowed for by an adjustment to the CI claim incidence rate, specific to each illness type.

For policies without a death benefit: it is important not to over-estimate mortality, as death reduces the probability of future health claims — over-estimating mortality would understate expected claim costs.
Medical advances generally reduce mortality — this can increase LTCI and IP claim costs (claims last longer) while having a smaller impact on CI claim incidence.

key_terms.definition: A policy condition requiring a CI claimant to survive for a specified period (eg 28 days) following diagnosis before a claim is payable. Reduces the insurer's exposure to near-immediately terminal illnesses.

subsections.title: Lapse Rate Assumptions
bullets: Lapse rates are based primarily on the insurer's own recent experience, as market data is much less useful — lapse rates are more unpredictable (influenced by human choice), more heterogeneous between insurers, and more sensitive to economic/commercial factors.
No population data exists for lapses — there is no population equivalent of stopping premium payments.

Lapse rates matter most when: they occur early in the policy term (financial loss when asset share is negative); a surrender value is payable; lapses are selective (healthier lives lapse, leaving a worse risk pool); or there is a lapse-and-re-entry risk.
Selective lapsing: for PMI, the lowest claiming members have the highest lapse rates — higher claiming members value the cover more and can less easily obtain it elsewhere.
Factors influencing lapses: economic (affordability), political (State benefit alternatives), commercial (competitor products), awareness (perceived need).
For policies without surrender values and with positive asset shares, a lapse benefits the insurer (asset share retained).

Economic deterioration effect: CI — minimal impact (low premiums, no surrender value); pre-funded LTCI — some increase in withdrawals (higher premiums, possible paid-up options); PMI — significant increase in non-renewals (high premiums, annual decision to renew).

key_terms.definition: Where policyholders lapse existing policies and take out new ones at current lower premium rates. Only healthy lives can benefit, leaving the insurer with deteriorating risk in the original portfolio.
The tendency for lower-risk (healthier) lives to lapse at a higher rate than higher-risk lives, leaving a deteriorating risk pool among those remaining.

subsections.title: Benefit Amount
bullets: Long-term contracts (IP, CI, LTCI): benefit level is fixed in policy terms; premiums are expressed as a rate per unit of sum insured. Only for very large policies would the insurer vary assumptions (due to stricter underwriting and better socio-economic profile of claimants).

PMI: the actuary must forecast both the number and amount of each claim. Average claim amounts are based on own experience or a similar insurer's data, adjusted for: differences in policy conditions; differences in negotiated hospital contracts; and future trends in provider capacity and charges.
Alternatively, private hospital treatment price lists can be used as a starting point for PMI claim amounts.

subsections.title: Benefit Inflation
bullets: Long-term contracts: benefits are often index-linked (eg consumer price index, earnings index, fixed percentage). Even with fixed-rate increases, a model must project the inflated benefit amounts throughout the term.

Best index for IP: national earnings index (replaces lost employment income). For CI: various options (care costs, debt repayment, earnings — in practice often fixed). For LTCI: nursing and residential care cost index.
PMI benefit inflation: depends on medical inflation, changes in treatment protocols, hospital charging structures, age profile of portfolio, and provider capacity.
Many insurers cap benefit inflation (especially LTCI) to contain costs within affordable premiums.

subsections.title: Expense Assumptions
bullets: Expense types: initial acquisition (commission, marketing); initial medical underwriting; initial administration; renewal administration; renewal commission; investment; withdrawal; claim administration; termination.

PMI has the highest renewal administration costs (full premium review each year with potential change in conditions). Investment and withdrawal expenses are unimportant for PMI (short-term, minimal reserves).

Health and care insurance generally has higher expenses than life insurance due to: lower non-medical limits (more applications requiring full underwriting); product complexity (more selling effort); higher claims management costs.

Per-policy expenses do not vary with benefit size. Since premiums are expressed per unit of benefit, smaller policies cross-subsidise larger ones. Solutions: individual premium calculations; policy fee addition; sum insured differential (different rates for different benefit size bands).
The most common approach is a policy fee addition, sometimes combined with a sum insured differential.
Expense assumptions based on own expense analysis; if insufficient, use industry or reinsurers' data.

key_terms.definition: The maximum benefit level for which an application can be accepted without automatically requiring a medical examination or private medical attendant's report.
A flat addition to the premium (or deduction from benefits) used to cover per-policy expenses that do not vary with the sum insured.

subsections.title: Expense Inflation
bullets: Expense inflation must be allowed for, especially for long-term products — expenses are typically a mix of price inflation and salary (earnings) inflation.

Relevant considerations: current price and earnings inflation rates; expected future rates; differential between nominal and index-linked government bond yields; recent actual company/industry experience.
Two aspects of expense inflation when pricing: inflation of expenses during the policy term itself; and inflation of all expenses between now and the dates when future policies will be issued.
An inflation assumption must be made even for reviewable contracts, as the insurer commits to cover being provided and expenses will increase over time.

subsections.title: Commission and Clawback Assumptions
bullets: Commission rates reflect current market levels and any legislative requirements. Commission is generally higher for health and care than for life insurance, due to greater product complexity and underwriting effort.
Commission can be expressed as: a percentage of premium; a percentage of sum insured; or a fixed amount.

Commission clawback: where initial commission is higher than renewal commission and the policy lapses early, the insurer can clawback unearned commission. Clawback assumptions must allow for the credit risk of the intermediary (possibility of default).
The actuary must estimate early lapse rates and the proportion of unearned commission that will actually be reclaimed when setting the pricing basis.
If multiple distributors receive different commission rates for the same product, a weighted average based on expected volumes by channel is required.

key_terms.definition: The recovery by the insurer of part of the initial commission paid to a salesperson when a policy lapses before the commission is deemed 'earned'. The insurer bears a credit risk that the intermediary cannot repay.

subsections.title: Investment Return Assumption
bullets: The investment return assumption must be consistent with inflation assumptions — the implied real return should be realistic.
The significance of the investment return assumption depends on: the size of reserves relative to cashflows; the duration of the policy; and the extent of any investment guarantees.
For regular premium CI insurance: not very important (small reserves, profits insensitive to investment return).
For pre-funded LTCI: very important — especially single premium, where reserves are very large for a long period.
For PMI: of almost no importance (one-year policies, minimal reserves).

The intended investment mix matters: for fixed benefits, fixed-interest bonds are most appropriate; for inflation-linked benefits, index-linked bonds; for equity-like benefit growth (LTCI care costs), some equity investment is appropriate.

Reinvestment risk: the uncertainty of the return from future investment. High for regular premium LTCI (cannot match all liabilities until late in term); low for single premium LTCI (better matching is possible); negligible for PMI.
Under a market-consistent approach, the risk-free rate is used as the discount rate regardless of actual assets held (risk-neutral calibration).

key_terms.definition: The uncertainty of the return that can be obtained from investing future positive cashflows. More important when the policy term is long and liabilities are difficult to match with existing assets.

subsections.title: Tax Assumptions
bullets: Tax rates assumed based on current rates plus known future changes.

Three main types: (1) taxes on profits — the insurer's profit is taxed; premiums must be set to achieve the required post-tax return; (2) taxes on investment income less expenses (I–E basis) — the insurer includes investment income and expenses net of tax; (3) taxes on premiums — the insurer collects tax from policyholders on the State's behalf; indirect impact on lapse/non-renewal assumptions.

key_terms.definition: A tax method where the insurer is taxed on the excess of investment income over allowable expenses. Each item of investment income and expenses is included net of the relevant tax rate.

exam_qa.q: Discuss the importance of the investment return, expense and withdrawal rate assumptions when pricing: (i) without-profits CI insurance; (ii) without-profits immediate needs LTC annuity.
exam_qa.a: (i) CI insurance: Investment return — not very important; reserves are small. Expenses — important; initial underwriting and claim administration costs are relatively high for CI; per-policy expenses must be allocated carefully. Withdrawals — important at early durations (when asset share is negative), a financial loss arises. Lapse and re-entry risk is also relevant if the market becomes more competitive. (ii) Immediate needs LTC annuity: Investment return — very important; these are single-premium policies with large, long-duration reserves; matching assets closely to liabilities is critical; reinvestment risk is relatively low since it is a single premium with immediate claim payments. Expenses — important, especially claim administration for LTCI with multiple benefit levels; but lower than for pre-funded LTCI as no initial underwriting. Withdrawals — not applicable; surrender is typically not permitted for immediate needs LTCI annuities.

subsections.title: New Business Volume
bullets: New business volume assumptions are needed primarily for pricing (to allocate per-policy contributions to fixed expenses) and to assess the capital required to support new sales (new business strain).
For reserving and profitability assessment, actual volumes and mix are known — new business volume assumptions are not required.

subsections.title: New Business Mix
bullets: Mix assumptions are needed when the insurer charges the same premium to heterogeneous groups — if the actual mix differs from assumed, cross-subsidies become losses or gains.
Anti-selection risk: competitors with more detailed risk subdivisions will attract the lighter risks, leaving the insurer with a higher-risk portfolio than assumed.
Mix assumptions are needed by: average policy size; product type; distribution channel; territory; gender (where unisex pricing is required); occupation.

Average policy size affects: the allocation of per-policy expenses; and expected claims experience (larger policies tend to have better claim experience due to stricter underwriting and higher socio-economic profile).
Mix by distribution channel is crucial where different commission rates apply but the same tariff is charged across channels.
Mix by territory: important where health and care risk varies materially by region (eg heavy industrial urban vs agricultural rural for IP).

key_terms.definition: The distribution of new policyholders across different risk categories (age, gender, occupation, distribution channel etc). Errors in assumed mix are a key source of pricing risk.

subsections.title: Components of the Risk Discount Rate
bullets: The risk discount rate reflects: (1) the return required by shareholders (risk-free rate + risk premium for investing in the insurer); and (2) the level of statistical risk attaching to the cashflows under the specific product.

Shareholders' required return is often assessed using the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM): E(i) = r + β·(E(m) − r), where r is the risk-free rate, E(m) is the expected market return, and β (beta) measures the riskiness of the company's shares relative to the market.
Beta > 1 implies the share moves more than the market (higher risk, higher required return). Beta is a measure of systematic (non-diversifiable) risk only.
The overall required return Ei from CAPM is not used directly — products that are more risky than average require a higher discount rate than Ei; less risky products require a lower one.
Product-specific (statistical) risk is assessed via: analytical methods (if variance of parameters can be derived); sensitivity analysis; stochastic modelling; or comparison with market data.
Key features that increase product risk (raising the discount rate): lack of historical data; high guarantees; policyholder options; high overhead costs; complexity of design; untested market.
Key features that decrease product risk: abundant reliable data; reviewable premiums; no policyholder options; low overheads; simple benefits; well-established market.

Important principles: the risk discount rate must exceed the risk-free rate; the margin over the risk-free rate must reflect all sources of risk; overall return on company capital must meet the required return (cost of capital).

key_terms.definition: E(i) = r + β·(E(m) − r). Used to estimate the expected return investors require for a risky asset relative to the risk-free rate. Beta measures systematic risk.
The covariance of a share's return with the market return, divided by the variance of the market return. A measure of an asset's systematic risk relative to the market.
The risk arising from the variability of cashflow components in a pricing model. Assessed through sensitivity analysis, stochastic models, or analytical methods.

subsections.title: Market-Consistent Approach
bullets: An alternative to the CAPM/statistical approach: use risk-free interest rates as the discount rate (risk-neutral calibration), with margins included in each parameter assumption to reflect the inherent risks in their estimation.
The risk margin under market-consistent approaches reflects the compensation required by the 'market' for taking on uncertain aspects of the liability cashflows.
Market-consistent valuations are also useful as a reasonableness check on traditional approaches and for verifying that risk margins are appropriate.

key_terms.definition: A valuation that uses risk-free discount rates (reflecting the risk-neutral measure) and includes explicit risk margins in parameter assumptions. Useful for products with options and guarantees.

exam_qa.q: What is the risk discount rate, and what two main factors should it reflect?
exam_qa.a: The risk discount rate is the rate used to discount net projected cashflows in a profit test, ensuring the expected return meets shareholders' requirements. It should reflect: (1) the return required by shareholders — the risk-free rate plus a risk premium for investing in the insurance company (often quantified via CAPM); and (2) the statistical risk attaching to the cashflows of the specific product — products that are riskier (due to lack of data, high guarantees, complex design, policyholder options, or untested markets) require a higher discount rate.

subsections.title: Inception / Disabled Life Annuity Approach
bullets: The inception / disabled life annuity approach determines the expected cost of benefits from claims incepting in a particular year. It considers two functions: (1) the claim inception rate, and (2) the disabled life annuity.
It ignores recoveries (or makes no distinction between recoveries and death) and ignores subsequent future sickness — a simplification of the full multi-state model.

Claim inception rate (iˣᵈ): probability that a claim will become payable to an individual aged x to x+1. The individual became sick d weeks/months earlier and remained sick through to benefit commencement.
Sickness inception rates ≠ Claim inception rates. Sickness inception = point individual first unable to work. Claim inception = end of deferred period when benefit payments commence.
Claim inception rates are derived from sickness inception rates by multiplying the sickness inception rate by the probability of remaining sick throughout the deferred period.

Disabled life annuity: present value at the date of claim inception of expected claim payments until policy expiry, allowing for any escalation of the claim amount, interest, and the probabilities of death and recovery.

Expected PV of claims incepting in a particular year = (1) survival probability (eligible to claim) × (2) claim inception rate × (3) disabled life annuity value × (4) discount factor × (5) annual benefit amount.
The survival probability excludes policyholders already claiming and those who have died before the year under consideration.

key_terms.definition: Probability that a claim becomes payable in a given year of age, after surviving the deferred period. Not the same as sickness inception rate.
PV at claim inception of expected benefit payments until policy expiry, allowing for escalation, interest, death and recovery.
Probability that policyholder is alive, not already claiming, and eligible to claim in the year under consideration.

exam_qa.q: When are sickness and claim inception rates equal?
exam_qa.a: When there is no deferred period, so benefit payments commence immediately as the policyholder falls sick.

subsections.title: Multi-State Modelling
bullets: Multi-state modelling is the more complex alternative to the inception/annuity approach. Policyholders are separately tracked through: (1) healthy premium payers, (2) lives falling sick within deferred period, (3) lives becoming claimants after deferred period, (4) lives recovering and reverting to premium payers, (5) lives dying.
Each subclass has its own set of transition probabilities: sickness inception, lapse, mortality, recovery, policy expiry.
Probabilities may vary by number of previous claims and by duration within a given state — making the model potentially very complex with hundreds of sub-cohorts.
In practice, lack of detailed credible data typically requires combining sub-cohorts and/or reducing the number of transition intensities required.
Transition intensities must be converted into transition probabilities before constructing projections of numbers in each state at future ages.

key_terms.definition: The instantaneous rate of moving from one state to another (e.g. healthy to sick). Must be converted to transition probabilities for use in projections.
A group of policyholders sharing a common history of states (e.g. same number of prior claims, same duration in current state).

subsections.title: CI Incidence Rates
bullets: CI pricing is much simpler than IP because the benefit is a lump sum rather than an income.
Overall CI incidence rate: iˣ = iˣʰᵈ + iˣˢ + iˣᶜ + iˣᵒ, where hd = heart disease, s = stroke, c = cancers, o = other causes.

Overlaps: a person may suffer both cancer and heart disease, but only one claim is paid. If claim experience data is used to derive incidence rates, double-counting is automatically avoided by attributing each claim to a single main cause.

Double-counting risk arises when population medical records (not insurance claims data) are used, as they may record disease incidence by cause without indicating whether other diseases are present simultaneously.

key_terms.definition: Rate at which policyholders are diagnosed with (and claim for) a covered critical illness in a given year of age. Sum of cause-specific rates.
Overstating combined CI incidence by counting a single claimant under multiple cause-specific rates. Leads to overstatement of claim cost (standalone) or understatement (accelerated).

subsections.title: Accelerated CI Insurance
bullets: Accelerated CI claim incidence rate: iˣ = iˣ + (1 − kˣ) × qˣ, where iˣ = CI incidence rate, kˣ = proportion of deaths due to CI, qˣ = mortality rate.
The term (1 − kˣ) × qˣ adjusts the mortality rate to remove deaths already counted as CI claims; equivalently, kˣ × qˣ reduces CI incidence to remove those CI events already captured as deaths.
Double-counting risk (overstating kˣ): understates claim cost → inadequate premiums and reserves — a solvency risk. This is the more serious error for accelerated CI.
Expected claim cost = claim incidence rate × CI sum insured.

key_terms.definition: Proportion of deaths in year of age [x, x+1] that are due to critical illness. Used in accelerated CI incidence rate formula.
CI benefit paid on earlier of death or CI diagnosis. The CI sum insured is an advance of the death benefit; death benefit is reduced by any CI payment made.

exam_qa.q: How does double-counting of kˣ cause problems for accelerated CI?
exam_qa.a: Overstating kˣ understates the net claim incidence rate, which understates the cost of claims, leading to inadequate premiums and reserves — a solvency risk.

subsections.title: Standalone CI Insurance
bullets: Standalone CI claim incidence rate = iˣ × probability of surviving the survival period.
Only claimants who survive the defined survival period receive the benefit, so the raw CI incidence rate is multiplied by this survival probability.
Double-counting for standalone CI leads to overstatement of incidence rates → uncompetitive premiums (less serious than understating for accelerated CI, as it does not threaten solvency).
The formula is applied to each CI condition separately and then combined to produce the overall risk premium.

key_terms.definition: Minimum period the claimant must survive after CI diagnosis before the benefit is paid (typically 14–28 days). Reduces overlap between CI and death benefits.
CI policy that pays only on diagnosis of an insured condition; no death benefit. Priced purely on CI incidence × survival probability.

exam_qa.q: Why is double-counting less serious for standalone than accelerated CI?
exam_qa.a: For standalone CI, double-counting overstates incidence rates, leading to uncompetitive premiums but not threatening solvency. For accelerated CI, double-counting understates rates, which can lead to inadequate reserves and premiums — a solvency risk.

subsections.title: Pricing Approach
bullets: LTCI pricing is very similar to IP: either inception / annuity methodology or multi-state modelling can be used.

Multi-state model tracks transitions between dependency levels, e.g.: State 0 (Healthy) → State 1 (Benefit level 1) → State 2 (Benefit level 2) → State 3 (Benefit level 3) → State 4 (Dead). Transitions to lower benefit levels may be assumed impossible.
Forces of transition between all states are required for each arrow in the state diagram. Transition rates may need to vary by duration in current state and by cause of incapacity.

Using inception/annuity for LTCI: three claim inception rates are needed at each future age (one per benefit level). Each is multiplied by the corresponding disability annuity value and the incremental benefit at that level.

Incremental benefit pricing: if Level 1 = £20/day, Level 2 = £50/day, Level 3 = £150/day, then the additional benefit at Level 2 is £30/day (not £50), because Level 1 benefit continues into all higher levels.
Expected PV of benefits = Σₜ [p(survive to t+½) × vᵗ⁺½ × (inception rate level 1 × annuity level 1 × benefit 1 + inception rate level 2 × annuity level 2 × incremental benefit 2 + ...)].

key_terms.definition: The additional daily/weekly benefit payable on transition to a higher care level, not the total benefit at that level. Used in the inception/annuity approach to avoid double-counting lower-level benefits.

exam_qa.q: How can advances in medical science affect LTCI modelling?
exam_qa.a: They can: (1) reduce transition rates to higher benefit levels (better treatments allow people to stay independent longer); (2) increase mortality improvement (prolonging life and thus care duration, increasing claim costs); (3) alter care costs (drugs, mobility aids etc).

exam_qa.q: What inflation index would you use for LTCI care costs?
exam_qa.a: Ideally a long-term care cost index (e.g. residential/nursing home fees index). Failing that, a national earnings index is most suitable as LTC is a people industry. A blend weighted toward earnings with some weight on price inflation would typically be used. Additional inflationary factors specific to LTC must also be considered.

subsections.title: Calculating the Risk Premium
bullets: PMI is a short-term product priced on an incidence × average claim cost basis.

Risk premium (age, gender) = Σₖ [iₖ × ACₖ], where iₖ = claim incidence rate for benefit/procedure class k, and ACₖ = expected average claim cost for class k. Summation is over all benefit/procedure classes k.
iₖ and ACₖ are specific to each age, gender, and product level (e.g. hospital band). Age bands (5- or 10-year) may be used to ensure sufficient data in each cell.

Rating factors beyond age and gender — location, hospital band, smoker status, occupation — may be used depending on market practice, legislation, and competitive pressure. What competitors do is often the strongest driver toward more risk classification factors.
Equality legislation in some territories prevents or restricts differential pricing even by age and/or gender.
Multi-state modelling is used if premiums are guaranteed for longer than one year (similar approach to long-term products).

Data collection process: (1) Choose base period; (2) Collect and check data; (3) Split into homogeneous groups; (4) Analyse and identify trends; (5) Adjust and project forward to obtain future risk premiums.

key_terms.definition: Element of premium covering expected claims only. No allowance for expenses, profit etc. Calculated as Σ [incidence rate × average claim cost] across all benefit/procedure classes.
Category of treatment or procedure (e.g. surgical, diagnostic, physiotherapy) for which separate incidence rates and average claim costs are estimated.

subsections.title: Adjustments to the Risk Premium
bullets: Excess: reduces both incidence (only claims above the excess are paid) and average claim cost (claim amounts are reduced by the excess). Three adjustments needed: (1) reduce incidence for proportion of claims below the excess; (2) reduce incidence further for disincentive effect (especially voluntary excess); (3) reduce average claim cost for the excess (not by the full excess amount, as some claims are below it). Claim amount distribution may need to be assumed.

No Claims Discount (NCD): policyholders with consecutive claim-free years receive a premium discount. A claim reduces or eliminates the discount. NCD acts like an excess by discouraging small claims. Multi-state NCD model required to capture policyholder movement between NCD states.

Gender-neutral pricing: if required, weight gender-specific risk premiums by expected male:female ratio among new policyholders. Risk: if one gender is materially cheaper, anti-selection results — disproportionate uptake by the cheaper gender worsens actual claims experience vs assumptions.
Coinsurance and other product features that impact incidence or average cost must also be reflected.

key_terms.definition: Premium reduction for policyholders with consecutive claim-free years. A claim (or multiple claims) reduces or eliminates the discount. Discourages small claims similarly to an excess.
Model tracking policyholders across multiple NCD states (e.g. 0%, 10%, 25%, 40% discount levels), with transition probabilities based on claim experience.

exam_qa.q: Suggest how you might allow for an excess in calculating the PMI risk premium.
exam_qa.a: Three adjustments: (1) Reduce incidence to allow only for claims expected to exceed the excess — may require assuming a claim amount distribution; (2) Reduce incidence further for the disincentive effect of the excess (especially if voluntary); (3) Reduce average claim cost to allow for the excess — the reduction is less than the full excess amount as some claims would have been below it.

subsections.title: Pricing Approach
bullets: Accident and sickness (ASU) insurance is short-term IP (typically providing benefits for up to 5 years), often used to protect loan repayments.
Rating method follows the inception / annuity approach, as with standard IP.

Average rate approach: an average rate per £ of loan is often agreed for a particular lender or scheme, charging the same premium to all policyholders regardless of age or gender. This simplifies administration but introduces cross-subsidy across the age/gender mix.
Large ASU schemes are rated using group techniques (experience rating), covered in Chapter 18.

key_terms.definition: Single premium rate per £ of loan applicable to all policyholders in a lender/scheme, regardless of individual risk characteristics. Involves cross-subsidy between age/gender groups.

subsections.title: Pricing Approach
bullets: Premiums determined by calculating expected claims for each benefit separately: expected claims per benefit = inception rate × average benefit expected to be paid (adjusted for any excess or coinsurance).
Benefits covered typically include: dental, optical, physiotherapy, maternity, daily hospital cash, nursing care, hearing aids, specialist consultations. Each benefit will have annual payment limits.
For indemnity-type benefits within cash plans (e.g. 50% of physiotherapy costs up to £200/year), expected average cost of the benefit is used rather than the defined benefit amount.
Coinsurance with the policyholder is common to reduce anti-selection and keep premiums affordable. An excess may also apply (e.g. benefit paid only from 4th consecutive day of hospital stay).
Flat-rate pricing over broad age ranges is common despite age-varying incidence — approximations are made based on prior claim experience.

Adjustment for inertia: people's propensity not to claim even when entitled. Two causes: (1) policyholders buy for one specific benefit and forget others; (2) benefit amounts are small in absolute terms, combined with customer apathy. No inertia adjustment needed if already embedded in the company's own claims experience data.
Inertia level may change if marketing strategy changes or if market awareness of cash plan benefits increases — monitor and update accordingly.

key_terms.definition: The propensity of policyholders not to claim even when entitled, due to small benefit sizes and low awareness of all covered benefits. Reduces actual claim frequency below theoretical maximum.

subsections.title: IP Insurance — Modelling Complexities
bullets: Transition intensities: must separate out first, second, and subsequent inceptions — prior claimants are more likely to claim again.
Recovery rates must vary by duration of claim (recovery rates reduce as duration increases) and by cause of incapacity.

Sales volumes are particularly hard to estimate: depend on economic and political factors; IP markets are typically under-insured; competitive position can dramatically affect sales; claims mishandling can damage reputation.

Transition intensities (and sales volumes) are influenced by: economic morale (downturn encourages prolonged claims; self-employed less motivated to return to work), government welfare provision (high State benefits reduce IP demand and reduce return-to-work incentive), and tax (premium taxes reduce sales; tax relief increases sales).

Modelling complications: multi-state movement hierarchy; guaranteed vs reviewable alternatives (guarantees require full allowance and suitable risk margins; reviewable products allow modelling only to next review date but competitive pressures and anti-selective lapses must still be considered).

Data limitations: published IP claim inception tables have limited credibility; worldwide statistics (especially from USA) are plentiful but may lack relevance due to heterogeneity in target markets, underwriting strategies, policy wordings, and claims management.

exam_qa.q: What is the implication of limited credibility of published IP parameters for modelling?
exam_qa.a: Cannot rely heavily on standard tables. Must seek alternative data sources: reinsurers, population statistics, own data if sufficient. If data is universally insufficient: (1) assume large margins for risk; (2) issue only reviewable products; (3) ensure product is well protected by reinsurance.

subsections.title: CI Insurance — Modelling Complexities
bullets: Must estimate up to 40 different condition distributions separately, including future trends for each — practical data is unlikely to support this for all but the most common conditions.
Medical advances affect new and in-force business differently: conditions can be removed from cover on new policies; existing guaranteed policies cannot be changed (unless reviewable).
Earlier diagnosis increases claim incidence rates — condition is diagnosed before severe deterioration, meaning more people qualify for the benefit.
Simpler/more available operations: may increase treatment-based CI claims.

Modelling should use separate approaches for disease-based vs treatment-based claims, as future trends differ — medical advances may reduce disease claims (prevention) but increase treatment-based claims (more available procedures).

Guaranteed vs reviewable: as for IP, guaranteed products require full risk margins; reviewable products allow modelling to next review but must still allow for competitive pressures and anti-selective lapses.
Data limitations: CI has not been around long enough for credible claims experience except possibly for cancers and heart attacks.

exam_qa.q: Why should disease-based and treatment-based CI claims be modelled separately?
exam_qa.a: They are subject to different future trends. Medical advances may prevent diseases (reducing disease-type claims) while simultaneously making treatments more available (increasing treatment-based claims). Modelling them together would obscure these diverging trends.

subsections.title: LTCI — Modelling Complexities
bullets: Claim frequency: limited experience data on transition rates — a major difficulty in most territories.

Claim amount: where policy pays funding-for-care (indemnity) benefits, must estimate a probability distribution of annual/monthly claim payments. Claim amounts depend on economy, inflation, and capacity of care provision (demand likely to grow faster than supply, driving real cost increases above general inflation).
Portfolio projection demand is very dependent on political commitment: government policy on State LTC funding directly affects new business volumes and mix.
Data limitations: absence of insurance statistics — very little pooled industry data for LTCI.

subsections.title: PMI — Modelling Complexities
bullets: Claim frequency: difficult to define a discrete claim (one event may involve multiple payments across a chain of care). Must establish a consistent claim definition used for both frequency and amount estimation.

Claim frequency parameters depend on: duration from inception (longer-duration policyholders more likely to claim as new conditions arise beyond the pre-existing exclusion period), age, gender, time of year, occupation.

Claim amount: indemnity, often without a ceiling. Distribution depends on hospital capacity (reduced supply increases costs), medical science progress (may increase or decrease costs), insurer/provider deals (reduce costs and variance), inflation (increases costs).
Macroeconomy: affordability pressure reduces demand; near recession onset, group members may accelerate treatment while still covered.
Style of government: affects State provision attractiveness and whether tax incentives exist for PMI.
Level of market competition: more competition → lower premiums; product differentiation makes direct comparison harder.
NCD multi-states: must model policyholder movement between NCD levels.
Reclaim of initial costs: renewal assumptions should be conservative (low) to reduce risk of unrecouped initial expenses if lapses are higher than assumed.

Claim chain: PMI claims often consist of multiple payments (operation + follow-up physiotherapy etc) — the full chain of claim events must be modelled. Settlement delay is usually short for PMI but must be allowed for.
Data limitations: absence of pooled industry statistics in many markets; family cover and group arrangements mean individual risk details may not be known.

exam_qa.q: Why is estimating PMI claim frequency difficult?
exam_qa.a: The difficulty lies in defining what constitutes a discrete claim. A single treatment event may involve multiple separate payments. A consistent claim definition must be established and used for both frequency estimation and claim amount modelling.

exam_qa.q: Why does PMI claim frequency increase with policy duration?
exam_qa.a: Pre-existing conditions are typically excluded at outset (via underwriting or moratorium). As the policy continues, new medical conditions arise and are covered. Longer-duration policyholders have also self-selected for continued cover, suggesting higher expected future claims.

subsections.title: Other Modelling Issues (All Products)
bullets: Role of genetics: genetic tests can reveal predispositions to certain conditions. In some countries (e.g. UK) this information does not have to be disclosed at underwriting, creating potential for increased anti-selection. Extent of claims impact from this practice is still emerging.
Trends in anti-selection: people who consider themselves high risk are most likely to apply for cover. Underwriting is the primary tool to combat this.
Quality of underwriting: good underwriting produces more optimistic pricing assumptions (e.g. lower CI rates) than ununderwritten business. Must be consistently reflected in pricing assumptions.

key_terms.definition: Individuals with known genetic predispositions to certain conditions may preferentially seek cover without disclosing this information, worsening the insured risk pool relative to pricing assumptions.

subsections.title: Key Differences from Individual Business
bullets: Free cover limit: group schemes may have a free cover limit below which members are not individually underwritten, reducing anti-selection risk.

Control of the intermediary: an intermediary may influence available data; the insurer must ensure information is accurate and consistent with competitors. The insurer may also want to influence group policyholders to reduce work-related health risks and encourage return to work, but this may be hindered by the intermediary.

Limited insured information: full details of individual members may not be known at the start of the insurance period. Changes in staff also occur during cover. A deposit premium is paid upfront, followed by a retrospective adjustment premium at period end.

Changes in the workforce can significantly alter the riskiness of a group: leavers may be healthy or unhealthy depending on reason for leaving; joiners are typically healthy (especially if young); downsizing may prompt members to claim while still covered; workforce expansion from a merger/acquisition requires careful review.
Flexible benefits (cafeteria plans) increase anti-selection risk by permitting individuals to choose covers and amounts. The insurer may impose limits and restrictions on benefit choices.

key_terms.definition: The level of benefit below which group members are automatically accepted without individual underwriting.
An upfront premium paid at the start of the group insurance period, subject to retrospective adjustment at period end based on actual membership.
A scheme structure allowing employees to choose from a menu of benefit options, increasing anti-selection risk for the insurer.

exam_qa.q: Why do changes in the workforce affect claims experience under a group scheme?
exam_qa.a: Leavers may be healthy or unhealthy depending on their reason for leaving. Joiners tend to be young and healthy. Downsizing may encourage remaining members to claim while still covered. Merger-driven growth may improve experience initially but needs careful monitoring.

exam_qa.q: Why does the insurer need control over the intermediary in group business?
exam_qa.a: The intermediary influences the data available to the insurer and may prevent the insurer from directly encouraging policyholders to reduce health risks or return to work, both of which affect claims experience.

subsections.title: Standard Rating Approach
bullets: The usual procedures apply: judge data for relevance to future experience; subdivide into homogeneous risk cells; adjust to make appropriate for future experience (both claim incidence and claim size, e.g. for inflation).
Rating factors for group business include individual-level factors (age, gender, occupation) and group-level factors (industry, location).
The book rate is the insurer's standard premium for a group of given characteristics, before any adjustment for that group's own past experience.

key_terms.definition: The insurer's standard premium rate for a group based on general rating factors, before experience rating adjustment.

subsections.title: The Credibility Formula
bullets: The renewal premium under experience rating is: RP = Z × A + (1 − Z) × E + L, where E is the book premium, A is the risk premium based on the group's own past experience, Z is the credibility factor (0 ≤ Z ≤ 1), and L is an expense/profit loading.
The credibility factor Z depends on the volume and reliability of the group's past experience data. Higher Z means more weight on the group's own experience.
Premiums for which Z > 0 are experience rated.

Experience rating is used because a book rate may be inappropriate due to peculiarities of a specific workplace, working processes, or the employer's attitude to safety. The group's own claims history is often the best predictor of future claims.
Experience rating makes premiums more competitive and reduces the need for a large number of rating factors. It is unsuitable for new or very small groups with insufficient past data.

key_terms.definition: A system where the premium for a group contract depends wholly or partly on the group's own past claims experience.
The weight given to the group's own experience in the premium formula, ranging from 0 (full book rate) to 1 (full experience rate).
The estimated cost of claims in the forthcoming period, calculated from past experience adjusted for changes in membership, cover and medical inflation.

exam_qa.q: Why is experience rating used for most group health contracts?
exam_qa.a: A single book rate may be inappropriate due to the peculiarities of a specific workplace, working processes, or employer safety attitudes. The group's own past claims history, suitably adjusted, is often the best predictor of future claims. It also makes premiums more competitive and reduces the number of rating factors needed.

exam_qa.q: When is experience rating unsuitable?
exam_qa.a: When the group's past claims experience is insufficient or unreliable, for example for a new or very small group.

subsections.title: Burning Cost Method
bullets: The burning cost is the estimated cost of claims in the coming period, calculated from past years' experience adjusted for: changes in the numbers insured, changes in the nature of cover, and medical inflation.
Yearly burning cost calculations compare emerging claim costs against risk premiums paid, providing the earliest possible feedback on premium adequacy.
Large individual claims are typically stripped out of the base data and added back using a grossing-up factor based on the ratio of large to total claims across all similar group policies.
Claims data must be adjusted to a full year (e.g. if only 9 months of data are available for the most recent year) and inflated to the midpoint of the prospective cover period.
Trend analysis is performed on the per-employee cost to identify and project any underlying deterioration or improvement.

exam_qa.q: How are large claims handled in a burning cost calculation?
exam_qa.a: Large claims are stripped out of the base claims data. A grossing-up factor (based on the ratio of large to total claims across all similar group policies) is then applied to add back an allowance for large claims, smoothing the distortion caused by one-off large events.

subsections.title: Group PMI Pricing Features
bullets: Experience rating is used extensively for group PMI pricing, because group PMI experience differs significantly between employers even in the same industry.

Reasons experience varies between groups: different geographical locations affect private medical treatment costs; nature of work affects employees' medical needs; employer attitudes to health and safety differ.
NCD systems may be used alongside experience rating to incentivise good claims behaviour.
Larger groups are often flat rated according to benefit class (e.g. all members of the same plan tier pay the same rate). The largest groups may self-insure.
Experience rating for group PMI encourages employers to actively manage claims and promote workplace health, reducing overall costs.
Applying group-specific premiums improves marketability and sales volume by making pricing fairer for low-claims groups.

key_terms.definition: Charging the same premium rate to all members within a benefit class, typically used for larger group PMI schemes.
Where the largest employers bear claims risk themselves rather than transferring it to an insurer, often with reinsurance above a certain threshold.

exam_qa.q: Why is group PMI experience rated rather than book rated?
exam_qa.a: Group PMI experience varies significantly between employers even in the same industry, due to differences in location (affecting treatment costs), work practices (affecting health needs), and employer attitudes to health and safety. Past claims history is a powerful predictor of future experience, and experience rating encourages employers to reduce claims costs.

subsections.title: Overview of Profit Criteria
bullets: A profit criterion is a single figure that tries to summarise the profit signature of a contract. The profit signature is the sequence of profits from inception to termination — it is the definitive guide to profitability but unwieldy for comparison.
Three main profit criteria: Net Present Value (NPV), Internal Rate of Return (IRR), and Discounted Payback Period (DPP).
Sensitivity tests should be performed throughout the pricing process to ensure different future scenarios do not lead to serious financial difficulty.

key_terms.definition: The sequence of expected annual profits from policy inception to termination, used as the definitive measure of a product's profitability.
A single figure derived from the profit signature (e.g. NPV, IRR, DPP) used to compare the profitability of different product designs.

subsections.title: Net Present Value (NPV)
