bullets: NPV is calculated by discounting the profit signature at the risk discount rate. A positive NPV means the rate of return exceeds the risk discount rate target.
Economic theory states NPV is the best profit criterion: an investor should choose the investment with the higher NPV to maximise company value.
NPV is subject to the law of diminishing returns — a company cannot sell unlimited policies with positive NPV as sales costs rise as the market saturates.
NPV is typically expressed as a percentage of initial commission (reflecting sales effort) or as a percentage of the present value of future premiums (reflecting market share).
NPV says nothing about competition — there is no point designing a high-NPV contract that cannot be sold.

key_terms.definition: The discounted value of the profit signature at the risk discount rate. The theoretically optimal profit criterion.

exam_qa.q: How is NPV expressed to make it useful for comparing products?
exam_qa.a: Either as a percentage of initial commission (measuring profit relative to sales effort) or as a percentage of the present value of future premiums (measuring profit relative to market share). A positive NPV indicates the product meets the risk discount rate target.

exam_qa.q: What are the drawbacks of NPV as a profit criterion?
exam_qa.a: It is subject to the law of diminishing returns; it says nothing about competition; comparisons are only valid if capital markets are perfectly free and efficient and the risk discount rate correctly reflects the riskiness of each investment.

subsections.title: Internal Rate of Return (IRR) and Discounted Payback Period (DPP)
bullets: IRR is the rate at which the discounted value of cashflows is zero. A higher IRR is generally preferred, but IRR does not always agree with NPV.

Disadvantages of IRR vs NPV: may not be unique if the profit signature changes sign more than once; cannot be related to sales effort or market share; may not exist if profits are made from the outset.
IRR is easier to understand conceptually but is not more informative than NPV in most cases.
DPP is the duration at which cumulative discounted profits equal zero — the time to recover the initial capital investment at the risk discount rate.
DPP ignores all cashflows after the payback period, so will not agree with NPV. It is useful for capital-constrained companies.
Common approach: use NPV (expressed as % of initial sales costs) as the primary criterion, with DPP as a secondary reference. DPP is more useful for product design than pricing.

key_terms.definition: The discount rate at which the NPV of the profit signature equals zero. Higher IRR is preferred, but has disadvantages vs NPV.
The policy duration at which the accumulated discounted profits equal zero. Useful for capital-constrained companies but ignores later cashflows.

exam_qa.q: Why might IRR not be a unique value?
exam_qa.a: If the profit signature changes sign more than once (i.e. profits alternate between positive and negative), there may be multiple discount rates at which NPV equals zero, so IRR is not unique.

subsections.title: Marketability
bullets: Premiums produced by the pricing model must be assessed for marketability. This may lead to reconsideration of: the product design; the distribution channel; the company's profit requirement; whether to proceed at all.
Re-examination of assumed expenses is also required, notably acquisition costs (commission), marginal administration costs, and contribution to fixed overheads.

subsections.title: Competitiveness
bullets: A close watch must be kept on competitors' pricing. Significant discrepancies should be investigated, though differences in target markets may explain them.
If a competitor uses more detailed risk differentiation, better risks migrate to the competitor — the insurer’s averaged pricing assumptions are called into question (anti-selection).
Two factors: (1) overall premium level vs competitors; (2) number and accuracy of rating factors used.
Fixed expenses are spread over volume — lower-than-expected volume increases per-policy expense loadings and reduces competitiveness (economy of scale principle).
Loss leaders: products priced below the full premium to gain market share, compensated by higher margins on other lines. Risk: adverse business mix if loss-leader sells more than high-margin lines.
Over time, each product should be brought back in line to produce its fair contribution to profits.
Return on capital: each profit centre must meet its return on capital target. Excess return suggests pricing can be relaxed to promote volume; insufficient return is grounds for withdrawal.

key_terms.definition: A product priced below its full expected cost to gain market share, with the shortfall compensated by margins on other product lines.
Any collection of business items with a separate target for contribution to insurer profit, ranging from a broad business line to a very specific segment.

exam_qa.q: What are the two factors involved in the competitive impact on pricing?
exam_qa.a: (1) The overall level of premiums compared with competitors; (2) the number and accuracy of rating factors used. Using fewer rating factors than competitors creates anti-selection as better risks go elsewhere.

sections.title: Reserving, Solvency Capital and Reinsurance Impact

subsections.title: Cost of Reserving and Solvency Capital
bullets: Writing a policy creates a need for regulatory reserves and solvency capital. Each policy should be charged for the cost of this capital.

The charge is the investment cost of holding assets as locked-in statutory capital rather than deploying them freely. Simplistically: if IRR is 10% and statutory assets yield 4%, the cost is 6% p.a. of the solvency capital requirement.

Reserving assumptions in the pricing model should match those the insurer realistically expects to use (prudent if regulations require prudent reserves; best estimate if regulations require best estimate reserves).
Solvency capital requirements may be reduced by suitable reinsurance — reinsurers may hold less capital per unit of risk, with cost savings potentially passed to consumers.

key_terms.definition: The charge in pricing for the investment return foregone by holding assets as locked-in statutory capital rather than deploying them freely.

exam_qa.q: Should reserving assumptions in the pricing model be best estimate or prudent?
exam_qa.a: They should match the assumptions the insurer realistically expects to use in practice under regulation — prudent if the regulatory regime requires prudent reserves, best estimate if the regime requires best estimate reserves. The pricing model must also allow for any solvency capital requirements on top of reserves.

subsections.title: Reinsurance Impact on Pricing
bullets: Reinsurance roles in pricing: technical assistance/data for new products; risk sharing; smoothing profitability; financing new business strain; tax arbitrage; solvency capital arbitrage; enabling acceptance of larger risks.
Unless arbitrage is possible, the insurer will expect a net loss from reinsurance (premiums paid > expected recoveries), which must be included in the pricing model.
Where financial benefits of reinsurance exceed costs (e.g. tax arbitrage), pricing net of reinsurance can improve competitiveness.
Under quota share arrangements, the reinsurer may require the right to vary commission if the insurer changes its premium rates.

exam_qa.q: Why does reinsurance usually have a net cost to the insurer?
exam_qa.a: The reinsurer must cover its own expenses and profit, so reinsurance premiums will generally exceed the expected present value of reinsurance benefits. This net cost must be included in the pricing model unless some arbitrage (e.g. tax or capital) makes the arrangement financially beneficial.

subsections.title: Regulatory Impact on Pricing
bullets: Pricing basis may be prescribed by the regulator (e.g. community rating) or certain rating factors restricted (e.g. gender, genetic test results).
Regulatory restrictions on operations (e.g. investment constraints, requirement to collect premium taxes) impose costs that must be reflected in pricing assumptions.
Such additions to costs affect all insurers equally and so do not alter competitive positions.

key_terms.definition: A regulatory requirement to charge all policyholders the same premium regardless of individual risk characteristics.

subsections.title: Monitoring and Reviewing Premium Rates
bullets: All items in the assumption set must be kept under review. When any assumption is no longer valid, its impact must be tested against the existing tariff before deciding whether to reprice. Changing prices incurs expense and may affect marketability.
For reviewable premium business: the actuary estimates premiums subject to periodic review. Margins are smaller because adverse experience can be corrected at the next review.
Risk of selective lapsing on rate increase: healthier lives are more likely to lapse if premiums rise significantly, leaving a worse risk pool. The rate increase must account for this risk.

In some territories, regulators specify what aspects of the basis can justify a premium review (e.g. in the UK, premiums can only increase for 'valid reason' related to general portfolio experience, not individual experience).
Where premiums are inadequate (e.g. loss leaders), reserves must be established. Their size depends on the speed of premium review and the likelihood of renewal as rates rise.

key_terms.definition: The tendency for healthier lives to lapse when premiums are increased, leaving the remaining pool with worse-than-average risk.

exam_qa.q: Why do reviewable premium products include smaller margins than guaranteed premium products?
exam_qa.a: Because any significant deterioration in future experience can be corrected at the next premium review. Guaranteed premium products cannot be repriced, so they need much larger margins to cover possible adverse experience over the full policy term.

exam_qa.q: Why must actuaries be careful not to increase reviewable premiums by too much?
exam_qa.a: A large increase encourages selective lapsing — healthier lives leave, leaving a worse risk pool. This can worsen the insurer's position, as the per-policy expense cost also rises on a smaller portfolio, potentially negating the benefit of the rate increase.

subsections.title: Nature and Terms of Health Options
bullets: Mortality/morbidity options (health options) allow policyholders to extend term or increase cover at normal premium rates without providing further medical evidence.

Common examples: option to purchase additional cover without evidence of health at normal rates; option to renew a CI policy at term end without further evidence; buy-back option to reinstate mortality cover after an accelerated CI claim.
Terms and conditions are designed to reduce anti-selection risk. Options may only be exercisable at fixed points (e.g. every 5 years) or on qualifying life events (e.g. marriage, new job).
For IP options, the policyholder must not be currently claiming or within a deferred/linked-claim period when exercising the option. Someone who has recovered may be eligible again.
Unlike life options (exercisable provided alive), health options cannot be exercised while sick — but are still valuable to those who have deteriorating health but are not yet claiming.

key_terms.definition: A policy feature allowing the policyholder to extend or increase cover at standard rates without further medical underwriting.
An option to reinstate the mortality cover under an accelerated CI policy after a CI claim has been paid, without fresh underwriting.

exam_qa.q: Why are health options valuable even if they cannot be exercised while the policyholder is sick?
exam_qa.a: There is a significant category of policyholders who are not yet sick enough to claim but have already developed conditions that increase their future risk (e.g. hypertension). These lives benefit most from exercising options at standard rates, since they would face rated or declined terms if underwritten afresh.

subsections.title: The Cost of Options
bullets: The cost of an option is the value of the excess of the premium that should be charged (given full underwriting information) over the normal premium rate that is actually charged.
The total cost depends on: (1) the health status of those who exercise the option; (2) the proportion of eligible lives who choose to exercise it.
In general, the smaller the proportion exercising the option, the worse the average health of those who do exercise it.
Cost of option ≈ proportion of lives exercising option × average extent that their health is worse than select.
If all eligible policyholders exercise the option (including healthy ones), the insurer is better off because healthy lives pay appropriate premiums, generating additional profit.
The value of options must be kept under constant review — a widespread disease outbreak could cause option costs to far exceed original pricing assumptions.

exam_qa.q: Is the insurer better off if all policyholders exercise the option, or only the unhealthy ones?
exam_qa.a: Better off if all policyholders exercise it. Healthy policyholders pay premiums appropriate to their select morbidity, generating additional profits. If only unhealthy lives exercise the option, the insurer bears the full anti-selection cost with no offsetting income from healthy lives.

subsections.title: Valuation Method and Assumptions
bullets: Options are normally valued using cashflow projections including: additional benefits expected under the option; additional premiums received (based on standard rates for a select life at the option date); any additional expenses (e.g. reminder letters, processing costs).
For pricing purposes, the valuation must also allow for additional reserves held before and after exercise.

Key assumptions required: probability that the option is exercised at each possible date; additional benefit level chosen; expected morbidity of lives who exercise vs those who do not; additional expenses.
Conventional method: assume all eligible policyholders take up the option at the maximum additional benefit. Simple but unrealistic; likely to overstate cost.
North American method: use more realistic take-up rate assumptions varying by exercise date and alternative option. More accurate but requires data (historically available only in North America).

Typically, the expected morbidity of lives who take up the option is heavier than those who do not (due to anti-selection). This can be expressed as a percentage loading on the base table (e.g. 150%) or an age addition (e.g. age x experiences mortality of age x+5).

A consistent set of assumptions is needed: the average mortality of all lives (exercisers and non-exercisers) should remain at the base expected level. If exercisers are assumed heavier, non-exercisers must be assumed lighter to maintain the average.
Stochastic modelling can be used to establish the core cost of options, simulating the distribution of take-up rates and subsequent morbidity across many scenarios.

key_terms.definition: Values options by assuming all eligible policyholders take up the option at the maximum benefit. Simple but tends to overstate cost.
Values options using realistic take-up rate assumptions based on observed experience. More accurate but requires credible data.

exam_qa.q: Why does a policy with a morbidity option need higher reserves than the equivalent policy without?
exam_qa.a: The option increases morbidity risk — an option to extend term increases the exposure period, an option to increase sum insured increases the amount at risk. Reserves must increase for expected additional claims and for the uncertainty in those claims. Before exercise, the insurer faces anti-selection risk; after exercise, it holds lives of unknown health without underwriting evidence.

exam_qa.q: Why must the morbidity assumption for non-exercisers be set lower than select if exercisers are assumed heavier?
exam_qa.a: The average morbidity across all lives (exercisers and non-exercisers) must equal the base expected level. If exercisers are assumed heavier (e.g. ultimate + 10 years), non-exercisers must be lighter than ultimate to maintain the average. Failing to do this would implicitly assume the overall portfolio is worse than expected.

subsections.title: Pricing and Reserving for Guarantees
bullets: Guaranteed premium rates must be assessed on a resilience basis: will the premium be sufficient to meet all outgoings under a range of adverse scenarios, with an agreed level of confidence?
The actuary must incorporate into the premium a loading for the additional cost of providing the guarantee, including the cost of holding guarantee reserves.
If the true cost of guarantees is reflected in premiums, it may encourage policyholders to prefer reviewable contracts (same benefits, lower premium because no guarantee cost).

Conventional (without-profits) contracts transfer mortality/morbidity, expense and investment risk from policyholder to insurer. Health and care contracts rarely include financially onerous investment guarantees because reserves are small relative to benefit amounts.
Exception: pre-funded LTCI, where large reserves accumulate and investment risk is material if the contract is written on fully guaranteed terms.
The local regulator will normally want to review the techniques and outputs used to justify the adequacy of guaranteed premiums and reserves.

key_terms.definition: Assessment of whether premium rates remain sufficient under a range of adverse scenarios, required when pricing guaranteed contracts.
An additional reserve held to cover the expected cost of providing a premium or benefit guarantee under a long-term contract.

exam_qa.q: Why do most health and care contracts not pose onerous investment guarantees on insurers?
exam_qa.a: Most health and care products are protection rather than savings contracts. Policy reserves are usually small relative to benefit amounts, so even significant underperformance of investment returns has little financial impact on the insurer. The main risk is claim incidence, not investment returns.

exam_qa.q: What is the risk of fully reflecting the guarantee cost in the premium?
exam_qa.a: It may make the guaranteed product significantly more expensive than an equivalent reviewable product. Policyholders may then opt for the reviewable contract, undermining sales volumes for the guaranteed product.

subsections.title: Reasons for Calculating Reserves
bullets: Reserves are calculated for multiple purposes: published accounts; supervisory solvency accounts; internal management accounts; estimating future premium requirements; merger/acquisition valuation; influencing investment strategy; assessing reinsurance arrangements.

The choice of assumptions depends on the purpose: published accounts use true-and-fair (best estimate or slightly prudent); supervisory accounts use the most prudent basis to protect policyholders (or best estimate with explicit risk margin); internal accounts use best estimate for a realistic view; pricing uses best estimate (possibly slightly prudent); M&A uses best estimate (optimistic for seller, prudent for purchaser); investment strategy and reinsurance assessment use best estimate.

key_terms.definition: The liabilities held by an insurer to meet its obligations to policyholders, calculated on a basis appropriate to the purpose of the valuation.

exam_qa.q: Why does the reserving basis differ depending on the purpose of the calculation?
exam_qa.a: Different purposes require different levels of prudence. Supervisory reserves protect policyholders and require the most prudent basis. Published accounts require a true-and-fair view. Internal management accounts require best estimates for realistic decision-making. M&A valuations reflect the interests of buyer vs seller. The basis should match the purpose for which results will be used.

subsections.title: Long-Term Insurance Reserves
bullets: Reserves for policies: the discounted present value of future expected claims + expenses − future premiums. The main reserve for long-term contracts, held when future outgo exceeds future income.

IBNR (Incurred But Not Reported): reserves for claims that have occurred but not yet been notified. Increasingly common in CI insurance as policyholders claim retrospectively upon discovering they had cover for a condition (often via brokers).
Reserves for reported but not yet settled claims: e.g. IP claims in payment or CI claims still in assessment.
Option reserves: additional reserves for options that may 'come into the money', e.g. guaranteed conversion options on CI policies.
Investment mismatching reserve: required in some territories to cover the extent to which assets do not match liabilities.

For a healthy IP policyholder: hold policy reserve + option reserve. If sick in deferred period (unreported): add IBNR. If reported but in deferred period: add reserve for expected cost allowing for probability of surviving through DP. If claiming: add reserve for current claim annuity value of benefits in payment.

key_terms.definition: Incurred But Not Reported — a reserve for claims that have occurred but have not yet been notified to the insurer.
An additional reserve held for the expected cost of options that policyholders may exercise, such as guaranteed conversion options on CI policies.
Alternative term for the policy reserve held for lives that are healthy and not yet claiming (prospective premium reserve).
Alternative term for the claims reserve held for lives currently receiving benefit payments.

exam_qa.q: Why is IBNR becoming increasingly common in CI insurance?
exam_qa.a: Policyholders sometimes do not realise they have cover for certain conditions (as the number of covered conditions has grown dramatically). They later discover they were covered — often through brokers — and lodge retrospective claims. Improved medical screening also means conditions are now being identified that policyholders were previously unaware of.

subsections.title: Short-Term Insurance Reserves
bullets: Short-term (annually renewable) contracts do not normally require reserves beyond the next renewal date, but must hold reserves for claims arising in the unexpired period.

Unearned Premium Reserve (UPR): the retrospective approach — the balance of premiums received in respect of the unexpired period of cover. Calculated as the proportion of the risk-related premium (excluding initial expenses) attributable to the unexpired risk period.

Unexpired Risk Reserve (URR): the prospective approach — the total amount needed to cover expected claims and expenses for the unexpired risk period. URR > UPR when premiums are inadequate. The Additional Unexpired Risk Reserve (AURR) is the excess of URR over UPR.
IBNR: also required for short-term business for claims incurred but not yet reported.
Claims in transit: claims reported but not yet assessed or recorded ('pipeline claims').
Outstanding claims reserve: claims notified but not yet fully settled.

IBNER (Incurred But Not Enough Reported): adjustment (positive or negative) to outstanding claims reserve where case estimates are believed to be inaccurate. Defined separately from outstanding claims for management information and potentially different tax treatment.
Equalisation/catastrophe reserves: amounts held back from profitable years for use in loss-making years (equalisation) or for potential future catastrophes. Regulation varies by territory.

key_terms.definition: The balance of premiums received in respect of the unexpired period of insurance. A retrospective measure of the amount of premium not yet 'earned'.
The prospective estimate of amounts needed to cover future claims and expenses for the unexpired risk period. Exceeds UPR when premiums are inadequate.
Incurred But Not Enough Reported — an adjustment to the outstanding claims reserve to reflect under- or over-estimation of case estimates.
Amounts held back in profitable years to smooth results in less profitable years. May receive favourable tax treatment depending on territory.

exam_qa.q: What is the difference between UPR and URR?
exam_qa.a: UPR is retrospective — it is the proportion of premiums already received that relates to the unexpired period of cover. URR is prospective — it is the actual amount needed to cover future claims and expenses for the unexpired period. The URR exceeds UPR when premiums are inadequate, with the excess being the AURR.

exam_qa.q: Why is IBNER defined separately from the outstanding claims reserve?
exam_qa.a: It provides more information to management for better decision-making; IBNER may show different trends from other reserves, making separate modelling valuable; and IBNER may receive different tax treatment from the main outstanding claims reserve.

subsections.title: Case Estimates
bullets: Case-by-case method: an experienced claims manager inspects each claim individually and estimates the ultimate outgo. Straightforward for lump-sum claims; requires assessment of likely duration for income-based claims.

For PMI case estimates, the assessor considers: procedure type; hospital to be used; surgeon/consultant; policy coverage (excess, limits, etc.); age, gender and past claims history; current medical inflation levels.
Advantages: uses all known data; allows qualitative judgement; may be the only option when statistical models are not reliable.

Disadvantages: relies on individual skill/judgement (bias risk); extremely difficult to check; very expensive for large portfolios; inconsistent inflation assumptions; cannot estimate IBNR (unreported claims must be estimated separately).
Case estimates cannot be used for IBNR — unreported claims must be estimated using statistical methods.

key_terms.definition: An individual assessment of the expected ultimate cost of a specific outstanding claim, performed by an experienced claims manager.

exam_qa.q: When is case estimation more suitable than statistical estimation?
exam_qa.a: When there is sufficient qualitative data per claim and an experienced assessment team; when there is insufficient historical data for statistical methods; when claims are highly heterogeneous; for new classes of business; when there is high variance in claim amounts; or when no appropriate statistical model exists.

subsections.title: Statistical Estimates
bullets: Statistical estimation: appropriate for homogeneous claims where the portfolio is large enough and experience is stable. Outstanding claims are assessed in aggregate using historical trends and patterns, adjusted for known future changes.
The portfolio is segmented by contract type, distribution type or region. A statistical distribution is fitted to past experience to estimate claims from earned premium.
Statistical methods include run-off triangles (chain ladder technique). These can automatically incorporate IBNR if historical data includes incurred claims on a consistent basis.
For long-term income contracts (IP, LTCI): statistical methods are standard; case estimates only used for very small portfolios. Most reserves relate to future claims on in-force policies.
For PMI: statistical estimation is the primary approach, but large or unusual individual claims are valued separately using case estimates due to their potential to distort average costs.

key_terms.definition: Aggregate assessment of outstanding claims using historical patterns and statistical models, suitable for large homogeneous portfolios.
A statistical reserving technique using run-off triangles to project ultimate claims costs based on historical development patterns.

subsections.title: Definition, Purpose and Calculation
bullets: Embedded Value (EV): the present value of future shareholder profits from existing business, including the release of shareholder-owned net assets. Most relevant to long-term insurance business.

EV is used to: establish business value for internal/published accounts; assess the major part of appraisal value for sale/purchase; analyse future surpluses for reinsurance EV financing; assess growth in EV for staff/salesperson bonuses.

EV has two components: (1) shareholders' share of net assets (excess of assets over liabilities, discounted if 'locked in' as solvency capital); (2) present value of future shareholder profits (PVFP) from existing in-force business.
Net assets held as locked-in solvency capital earn a constrained return (e.g. 4%) rather than the risk discount rate (e.g. 5%), so are included in EV at their discounted value, not full market value.
PVFP arises because EV projections use realistic assumptions, while supervisory reserves use prudent assumptions. The difference between the two bases generates future profits as margins unwind.
Appraisal value = EV + goodwill, where goodwill represents the estimated present value of profits from future new business. Used as the starting point for M&A valuation.

key_terms.definition: The present value of future shareholder profits from existing in-force business, plus the shareholders' share of net assets.
Embedded value plus goodwill (estimated present value of future new business profits). Used in merger and acquisition valuations.
Present Value of Future Profits — the component of EV representing the discounted value of expected future shareholder profits from in-force business.

exam_qa.q: Why does PVFP arise if the EV basis and reserving basis use the same assumptions?
exam_qa.a: If both bases used identical assumptions, future profits would be zero — net cashflows plus investment income on reserves would exactly equal the reserve release each year. PVFP arises because the EV basis is realistic (best estimate) while the supervisory reserving basis is prudent. The margins in the supervisory reserves are released as profit as policies run off.

exam_qa.q: Why might locked-in solvency capital be included in EV at less than market value?
exam_qa.a: Solvency capital must be invested cautiously to ensure regulatory requirements are met, earning a lower return (e.g. 4%) than shareholders' required risk discount rate (e.g. 5%). The capital is therefore worth less to shareholders than its market value, so it is discounted at the risk discount rate.

22.title: Approaches to Setting Reserves and Solvency Capital Requirements

subsections.title: Purpose and Formula Approaches
bullets: Solvency capital provides an additional layer of protection to policyholders beyond the technical reserves, protecting against: reserves being underestimated (adverse future experience); a drop in asset values (including individual asset defaults).
The level of required solvency capital may be specified as a simple formula (e.g. 3% of reserves + 0.3% of sum at risk) or based on a risk measure such as Value-at-Risk (VaR).
Available capital = excess of assets over liabilities. The insurer must hold solvency capital such that available capital exceeds required capital. Any excess is free capital (free surplus).

Changing the reserving basis does not affect the true financial position but changes the disclosed result. Indirect effects are possible (e.g. an over-cautious basis causing a change in investment policy).

key_terms.definition: The excess of the insurer's assets over its liabilities (technical reserves).
The minimum solvency capital the insurer must hold in addition to reserves, as determined by regulation.
The excess of available capital over required capital — capital that is not tied up in reserves or solvency requirements.

subsections.title: Interplay Between Reserves and Solvency Capital
bullets: Reserves and solvency capital must be assessed together, not in isolation. The appropriate balance between the two varies by regulatory jurisdiction.
Some jurisdictions use relatively realistic reserves (small margins) but require substantial risk-based solvency capital — the risk is explicitly captured in the capital requirement.
Other jurisdictions use relatively prudent reserves (large margins) but require only a small, formula-based solvency capital requirement.
In both approaches, the overall level of policyholder protection should be broadly equivalent.

exam_qa.q: How does the interplay between reserves and solvency capital differ between regulatory jurisdictions?
exam_qa.a: Some jurisdictions use realistic (best estimate) reserves with a substantial risk-based solvency capital requirement (e.g. VaR approach under Solvency II). Others use prudent reserves with a small formula-based solvency capital requirement. The two approaches achieve broadly equivalent policyholder protection through different mechanisms.

subsections.title: Value-at-Risk (VaR) Approach
bullets: VaR: the capital required so that assets exceed liabilities with a specified confidence level (e.g. 99.5%) over a defined period (e.g. one year). A 99.5% VaR is effectively a 1-in-200 year stress.

The supervisory balance sheet (on a market-consistent basis) is stressed by adverse shocks to each risk factor. The required capital is the amount needed at the start so that assets still cover liabilities after the shock.

Stress tests are calculated separately per risk factor (e.g. morbidity, expenses, lapses). Capital requirements are then aggregated using a correlation matrix to reflect diversification between risks.
Aggregated capital = sqrt(sum of Corr_ij × Cap_i × Cap_j across all i,j). Diversification means the aggregate capital is less than the simple sum of individual capital requirements.

Non-linearity: the capital required may not scale linearly with the risk driver (e.g. interest rate guarantees that suddenly bite). Non-separability: two risks occurring together may cause more damage than each alone (e.g. longevity + expense risk for annuities). Both effects generally increase capital requirements beyond what correlation matrices suggest.

Stochastic (real-world) models are used to quantify economic capital requirements. The tail of the distribution must be accurately modelled, not just the mean — this is particularly challenging as a 1-in-200 event requires extrapolation beyond most historical datasets.
An alternative is the run-off method: capital required at outset to ensure assets exceed liabilities throughout the full run-off of existing business under appropriate stresses.

key_terms.definition: The amount of capital needed so that losses do not exceed a given level with a specified probability (e.g. 99.5%) over a defined period (e.g. one year).
A matrix of pairwise correlations between risk factors used to aggregate individual capital requirements, reflecting diversification benefits.
The property where the capital required is not a linear function of the risk driver — e.g. a guaranteed annuity rate that suddenly triggers at a certain interest rate level.
The property where two risks occurring together cause more damage than each occurring individually — e.g. longevity + expense risk for annuities.

exam_qa.q: Why is the aggregated capital requirement less than the sum of individual capital requirements?
exam_qa.a: Because the individual risks are not perfectly correlated — it is unlikely that all adverse shocks occur simultaneously. The correlation matrix reflects the degree to which risks move together, and diversification between risks means the insurer can hold less capital in aggregate than the sum of the individual requirements.

exam_qa.q: Why might the correlation matrix approach understate capital requirements?
exam_qa.a: Due to non-linearity (the capital required may be disproportionately large at extreme stress levels, e.g. where guarantees bite) and non-separability (two risks together may cause more damage than each separately, an interaction not captured in simple correlation). Both effects generally lead to higher capital needs than the matrix suggests, requiring upward adjustments.

subsections.title: Market-Consistent Methodology
bullets: A market-consistent value of a liability is, in theory, the price a third party would charge to assume responsibility for it in a freely traded market. In practice, this market rarely exists so an approximate approach is used.
Future cashflows are projected using best-estimate assumptions and discounted at current risk-free rates (government bond yields or swap rates), irrespective of the assets actually held.
The liability value equals the current market price of a replicating portfolio of risk-free assets whose cashflows exactly match the expected liability cashflows.
If the yield curve is not flat, different risk-free rates are used for cashflows at different durations.

Swap rates may be used instead of government bond yields if the swap market is sufficiently deep and liquid. Both may be adjusted for credit risk (swaps carry counterparty default risk; some government bonds are not fully risk-free).
Market-consistent valuation is increasingly used in some jurisdictions (e.g. Solvency II in the EU).

key_terms.definition: A valuation approach where liabilities are discounted at risk-free rates, with assumptions set consistently with market prices. Equivalent to pricing the replicating portfolio of risk-free assets.
A portfolio of assets whose cashflows exactly replicate those of the liabilities. Its current market price gives the market-consistent value of the liabilities.

subsections.title: Illiquidity Premium and Risk Margin
bullets: Corporate bonds yield more than government bonds, reflecting: (1) default risk premium; (2) illiquidity premium (compensation for greater price volatility / lower liquidity).

In some jurisdictions, insurers can include an illiquidity premium in the market-consistent discount rate for long-term predictable liabilities matched by bonds held to maturity — because they are not exposed to price volatility risk if they hold to maturity.
Example: government bond yield 4%, corporate bond yield 7% = 2% default risk + 1% illiquidity. Market-consistent discount rate may be set at 5% (4% + 1% illiquidity premium).
The illiquidity premium can only be used for long-term predictable liabilities (e.g. immediate needs LTCI). It cannot be used where withdrawal risk means assets may need to be sold before maturity.

Risk margin: for uncertain cashflows, projecting expected amounts and discounting at risk-free rates gives a value lower than the true market-consistent value (because a risk-averse purchaser requires extra compensation). A risk margin is added.

Risk margin can be calculated using the cost of capital approach: project required capital at each future point; multiply by cost of capital rate (e.g. 6% p.a.); discount at risk-free rates. Simplified proxy models (using reserves or sum at risk as drivers) are often used to project future capital.

key_terms.definition: The extra yield on corporate bonds over government bonds attributable to their lower liquidity (price volatility), as distinct from the default risk premium.

An addition to the best-estimate liability to reflect the compensation a third party would require for taking on the uncertainty in the liability cashflows. Can be calculated using the cost of capital approach.
A method of calculating the risk margin by projecting future capital requirements, multiplying by the cost of capital rate, and discounting at risk-free rates.

exam_qa.q: Why can the illiquidity premium only be applied to long-term predictable liabilities?
exam_qa.a: If the insurer holds bonds to maturity to match these liabilities, it is not exposed to price volatility risk — the illiquidity premium is therefore a genuine risk-free additional return. But for liabilities with withdrawal risk (e.g. pre-funded LTCI with surrender values), the insurer may need to sell bonds before maturity, exposing it to price volatility. In this case, the illiquidity premium is not a risk-free return and cannot be included.

exam_qa.q: How is the risk margin calculated under the cost of capital approach?
exam_qa.a: Step 1: project the required capital at each future time period throughout the run-off of the business. Step 2: multiply the projected capital at each period by the cost of capital rate (e.g. 6% p.a.). Step 3: discount these charges at market-consistent (risk-free) rates. The sum is the risk margin. Simplified proxy models are often used to project future capital requirements.

subsections.title: Passive Valuation Approach
bullets: A passive valuation approach uses a methodology that is relatively insensitive to market conditions, with assumptions updated infrequently.

Example for liabilities: net premium valuation — the net premium is recalculated under new assumptions, so changes in the valuation interest rate have a smaller impact than under a gross premium valuation (the premium and benefit valuations move in a more offsetting way).

Assumptions may be 'locked in' at policy inception and remain unchanged throughout the term. Non-economic assumptions (e.g. morbidity) may still need to be updated if experience worsens, as deterioration in claims does not produce a corresponding change in asset values.
Example for assets: historic cost ('book value'), possibly with amortisation. Ignores changes in market prices.
Solvency capital may be set as a simple prescribed percentage of best estimate liabilities.
Advantages: straightforward to implement; less subjectivity; more stable profit emergence.
Disadvantages: at risk of becoming out of date; may not reflect true solvency; can give false sense of security — management may not react to emerging problems until too late.

key_terms.definition: A reserving approach relatively insensitive to market conditions, with infrequently updated assumptions. Example: net premium valuation with locked-in interest rates.
Valuation assumptions fixed at policy inception and unchanged throughout the policy term, regardless of changes in market conditions.
A reserving method using a net premium (calculated on the valuation basis, not the actual office premium) that partially offsets the impact of interest rate changes on the liability value.

exam_qa.q: Why is a net premium valuation relatively insensitive to changes in the valuation interest rate?
exam_qa.a: Under a gross premium valuation, a fall in interest rates increases both the PV of benefits and PV of premiums, but the benefit increase dominates (benefits are paid later and discounted for longer), so reserves rise. Under a net premium valuation, the net premium is also recalculated at the new interest rate — a lower rate increases the net premium, so the PV of premiums rises more than under a gross premium approach, partially offsetting the increase in benefit PV. The net reserve is therefore less sensitive to interest rate changes.

subsections.title: Active Valuation Approach and Combinations
bullets: An active valuation approach is based closely on current market conditions, with assumptions updated frequently. Example: market-consistent valuation of both assets and liabilities, combined with risk-based capital (VaR approach).
Advantages: more informative about the true impact of market conditions on solvency; better reflects financial guarantees and options.

Disadvantages: results are more volatile — in an equity market crash, an active approach would require higher capital, forcing insurers to sell equities, which exacerbates market conditions (systemic risk). This occurred in the 2007/2008 financial crisis. Active approaches are also more complex and costly.
Regulators may introduce amendments to valuation approaches under extreme market conditions to prevent pro-cyclical behaviour.

Combination approaches (e.g. market value of assets with net premium valuation of liabilities) can create greater apparent volatility than either pure approach, even when the true solvency position has not changed — because assets and liabilities do not move consistently with market conditions.

If assets and liabilities are well matched, both active and passive approaches show little change in solvency under market movements. The combination approach can show dramatic apparent changes that do not reflect reality.

key_terms.definition: A reserving approach closely linked to current market conditions, with frequently updated assumptions. Example: market-consistent valuation + risk-based capital.
The risk that actions taken by multiple institutions simultaneously (e.g. all selling equities to restore solvency) amplify market stress and worsen the situation for all.
A tendency for regulatory capital requirements to increase in downturns (when capital is hardest to raise), potentially amplifying economic stress.

exam_qa.q: What is the key disadvantage of an active valuation approach?
exam_qa.a: Results are more volatile. Under adverse market conditions (e.g. a stock market crash), required capital rises under a risk-based approach, forcing insurers to sell risky assets to restore solvency. As all insurers do this simultaneously, asset prices fall further, worsening the situation for everyone (systemic and pro-cyclical risk). Regulators may need to modify valuation rules under extreme conditions to prevent this.

exam_qa.q: Why can combining active asset valuation with passive liability valuation cause apparent volatility in solvency?
exam_qa.a: If assets are valued at market value (active) but liabilities use a net premium valuation (passive), a change in interest rates will alter the market value of assets but have little effect on the net premium liability value. The solvency position will appear to change significantly even though the true economic position (with well-matched assets and liabilities) has barely moved.

subsections.title: Policy Data
bullets: An insurer's policy database records items such as age at entry, date of entry, policy term, and date of diagnosis. High-quality policy data is vital for the good management of an insurance company.

Policy data is used to carry out regular valuations of liabilities. Because the actuary typically does not directly control this data, there is a risk it will be inadequate, inaccurate, or incomplete — leading to incorrect valuation results, potentially declaring solvency when the insurer is actually insolvent, or vice versa.

Two distinct aspects of data risk: (i) Data risk — the insurer's underlying policy data may itself be inaccurate, inadequate or incomplete; (ii) Model (modelling) risk — where model points are used to represent the in-force portfolio, there is a risk those model points do not adequately capture the characteristics of the actual book, causing results to behave unrealistically.
Even less-serious data errors can cause incorrect business decisions — for example, underestimating the capital available to fund new business.

The actuary should carry out checks on data quality and must at a minimum be aware of any data problems. Incorrect advice arising from bad data is potentially very serious for both the insurer and the actuary.

key_terms.definition: The risk that an insurer's policy or other data are inaccurate, inadequate or incomplete, causing actuarial analyses relying on those data to be similarly flawed.
The risk that a model (e.g. a set of model points representing in-force business) is an over-simplification of reality, causing results to be unrealistic or misleading.
Representative policies used to stand in for all or part of the in-force business in actuarial calculations; their adequacy in reflecting the actual portfolio is itself a source of risk.

exam_qa.q: Explain how policy and other data relating to mortality and critical illness benefits may be a source of risk to an insurer launching a new CI product.
exam_qa.a:
Policy data — CI: The insurer has no own policy data for the CI benefit. Mortality data may exist but poses risk if: data are inaccurate/incomplete; volume is insufficient for credibility; underwriting procedures have changed; the new CI benefit attracts a different market.
Other data — The insurer will need to rely on reinsurer or industry data for CI. This may be unreliable, incomplete, or unrepresentative due to: varying CI definitions across insurers; different target markets; different underwriting and claims management standards. Even after adjustment there remains a major risk of incorrect assumptions, especially for CI where there is no own data. External data may also become inappropriate in future due to changes such as new screening programmes.

subsections.title: Other Data
bullets: Other data useful for actuarial investigations includes asset holdings and external data (e.g. population statistics, industry data, overseas markets data) needed to determine morbidity and mortality rates.

Internal data may be inadequate due to missing records, inaccuracy, or insufficient volume for credibility. Even high-quality internal data may be unsuitable as future assumptions if it relates to an inappropriate class of lives.

External data carries the specific risk of being unrepresentative: population data, overseas data, and even insured data from other companies may reflect different policy conditions, underwriting, and claims procedures — increasing parameter risk when applied to the actuary's own company.

Example of inapplicability: using long-term care expense data as the expense assumption for a new CI product is inappropriate because underwriting depth, claims management, commission structures, distribution mix, and business volumes all differ between the two product types — leading to different per-policy costs.

key_terms.definition: The risk that the parameters (e.g. rates, probabilities, factors) chosen for an actuarial model do not adequately reflect the future experience of the group being modelled, even if the structure of the model is appropriate.

The concept that uncertainty in a forecast parameter grows the further into the future the projection extends, because new diseases, medical advances, and other unpredictable events cannot be ruled out.

subsections.title: Claim Rates — Model, Parameter and Random Fluctuations Risk
bullets: Three risks attach to claim rate (morbidity/mortality) assumptions: (i) Model risk — the chosen probability distribution may be inappropriate or contain errors; (ii) Parameter risk — the parameters of an otherwise correct model may not reflect future experience of the lives insured; (iii) Random fluctuations risk — even with a correct model and parameters, actual experience deviates from expectation due to heterogeneity of lives and insufficient numbers for the law of large numbers to apply.

These three risk types are mutually non-exclusive and their relative importance is not always clear-cut. For example, if mortality is rising over time the parameters will be wrong, but the root cause may be that the model has failed to allow for future mortality improvements — blurring the line between model and parameter risk.

The main risk is that mortality or morbidity turns out to be more adverse than assumed. The likelihood of this depends on the quality and relevance of available data: well-established products with stable, predictable experience and abundant data carry low risk; brand-new product types requiring intelligent guesswork carry high risk.

There will always be an expanding funnel of doubt — uncertainty increases the further into the future a projection goes, however good the experience data. New diseases or sudden advances in medical treatment can never be ruled out.

Random fluctuations risk is reduced — but never eliminated — by larger portfolio size. Heterogeneity also arises where the model is overly simplistic (e.g. using only age as a rating factor when claim rates also vary by gender and smoker status), which might actually be model risk in disguise.

Claim rate risk for IP insurance: sickness experience is the most critical assumption. Even in established markets with good data, the possibility of new diseases represents a hard-to-quantify risk. Portfolio size affects the magnitude of potential loss but reduces random fluctuation risk in larger books.

key_terms.definition: The risk that the mathematical or statistical model chosen to represent future experience is inappropriate or contains errors.
The risk that actual experience deviates from the model and parameters adopted, arising from the heterogeneity of lives and the finite size of the insured population.
The statistical principle that as the size of a sample grows, observed averages converge toward the true expected value — reducing but never eliminating random fluctuations risk.

exam_qa.q: Insurers developing LTCI in new markets have used US experience data. Explain whether this leads to large or small parameter risk.
exam_qa.a: There will be HIGH parameter risk. Although the US data may be voluminous and credible in itself, it has low relevance to a different country because of: different population health (environment, lifestyle, behaviour); different attitudes to claiming; different levels of family/home care support; different policy wordings and definitions; different levels of underwriting and claims management. Adjustments to the US data would be required but are likely to be intuitive guesswork at best, meaning there is a high risk that the resulting parameter estimates are materially incorrect.

subsections.title: Claim Amounts and Earlier Screening / Diagnosis
bullets: Under PMI, a further claim risk is the estimation of future average claim amounts per procedure. This is uncertain because the insurer guarantees to cover whatever a third-party provider charges, introducing short-term uncertainty even though most re-pricing occurs annually.

Claim costs vary not only with inflation and changes in the insured mix but also as: different medical procedures are adopted to treat the same conditions; hospital capacity changes in-patient duration for a given procedure; pricing agreements with providers are renegotiated.

Earlier screening / diagnosis risk (most relevant to CI): if medical technology advances diagnostically, insurers may face claims significantly earlier than expected — and some claims may occur that would not otherwise have arisen (because the policyholder might have died before diagnosis, or the policy term might have expired).

National screening campaigns (e.g. for breast cancer) accelerate the earlier-diagnosis risk for CI. PMI insurers face increased claim frequency from screening programmes, but not necessarily increased cost if earlier detection requires less treatment.

Advances in available treatments increase claim costs: e.g. greater availability of coronary by-pass grafting leads directly to more PMI/CI claims. A secondary effect is that treated patients live longer, potentially generating further claims later — a particular risk for PMI and stand-alone CI.

key_terms.definition: The risk that advances in medical diagnostics bring forward the point at which a condition is diagnosed, causing CI or other claims to occur earlier — and in greater numbers — than pricing assumptions anticipated.

subsections.title: Future Investment and Existing Assets
bullets: Investment return assumptions are required for amounts to be invested in the future (reinvested proceeds, future premiums) and for the existing asset portfolio. Both model and parameter risks arise in either case.

Historically actuaries used deterministic return assumptions, exploring variation by sensitivity testing. Stochastic modelling is now widely used, generating thousands of simulated outcomes from specified probability distributions for each asset class.

Model risk in a stochastic investment model arises if the probability distribution for the random error term is inappropriate (e.g. normal when log-normal is correct). Parameter risk arises if the distributional parameters are set incorrectly.

Investigations comparing the value of liabilities with assets are also subject to a capital values risk — the risk that asset values change between valuation dates, potentially turning a solvent position into an insolvent one overnight.

Delegating investment to a third party introduces additional risks, though the expertise brought may justify this. The insurer should select the investment manager carefully and monitor performance closely.

key_terms.definition: The risk that the market value of existing assets falls between valuation dates, causing a company that appears solvent at one point to become insolvent the next.

A model in which investment returns in any period are treated as outcomes of random variables with specified probability distributions, generating a range of possible outcomes from simulated scenarios.

subsections.title: Significance of Investment Performance by Product
bullets: Investment performance is of lesser significance for PMI, due to its short claims settlement tail (typically no more than 3–5 years between premium receipt and claim settlement), which means reserves are small.
For pre-funded LTCI, investment assumptions and risks may be major considerations because premiums must be invested over very long periods and substantial reserves build up in advance of claims.

For standard CI policies, investment risk is usually small because claim probability is relatively low, leading to small reserves and therefore limited investment income. Exception: comprehensive CI policies (e.g. in South Africa covering a wide range of conditions) or policies with long guaranteed terms carry larger reserves, making investment returns more important.
For PMI insurers paying claims in foreign currencies, exchange rate risk arises; the insurer may need to hold assets in the relevant foreign currencies.

subsections.title: Expense Risk
bullets: Assumptions about future expenses — deterministic or stochastic — give rise to parameter risk and, where stochastic, model risk. In stochastic models, expense inflation is often linked to a stochastically generated price inflation rate.

Premium charges designed to cover expenses are typically fixed or semi-fixed, while actual expenses change over time. This creates a mismatch risk: the charges accruing in a given year may not cover actual expenses incurred that year.

Specific expense mismatch situations: (i) initial expenses recouped over the policy term — insurer is vulnerable to early lapses; (ii) long-duration contracts where inflation erodes originally loaded expense allowances; (iii) volume-driven overhead coverage — fixed costs must be spread over the actual (potentially smaller) in-force portfolio.
Risks widen when third parties manage claims (e.g. IP and PMI claims managed by specialists). The ultimate cost is hard to predict, and these costs may be treated as part of total claim amount.
In stochastic investment models, expense inflation is often modelled as a function of general price inflation, but the two may diverge — a further source of model risk for health and care insurers.

key_terms.definition: The risk that charges loaded into premiums to cover future expenses prove insufficient because actual expenses (and their inflation) diverge from those assumed at pricing.

subsections.title: Persistency Risk — Overview and Selective Withdrawals
bullets: Persistency (lapse rates for long-term business; renewal rates for short-term business) is a source of parameter risk and potentially model risk. Random fluctuations risk can also affect persistency, particularly in small portfolios.

Selective withdrawals leave behind a substandard remaining group: policyholders who know they are lower risk tend to lapse, worsening the average claims experience of those who remain. Insurers allow for the expected effect in pricing, but worse-than-expected selective lapses create residual risk.

Biased lapses differ from selective lapses: they occur when lapses have systematic features not driven by the policyholder's own risk assessment — for example, in a recession, small-policy holders may disproportionately lapse, leaving the insurer with a larger-average-policy portfolio whose experience may differ from assumed.

Key persistency risks: (i) early lapses when asset share is negative; (ii) worsened claims experience from selective withdrawals; (iii) increased per-policy fixed expenses from loss of volume; (iv) lapse-and-re-entry risk.

PMI persistence is particularly important because initial acquisition costs (including commission) are often amortised over an assumed number of renewals. Shortfalls in renewals can leave the insurer unable to recoup those costs.

Lapse-and-re-entry risk is most acute in markets where increasing data availability and aggressive competition drive new-business prices down, as policyholders can lapse and re-enter at lower rates — a feature in some CI markets driven partly by excess reinsurer capacity.

key_terms.definition: Lapses driven by information asymmetry: lower-risk policyholders deliberately lapse, leaving the insurer with a worse-than-assumed risk pool. This is a direct adverse impact on claims experience.
Lapses with systematic characteristics not due to individual policyholder risk selection — e.g. disproportionate lapse of small policies in a recession — which change the mix of remaining business.
The risk that policyholders lapse their existing policies and take out new policies at lower market rates, crystallising losses for the insurer on the original policies.

sections.title: Mix and Volume of New Business

subsections.title: Mix by Nature, Size, and Source
bullets: An unintended change in the mix of new business by nature (class, contract type, design, premium frequency) or size can change the insurer's risk profile and capital requirements beyond available resources.

If contracts are smaller on average than assumed, premium-related income and expense loadings fall but per-policy fixed costs do not — squeezing margins. This is especially pronounced on conventional products where expenses are largely per-policy but charges are premium-related.
A change in mix by nature and size may be another cause of mismatch between charges and actual expenses — for example if a low-overhead-loading product increases its share of the book.

Mix by source (distribution channel): different channels reach different populations, producing different demographic experience, persistency, and expense profiles. If the assumed channel mix proves incorrect, all parameters based on that mix become potentially invalid.

An insurer pricing uniformly across channels uses a weighted-average experience assumption; if the actual mix deviates, it either makes or loses money. An insurer that prices differently by channel eliminates this source of mix risk.

key_terms.definition: The risk that the actual distribution channel mix of new business differs from that assumed in pricing, causing demographic, expense, and persistency parameters to be invalid.

subsections.title: Volume of New Business
bullets: Too much new business: the capital and administrative requirements of writing new business may exceed available resources. Writing exceeds capital because of prudent statutory reserving and high initial expenses — the insurer may have to impose new-business limits or face regulatory insolvency.

Too little new business: failure to recover fixed development costs already incurred. Over-stretched resources from too much business lead to delays or errors (e.g. in underwriting), generating bad public relations and marketing risks.

Expense assumptions are based on modelled future new business volumes. If actual volumes diverge materially, both new-business and in-force expense assumptions may become invalid. Fixed overheads (e.g. the CEO's salary) cannot easily be scaled, so per-policy costs rise on lower volumes.

For short-term health insurance, risk monitoring focuses more on the overall volume and mix of in-force business than specifically on new business, since in-force risks combine both new business and persistency effects.

key_terms.definition: The capital consumed when writing new business, arising from initial expenses, prudent statutory reserves, and solvency capital requirements — which must be financed before the policy generates profits.

subsections.title: Guarantees and Options
bullets: When an insurer offers a guarantee or option, it commits to terms before the triggering event occurs. Model and parameter risks arise from the need to estimate the cost of honouring those terms using assumptions about future experience.

Health and care events are inherently less predictable than life insurance events, so pricing and reserving for options and guarantees requires extra care. Greater uncertainty should be reflected in extra prudence built into parameters.

A deterministic model can make a guarantee appear costless if an optimistic single scenario is chosen. A range of deterministic scenarios with assigned probabilities is better, but highly subjective. A stochastic model is usually the best approach — run via simulation (thousands of outcomes) to estimate the expected cost of the guarantee as an average across scenarios.
Even a stochastic model is only valid if both the model structure and its parameters are appropriate — model and parameter risks still remain.

Example: a unit-linked pre-funded LTCI policy guaranteeing the fund at retirement will not fall below premiums accumulated at 3% pa. The insurer bears the risk that the net-of-charges fund underperforms this floor. The expected cost must be assessed and reserved for.

key_terms.definition: A commitment by the insurer to offer specified terms (e.g. minimum return, option to renew) regardless of future experience — creating a financial risk if experience is adverse.

A model used to price or reserve for guarantees by simulating many possible future scenarios, each with an associated probability; the expected cost of the guarantee is the probability-weighted average outcome.

exam_qa.q: A health insurer adds a renewal option to its 10-year CI contract, charging for it by increasing the base premium. Has the insurer's overall risk increased?
exam_qa.a: Yes — several new risks arise even though the expected cost has been charged for: (i) the model and parameters used to cost the option may prove incorrect; (ii) unforeseen changes over the 10-year original term may affect the option's value; (iii) weaknesses in original underwriting may be compounded when policyholders exercise the renewal option; (iv) the higher premium may reduce sales volumes. Risk is therefore greater than before the option was added.

subsections.title: Competition
bullets: Competitive pressure may lead management to take decisions that increase the insurer's risk profile beyond what its resources can support: reducing premium rates or charges; offering additional guarantees/options; extending coverage (e.g. adding CI conditions); increasing commissions; constraining future charge growth on reviewable contracts.
These decisions can be compounded if they succeed in attracting higher volumes of new business — amplifying the capital strain.

Competitors' actions can also reduce new-business volumes or cause selective non-renewals on short-term products. Cheaper competitor cover may trigger policyholders to lapse selectively, worsening the insurer's remaining risk pool.

subsections.title: Actions of Management
bullets: Management may choose not to follow the actuary's risk recommendations for competitive reasons, to achieve strategic goals (maximising volumes, funds under management, or market segmentation), or to maximise shareholder earnings.

There may be a fundamental conflict between shareholders and without-profits policyholders: shareholders benefit from high-risk, high-return strategies, but without-profits policyholders only suffer from the downside — insolvency — without sharing in the upside.

The actuary may have both regulatory and professional responsibility to ensure inappropriate risks are not taken, particularly where policyholder security and fair treatment are concerned. The actuary is often well placed to quantify the financial risk of a particular management decision.

subsections.title: Reinsurance and Investment Counterparties
bullets: Counterparty risk (also called credit risk) is the risk that another party to an agreement fully or partially defaults on its obligations, or performs them to an unacceptable standard.

Reinsurance counterparty risk: under most reinsurance arrangements, the cedant remains fully liable to policyholders. If the reinsurer defaults, the insurer must absorb the reinsured claim cost itself. Careful selection and ongoing credit monitoring of reinsurers is therefore essential.
Investment counterparty risk: corporate bonds and deposits expose the insurer to default by the issuing company or deposit holder on coupon/interest payments and/or on principal repayment.
Using counterparties can also increase operational risks by reducing the insurer's direct control over processes.

key_terms.definition: The risk that a party to a contract (reinsurer, distributor, investment counterparty) defaults on, or performs inadequately under, its obligations.
A specific form of counterparty risk relating to default on financial obligations — e.g. a reinsurer failing to pay its share of a claim, or a bond issuer failing to pay a coupon.

subsections.title: Distribution and Medical Services Counterparties
bullets: Distributor counterparty risks: (i) the distributor may commit the insurer to policy conditions not intended (e.g. a broker changing ADL definitions in a LTCI policy); (ii) the distributor may hold policyholder premiums or claim payments and default before passing them on — a credit risk at both ends of the premium/claim cycle; (iii) the distributor may bring the insurer into disrepute through poor client handling.

Third-party medical service providers (underwriting agencies, claims assessors, disability counsellors) may commit expenditure on the insurer's behalf — their spending decisions are a direct source of risk.

Under medical expenses and some LTCI covers, the benefit itself is provided by third parties on an indemnity basis, so the ultimate claims cost lies partly in the hands of those providers. Insurers mitigate this through fee agreements, monitoring, and contractual controls.

Mitigation of third-party claim handling risk involves: thorough due diligence before engagement; watertight contractual terms (including fixed fees where possible); ongoing monitoring and regular spot checks.

subsections.title: Legal, Regulatory, Tax, Reputation, and Internal Fraud
bullets: Legal / regulatory / tax: local law may override explicit policy conditions; new legislation can apply to existing in-force policies, changing the nature of the contract (e.g. deeming exclusions unacceptable, increasing payouts by court precedent, taxing benefits/premiums). Prohibition of rating factors (e.g. race in some jurisdictions) is another form of legal risk.

LTCI is particularly vulnerable to sudden mandatory legal changes because of the societal expectation that the state should support the sick and elderly. The best mitigation is to monitor legislative developments closely and build prudence into assumptions.

Reputational risk: in markets with strong consumer protection cultures, poor customer service — whether via formal legal/regulatory action, or informally through press, television, word-of-mouth, or social media — can damage both the existing client base and new business prospects. Health and care insurers face heightened moral obligations over and above contractual duties, especially regarding the sick and elderly.

Internal audit failures / fraud: staff in positions of trust may leak confidential information or embezzle funds. Appropriate training, governance, and robust internal audit procedures are vital mitigants.

key_terms.definition: The risk that negative publicity — from legal proceedings, media exposure, social media, or word-of-mouth — damages the insurer's relationship with existing policyholders and its ability to attract new business.

subsections.title: Physical Risks, Aggregation / Concentration, Catastrophes
bullets: Physical risks (fire, flood, loss of key staff) can disrupt business operations. Business continuity procedures — including systems back-ups, alternative premises, and appropriate insurance cover (including business interruption) — are essential.

Aggregation and concentration of risk: over-exposure to a particular risk type or region (through product specialisation or concentrated distribution) creates vulnerability to correlated events. Examples: a small sickness insurer concentrated in one region risks an outbreak of local illness; a group IP insurer focused on one industry risks an industry-specific disease.

Mitigation of aggregation/concentration: broader geographical marketing, reinsurance, and coinsurance with reciprocation — sharing risks with an insurer whose exposures are complementary (e.g. in different regions), so both achieve diversification.

Catastrophes (epidemics, pandemics, war, terrorism) are difficult to predict and typically generate many individual claims from a single cause rather than one enormous single loss. Resolution lies primarily in catastrophe excess-of-loss reinsurance. Policy exclusions may also apply to specified catastrophe events.

Non-disclosure: policyholders' failure to disclose risk-relevant information at proposal makes premium rating more problematic. Moratorium underwriting (accepting all proposals initially, underwriting at claim stage) complicates proof of knowledge but can increase sales and reduce initial admin costs. Mitigation includes clear sales literature, well-worded proposal forms, doctors' reports, closer audit of cases, and dialogue between underwriting, sales, and claims management.

Anti-selection: the key risk is greater anti-selection than anticipated in premium rates — highlighting the critical link between pricing and underwriting. Access to genetic test results is especially relevant and may be subject to local legislation.

key_terms.definition: A risk-sharing arrangement whereby two insurers swap complementary exposures — e.g. each cedes a share of risks concentrated in its own region to the other — achieving mutual diversification.

An approach in which an insurer accepts all policyholders initially on a declaration of health, then conducts underwriting at the claims stage. It increases sales and reduces initial admin but makes it harder to prove non-disclosure.

The tendency for higher-risk individuals to seek insurance more actively than lower-risk individuals, producing an insured pool with worse expected experience than the general population — a risk whenever insurers cannot fully observe or price individual risk.

subsections.title: IP Insurance Risks
bullets: Main IP risk: sickness transition probabilities in the multiple-state model — specifically claim inception rates (healthy→sick) and claim termination rates (sick→healthy recovery and sick→dead). The broader (more lenient) the contract's definition of incapacity, the greater the uncertainty of expected sickness experience.

A simple IP multiple-state model has three states: healthy, sick, dead. Transition probabilities: healthy-to-sick (inception); sick-to-healthy (recovery) and sick-to-dead (termination rates). Lapses require a fourth 'lapsed' state, with transition only from the healthy state.

Because 'unfit to work' is less well-defined than 'dead', IP claim experience is inherently harder to predict. More lenient definitions increase both expected cost (which can be priced for) and variance (which creates risk).

Industry IP data is difficult to use as a benchmark because policy conditions vary greatly between insurers — more so than for life products — increasing parameter risk on any industry-derived assumption.
Anti-selection risk is significant on individual IP contracts (individuals likely to claim may seek cover); reduced on group contracts because group members have less active choice.

Moral hazard: IP policyholders receiving benefits have reduced incentive to return to work. This reduces the sick-to-healthy transition probability and may increase inception rates (insured individuals may take sick leave sooner than uninsured equivalents).

Other IP risks: early deaths of healthy policyholders before initial expenses are recouped; higher-than-expected expenses; lower investment returns (though reserves are not usually large enough for investment to be a dominant risk); lapse losses when asset share is negative.
Capital requirements for IP business are generally low relative to other contract types, though greater claim unpredictability may justify larger prudent margins than for life products.

key_terms.definition: The probability of a policyholder transitioning from the healthy state to the sick (claiming) state in a given period — the primary risk driver on IP contracts.
The probability that a current IP claimant ceases to claim — either by recovery (sick→healthy) or by death (sick→dead). Together with inception rates, these define the expected cost of IP business.
The tendency for IP policyholders receiving benefits to delay recovery (return to work) because the insurance benefit reduces their financial incentive to recover quickly.

exam_qa.q: Explain how moral hazard arises on IP policies and state which transition probabilities are affected.
exam_qa.a: Moral hazard arises because policyholders receiving IP benefits have reduced financial incentive to return to work compared to uninsured individuals in the same health situation — the insurance alters their behaviour. Transition probabilities affected: (i) sick-to-healthy (recovery) rate is REDUCED — claimants take longer to return to work; (ii) healthy-to-sick (inception) rate may also INCREASE — insured individuals are more likely to go off work sick while uninsured individuals may struggle on.

subsections.title: CI Insurance Risks
bullets: Main CI risk: rates of diagnosis of specified critical illnesses. Because CI is relatively new in most markets, data are limited, making estimates of historical and future CI rates unreliable — leading to high parameter risk and high probability that actual experience significantly exceeds expectations.

Heterogeneity in CI industry data: different insurers cover different conditions and use different claim definitions. Industry data may therefore be very heterogeneous and misleading as a basis for any individual insurer's assumptions.

Medical advances create CI-specific risks: prolonging life (more illnesses survive to be diagnosed, more stand-alone claims paid); improving screening (earlier, more frequent diagnosis, more claims triggered, potentially before policy term expires); enabling identification and prevention of susceptibility (could improve experience). Net effect depends on the interaction of all three.
Anti-selection is significant on individual stand-alone CI contracts; greatly reduced on group CI. Selective lapses create additional risk on both stand-alone and rider benefit contracts.
Expense risk similar to IP; investment risk usually minimal because reserves are small on most CI products (protection-focused, relatively low claim probability).
Capital requirements for stand-alone CI or marginal requirements for rider CI benefits are of the same order as for IP.

subsections.title: Long-Term Care Insurance Risks
bullets: Main LTCI risk: transition probabilities in a multi-state model with states such as: healthy, needing own-home care, needing residential home care, needing nursing home care, dead (plus withdrawn).
