Comparison with previous premiums: normal Level 1 premiums will rise (lighter mortality assumed). Level 2 premiums expected unchanged, except for possible reduction from lower expense assumptions (higher volumes). All premiums may be partially offset by reduced expense loadings from expected higher overall volumes. Competition will constrain pricing, particularly for the enhanced Level 1 product which must be attractively priced relative to normal Level 1 to justify the higher cost. [5]

exam_qa.q: Q30.1(iv): Describe the investigations the company might undertake to determine appropriate mortality assumptions for pricing the enhanced Level 1 variant, and explain how the assumptions would be determined.
exam_qa.a: Level 2 mortality: investigate mortality experience under existing Level 2 policyholders. Use most recent credible experience, creating cells by age, gender, policy duration, smoker status, health/family history, ADL assessment results, and possibly territory/sales channel. Compare actual deaths to expected under a standard mortality table or current pricing basis. Project future rates allowing for: mortality improvement trends; planned changes to underwriting/risk classification. Extrapolate trends using own longer-term experience and relevant industry/population data. Include a prudential margin. Level 1 (while at Level 1) mortality: analyse existing Level 1 data in the same way, but exposed to risk must include only time spent at Level 1 status — i.e. exclude time after the policyholder would have qualified for Level 2. This is difficult to assess without historical address/care status records (address changes may signal transfer to a nursing home). An approximation: group data by policy duration (the proportion eligible for Level 2 rises with duration) and use only short-duration experience to approximate pure Level 1 mortality. Industry standard tables split by ADL level may be available. Given this uncertainty, assume lighter mortality prudently at the outset. Monitor emerging experience under enhanced Level 1 policyholders closely and update the pricing basis as experience develops. [10]

exam_qa.q: Q30.2(i): Explain how model error, parameter error and random fluctuations can each lead to losses from morbidity experience, giving a simple example of each.
exam_qa.a: Model error: losses due to error in the specification (structure) of the morbidity model. Example: pricing IP contracts using the claim inception and annuity method rather than a more realistic multiple-state model — the approximations cause premiums to be set too low. Parameter error: losses due to incorrect parameter values in the morbidity model. Example: when pricing CI contracts, assuming incidence rates at each age that are lower than actual claim probabilities in the insured population. Random fluctuations: losses arising purely by chance, even with a correctly specified model and correct parameter values. Example: a CI portfolio whose actual parameters equal those in the pricing model still experiences more CI claims than expected in a particular period due to random variation. [3]

exam_qa.q: Q30.2(ii)(a): How can the actuarial control cycle help the company monitor and manage a morbidity problem?
exam_qa.a: Monitor the experience: compare actual morbidity rates with those expected under the premium and/or reserving basis. Perform an analysis of surplus to quantify the loss from morbidity separately from other profit sources. Assess the magnitude and persistence of the losses (trend analysis across multiple years). Develop the solution: feed revised assumptions back into: (1) the pricing basis, leading to premium/charge revision (especially if premiums are reviewable); (2) the reserving basis, preventing over-distribution of profit and protecting solvency; (3) the company's risk management models (e.g. reinsurance decisions based on revised probability of ruin). The iterative nature of the cycle allows ongoing refinement as more experience emerges. [As per ACC framework]

exam_qa.q: Q30.2(ii)(b): Suggest and briefly describe the various steps the company could take to manage a morbidity problem.
exam_qa.a: Underwriting: tighten underwriting strategy to reduce anti-selection; ensure underwriting procedures are consistent with the morbidity rates implied by standard premiums. Claims control: reassess/recertify claimants' sickness status more frequently or more objectively; this may reduce claims duration and cost. Rehabilitation: introduce rehabilitation and counselling services if cost-effective (cost of services < cost of claims saved). Product design (longer term): change policy wording (redefine insured conditions); introduce a partial return-to-work benefit; reduce guarantees and options; introduce a premium/charge review facility; shorten the contractual linked-claims period; switch from without-profits to unit-linked designs. Sales strategy: more focused selling based on financial need — reduces selective withdrawal (lapses by healthier lives) and thereby improves portfolio morbidity. Reinsurance: revise reinsurance strategy — a surplus reinsurance treaty on an original terms or risk premium basis may be appropriate to limit exposure to continued adverse morbidity. [10]
